message.content: read ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/agentrail-rs to see how to set this project up to use that tool.  Read docs/research.txt to see the purpose of this tool and what is needed so you can create a agentrail "saga".  Look at ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/web-tml24c for how to set up a live demo for a similar (Lisp not Forth) tool.  Look at ~/github/sw-embed/cor24-rs for the runtime used by the other web tools, and look at ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/tf24a for the Forth interprestor to be run by cor24-rs runtime insude your WASM app built here.  Questions (after reading/researching)?

content.text: Let me read all the relevant sources in parallel.

1→
     2→
     3→
     4→
     5→What does a Forth debugger look like?  What is important when looking at how words are stored in-memory?  I can add a web UI debugger (in Yew/Rust/WASM) that shows memory and registers.  How should I show the different memory regions/usage/conventions for Forth code and data?
     6→
     7→
     8→Thought for 7s
     9→You want a debugger/UI that helps you understand how Forth executes and how words live in memory, not just a generic CPU debugger. That means the design should make three things visible at once:
    10→
    11→The machine state: registers, PC/IP, memory.
    12→
    13→The Forth VM state: data stack, return stack, current word, next word, interpret/compile state.
    14→
    15→The dictionary state: how words are laid out, linked, found, and executed.
    16→
    17→That is what makes a Forth debugger feel different from a C or assembly debugger.
    18→
    19→What a Forth debugger usually looks like
    20→Historically, a Forth debugger is often word-oriented, not instruction-oriented. A typical debugger shows or lets you inspect:
    21→
    22→the data stack
    23→
    24→the return stack
    25→
    26→the currently executing word
    27→
    28→the input source / source location
    29→
    30→the dictionary / word definition
    31→
    32→optionally a memory dump and disassembly
    33→
    34→For example, Gforth’s debugging and examining tools emphasize stack inspection, source-level stepping, and examining code/data rather than only raw CPU stepping. .s shows the data stack non-destructively, with the top of stack shown at the right; Gforth also documents return-stack concerns explicitly because misuse usually crashes the program. 
    35→
    36→So the most useful mental model is:
    37→
    38→A Forth debugger is a combined stack tracer + dictionary browser + optional machine debugger.
    39→
    40→The most important views to include
    41→1. Data stack
    42→This is the first-class UI element in Forth.
    43→
    44→Show:
    45→
    46→depth
    47→
    48→cells from bottom to top
    49→
    50→top-of-stack clearly highlighted
    51→
    52→multiple display formats:
    53→
    54→signed decimal
    55→
    56→unsigned
    57→
    58→hex
    59→
    60→address/symbolic if inside known regions
    61→
    62→Why it matters:
    63→Most Forth bugs are stack-effect bugs before they are “CPU bugs.”
    64→
    65→Recommended UI:
    66→
    67→vertical stack, top at bottom or top at right; just be consistent
    68→
    69→include a small inferred tag:
    70→
    71→value
    72→
    73→addr
    74→
    75→xt
    76→
    77→flag
    78→
    79→char
    80→
    81→2. Return stack
    82→This is equally important in Forth, but many tools under-emphasize it.
    83→
    84→Show:
    85→
    86→raw contents
    87→
    88→symbolic interpretation when possible:
    89→
    90→return address into a colon definition
    91→
    92→loop parameters
    93→
    94→temporary values if the program explicitly used >R R> R@
    95→
    96→Why it matters:
    97→In standard Forth systems, the return stack holds return addresses and loop-control state, and incorrect use often ends in a crash. 
    98→
    99→Recommended UI:
   100→
   101→separate pane from data stack
   102→
   103→color-code entries:
   104→
   105→return address
   106→
   107→loop frame
   108→
   109→user-pushed temporary
   110→
   111→if an entry points into a word body, annotate:
   112→
   113→return into FOO + 3 cells
   114→
   115→3. Current execution view
   116→This is the heart of a Forth debugger.
   117→
   118→Show:
   119→
   120→current instruction pointer / threaded IP
   121→
   122→current word being executed
   123→
   124→caller word
   125→
   126→next few words to be executed
   127→
   128→current source line if available
   129→
   130→If you have indirect-threaded or token-threaded execution, raw machine PC is often less useful than the Forth IP stepping through word bodies.
   131→
   132→Recommended UI:
   133→
   134→breadcrumb:
   135→
   136→MAIN -> PARSE-LINE -> INTERPRET -> FIND
   137→
   138→current slot highlighted inside the word body
   139→
   140→“step word”, “step primitive”, “step out”, “run to next colon word”
   141→
   142→4. Dictionary browser
   143→This is the most distinctive Forth-specific panel.
   144→
   145→A Forth dictionary entry traditionally contains some form of:
   146→
   147→link field: pointer to previous definition
   148→
   149→name field
   150→
   151→code field / code pointer field
   152→
   153→parameter/body field
   154→
   155→This terminology appears widely in Forth explanations: link field, name field, code field address (CFA), parameter field address (PFA), and body. 
   156→
   157→Your UI should let the user click a word and see:
   158→
   159→word name
   160→
   161→flags:
   162→
   163→immediate
   164→
   165→hidden/smudged
   166→
   167→compile-only if applicable
   168→
   169→link to previous word
   170→
   171→execution token / CFA
   172→
   173→body/PFA
   174→
   175→interpreted type:
   176→
   177→primitive
   178→
   179→colon definition
   180→
   181→constant
   182→
   183→variable
   184→
   185→CREATEd word
   186→
   187→DOES> word
   188→
   189→This is where Forth becomes teachable.
   190→
   191→What is important when looking at words in memory
   192→The key question is not just “what bytes are here?” but:
   193→
   194→How does this word participate in name lookup, compilation, and execution?
   195→
   196→You want to make the following visible.
   197→
   198→A. Header vs body
   199→A word usually has a header and a body.
   200→
   201→Header commonly includes:
   202→
   203→link
   204→
   205→name
   206→
   207→flags/length
   208→
   209→Body commonly includes:
   210→
   211→code field / execution behavior
   212→
   213→parameter data or compiled word list
   214→
   215→This distinction is explicitly described in Forth references: the “head” is name/link information, while the “body” is code/data; colon definitions use the code field to point to the address interpreter, while other defining words arrange different runtime behavior. 
   216→
   217→UI suggestion:
   218→Use a structured expandable row like:
   219→
   220→WORD: DUP
   221→  Header
   222→    Link: 0x1234 -> DROP
   223→    Name: "DUP"
   224→    Flags: immediate=0 hidden=0
   225→  Execution
   226→    CFA / xt: 0x87A0
   227→    Kind: primitive
   228→  Body
   229→    <none or primitive code pointer>
   230→For a colon definition:
   231→
   232→WORD: SQUARE
   233→  Header
   234→    Link: ...
   235→    Name: "SQUARE"
   236→  Execution
   237→    CFA: DOCOL
   238→    Kind: colon
   239→  Body
   240→    0: DUP
   241→    1: *
   242→    2: EXIT
   243→B. The link chain
   244→Traditional Forth dictionaries are often organized as a linked list of definitions, with the newest word first and the link field pointing to the previous definition. JonesForth and several explanatory sources describe this structure directly. 
   245→
   246→Why it matters:
   247→
   248→explains name lookup
   249→
   250→shows shadowing/redefinition
   251→
   252→makes hidden/smudged words understandable
   253→
   254→UI suggestion:
   255→Show both:
   256→
   257→linear memory order
   258→
   259→search order / link-chain order
   260→
   261→Those are not always identical in advanced systems.
   262→
   263→C. Execution semantics
   264→The same “word” name can correspond to different runtime forms:
   265→
   266→primitive
   267→
   268→colon definition
   269→
   270→constant
   271→
   272→variable
   273→
   274→deferred word
   275→
   276→CREATE ... DOES> instance
   277→
   278→This matters more than raw bytes. A debugger should decode a word according to its defining semantics.
   279→
   280→Example:
   281→
   282→a CONSTANT’s body is data, not a sequence of called words
   283→
   284→a colon definition body is a list of execution tokens / threaded addresses
   285→
   286→a VARIABLE body is storage
   287→
   288→a CREATEd word’s body is usually user data, while DOES> supplies runtime behavior
   289→
   290→The Forth execution model and defining-word structure are central in “Under the Hood” style explanations. 
   291→
   292→So in your UI, every word should have a kind and a custom renderer.
   293→
   294→D. Immediate and hidden flags
   295→These are crucial during compilation and debugging compiler behavior.
   296→
   297→JonesForth explicitly discusses flag bits for hidden and immediate words in dictionary headers. 
   298→
   299→Why it matters:
   300→
   301→explains why a word executes during compilation
   302→
   303→explains why some words do not appear in searches
   304→
   305→helps debug the text interpreter/compiler
   306→
   307→UI suggestion:
   308→Display flags prominently, not buried in a raw byte dump.
   309→
   310→E. Code field and parameter field boundaries
   311→If you are teaching or debugging a threaded Forth, the most educational view is:
   312→
   313→CFA / code field
   314→
   315→PFA / body
   316→
   317→current IP relative to the PFA
   318→
   319→This helps the user answer:
   320→
   321→Is the word executable?
   322→
   323→What runtime code handles it?
   324→
   325→Is the body code or data?
   326→
   327→Where will >BODY land?
   328→
   329→How to show memory regions in a web debugger
   330→You said you can add a Yew/Rust/WASM debugger with memory and registers. For Forth, I would not begin with a single giant hex dump. Start with a memory map view, then drill down.
   331→
   332→Recommended top-level regions
   333→Show a region map like this:
   334→
   335→Region	Purpose	Grows	Important indicators
   336→Code / primitives	native code / kernel	fixed or upward	entry points, primitive table
   337→Dictionary / heap	word headers + bodies	upward	HERE, latest word, free space
   338→Data stack	parameters	downward or upward	SP, depth, underflow/overflow
   339→Return stack	return addresses, loop frames	downward or upward	RP, nesting depth
   340→User area	task-local vars	fixed	BASE, STATE, etc.
   341→TIB / input buffer	source text	variable	parse pointer
   342→PAD / scratch	transient formatting	variable	recent temporary usage
   343→Variables/buffers	application data	variable	named blocks
   344→These exact region boundaries vary across Forths, but the concepts—dictionary, data stack, return stack, input buffer, scratch area—are standard enough to make them excellent UI concepts. References discussing dictionary, input message buffer, pad, stacks, and execution model support this breakdown. 
   345→
   346→Region-map UI
   347→Use a horizontal or vertical bar:
   348→
   349→0x000000 ├─ kernel/primitives
   350→0x001000 ├─ dictionary start
   351→0x003840 ├─ latest word / HERE
   352→0x004000 ├─ free
   353→0x007000 ├─ PAD / TIB / user area
   354→0x008000 ├─ return stack top
   355→0x009000 ├─ data stack top
   356→0x00A000 └─ end RAM
   357→Make each region clickable.
   358→
   359→The most useful visualizations
   360→1. Dual representation: semantic + raw
   361→Every pane should support two modes:
   362→
   363→semantic mode: word names, flags, fields, stack annotations
   364→
   365→raw mode: hex bytes / cells
   366→
   367→For example, dictionary browser:
   368→
   369→[semantic]
   370→FOO
   371→  link -> BAR
   372→  flags -> immediate
   373→  cfa  -> DOCOL
   374→  body -> DUP * EXIT
   375→and
   376→
   377→[raw]
   378→001240: 34 12 83 46 4F 4F 00 ...
   379→001248: A0 00
   380→00124A: 91 10 77 02 E0 00
   381→This is ideal for teaching and for validating your implementation.
   382→
   383→2. Word-body disassembly view
   384→For a selected word, show its body as decoded cells:
   385→
   386→For colon definitions:
   387→
   388→called word names
   389→
   390→branches with target annotations
   391→
   392→literals decoded
   393→
   394→current IP highlighted
   395→
   396→Example:
   397→
   398→: ABS
   399→  0: DUP
   400→  1: 0<
   401→  2: ?BRANCH -> 5
   402→  3: NEGATE
   403→  4: EXIT
   404→  5: EXIT
   405→For primitives:
   406→
   407→native disassembly or pseudo-op view
   408→
   409→For constants/variables:
   410→
   411→show stored cell values
   412→
   413→3. Search-order and vocabulary view
   414→If you later support vocabularies or wordlists, include:
   415→
   416→current search order
   417→
   418→current compilation wordlist
   419→
   420→latest word per wordlist
   421→
   422→Without this, a lot of “why did FIND return that word?” becomes mysterious.
   423→
   424→4. Stack-effect overlay
   425→If you know or infer stack comments, display them while stepping.
   426→
   427→Example:
   428→
   429→DUP      ( x -- x x )
   430→*        ( a b -- a*b )
   431→This is not strictly required for a machine debugger, but it is hugely useful for a Forth debugger.
   432→
   433→Best conventions for showing Forth memory
   434→Prefer cells over bytes
   435→Forth is naturally cell-oriented.
   436→
   437→So the default memory dump for dictionary bodies and stacks should be in cells, with an option to switch to bytes.
   438→
   439→For example:
   440→
   441→Addr      Cell(hex)   Meaning
   442→0x1200    0x08A0      CFA = DOCOL
   443→0x1202    0x1310      xt DUP
   444→0x1204    0x1422      xt *
   445→0x1206    0x0810      xt EXIT
   446→For name/header area, byte view is useful.
   447→For code/data bodies, cell view is better.
   448→
   449→Show growth directions
   450→This matters a lot in Forth.
   451→
   452→Always label:
   453→
   454→dictionary grows up
   455→
   456→stacks grow down or up depending on your implementation
   457→
   458→Show collision risk visually:
   459→
   460→free memory band between dictionary and stacks
   461→
   462→warning when margin is small
   463→
   464→This matters especially on small targets.
   465→
   466→Show symbolic addresses
   467→Instead of only 0x1234, annotate:
   468→
   469→xt DUP
   470→
   471→body of FOO + 2 cells
   472→
   473→return to BAR + 1
   474→
   475→TIB + 12
   476→
   477→user variable STATE
   478→
   479→That turns a memory monitor into a real debugger.
   480→
   481→A practical UI layout for your Yew/Rust/WASM debugger
   482→Here is the layout I would recommend.
   483→
   484→Main layout
   485→Left column: execution
   486→current word
   487→
   488→caller chain
   489→
   490→step controls
   491→
   492→source text / current token
   493→
   494→interpret state:
   495→
   496→STATE
   497→
   498→BASE
   499→
   500→current input pointer
   501→
   502→Center column: stacks
   503→data stack
   504→
   505→return stack
   506→
   507→optional floating stack later
   508→
   509→Right column: selected memory object
   510→Tabs:
   511→
   512→Word
   513→
   514→Memory
   515→
   516→Disasm
   517→
   518→Dictionary
   519→
   520→Regions
   521→
   522→Bottom strip: registers and status
   523→CPU registers
   524→
   525→machine PC/SP
   526→
   527→Forth IP/W if applicable
   528→
   529→breakpoints/watchpoints
   530→
   531→cycle count if emulator supports it
   532→
   533→Breakpoints and watchpoints that matter in Forth
   534→A normal debugger breaks on addresses. A Forth debugger should also break on:
   535→
   536→entry to a named word
   537→
   538→exit from a named word
   539→
   540→definition of a new word
   541→
   542→changes to STATE
   543→
   544→writes to HERE
   545→
   546→dictionary overflow / stack near-collision
   547→
   548→ABORT, THROW, or invalid word lookup
   549→
   550→return-stack misuse
   551→
   552→Very valuable watchpoints:
   553→
   554→break when a particular header is created
   555→
   556→break when a given variable or user variable changes
   557→
   558→break when a dictionary cell is compiled
   559→
   560→Pros and cons of different visualization styles
   561→A. Raw hex-dump-first
   562→Pros
   563→
   564→closest to the machine
   565→
   566→easiest to implement first
   567→
   568→great for validating emulator correctness
   569→
   570→Cons
   571→
   572→bad for learning Forth
   573→
   574→hides semantics of headers and threaded code
   575→
   576→too much mental decoding
   577→
   578→B. Semantic Forth-first
   579→Pros
   580→
   581→best for teaching
   582→
   583→helps explain execution and compilation
   584→
   585→easier to reason about bugs in words
   586→
   587→Cons
   588→
   589→requires decoders for multiple word kinds
   590→
   591→can hide low-level corruption unless raw view exists
   592→
   593→C. Split semantic/raw view
   594→Pros
   595→
   596→best overall
   597→
   598→supports both teaching and implementation validation
   599→
   600→scales from simple indirect-threaded Forth to richer systems
   601→
   602→Cons
   603→
   604→more UI work
   605→
   606→For your use case, C is the clear winner.
   607→
   608→A concrete set of conventions I would use
   609→Dictionary pane
   610→Show rows like:
   611→
   612→Addr	Name	Kind	Flags	Link	CFA	Body
   613→0x1200	DUP	primitive	-	0x11E0	0x0800	primitive code
   614→0x1210	SQUARE	colon	-	0x1200	DOCOL	DUP * EXIT
   615→0x1220	TEN	constant	-	0x1210	(CONST)	10
   616→Memory-region pane
   617→Show:
   618→
   619→start/end address
   620→
   621→current pointers:
   622→
   623→HERE
   624→
   625→LATEST
   626→
   627→SP
   628→
   629→RP
   630→
   631→TIB
   632→
   633→growth direction arrows
   634→
   635→Stack pane
   636→Show cells and symbolic decode:
   637→
   638→Data stack
   639→  [0]  0x0003   3
   640→  [1]  0x1200   xt SQUARE   <- TOS
   641→Word pane
   642→Show selected word as:
   643→
   644→Name: SQUARE
   645→Kind: colon definition
   646→Header address: 0x1210
   647→Link: 0x1200 -> DUP
   648→Flags: none
   649→CFA: DOCOL
   650→Body/PFA:
   651→  0: DUP
   652→  1: *
   653→  2: EXIT
   654→What matters most for small-target Forth systems
   655→Since your target sounds resource-constrained and you are building your own environment, these are the most important conventions to expose:
   656→
   657→Dictionary growth and free space
   658→
   659→Data stack depth and high-water mark
   660→
   661→Return stack depth and misuse
   662→
   663→Header layout of each word
   664→
   665→CFA/body decode
   666→
   667→Whether a word is immediate or hidden
   668→
   669→Current compile vs interpret mode
   670→
   671→Exact location of HERE and latest word
   672→
   673→If you only build the first version, build around those.
   674→
   675→Recommended first milestone
   676→Implement these panes first:
   677→
   678→Data stack
   679→
   680→Return stack
   681→
   682→Current word / IP
   683→
   684→Dictionary list
   685→
   686→Selected-word inspector
   687→
   688→Region map
   689→
   690→That will already feel like a real Forth debugger.
   691→
   692→Then add:
   693→
   694→word breakpoints
   695→
   696→source mapping
   697→
   698→raw hex/cell dump
   699→
   700→compile-event trace
   701→
   702→Bottom line
   703→A good Forth debugger should not look like “just memory and registers.” It should look like:
   704→
   705→a stack debugger
   706→
   707→a dictionary explorer
   708→
   709→a word-level tracer
   710→
   711→and only secondarily a machine debugger
   712→
   713→The most important memory concepts to show are:
   714→
   715→header vs body
   716→
   717→link chain
   718→
   719→CFA/xt and PFA/body
   720→
   721→word kind
   722→
   723→flags
   724→
   725→dictionary growth
   726→
   727→data stack / return stack separation
   728→
   729→That combination will make your Yew/Rust/WASM debugger genuinely useful for learning, implementing, and debugging Forth. The overall emphasis on stack inspection, source/word-level stepping, dictionary structure, and header/body terminology is consistent with modern Gforth documentation and classic explanatory material on Forth internals. 
   730→
   731→I can sketch a concrete mock UI layout next, including exact panes, labels, and a sample memory map for a minimal indirect-threaded Forth.
   732→
   733→
   734→sure that would help
   735→
   736→Below is a concrete mock debugger layout for a small Forth, especially a traditional threaded Forth on a constrained target. I will assume:
   737→
   738→separate data stack and return stack
   739→
   740→a dictionary growing upward
   741→
   742→either:
   743→
   744→indirect-threaded or direct-threaded code, or
   745→
   746→a close equivalent where colon definitions are still visible as bodies of cells/tokens
   747→
   748→a machine/debugger layer underneath with registers and raw memory
   749→
   750→The goal is to let you understand, at a glance:
   751→
   752→what word is executing
   753→
   754→what the stacks contain
   755→
   756→what the selected word looks like in memory
   757→
   758→how close memory regions are to collision
   759→
   760→what just got compiled or executed
   761→
   762→1. High-level screen layout
   763→A very practical first layout is a three-column + bottom strip arrangement.
   764→
   765→+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
   766→| Toolbar: [Run] [Pause] [Step Word] [Step Primitive] [Step Out] [Reset] [Compile Log] |
   767→+-------------------------+--------------------------------+---------------------------+
   768→| Execution / Trace       | Stacks                         | Selected Object           |
   769→|                         |                                |                           |
   770→| Current word            | Data Stack                     | [Word] [Memory] [Disasm] |
   771→| Caller chain            | Return Stack                   | [Regions] [Source]       |
   772→| Current token/IP        |                                |                           |
   773→| Current input token     |                                | Details of selected word |
   774→| STATE / BASE / HERE     |                                | or selected memory cell  |
   775→|                         |                                |                           |
   776→+-------------------------+--------------------------------+---------------------------+
   777→| Dictionary / Search Order / Compile Events / Breakpoints                             |
   778→+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
   779→| CPU registers | Forth VM registers | region usage bar | warnings/errors             |
   780→+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
   781→This is better than a single hex dump because it separates:
   782→
   783→live execution
   784→
   785→live stacks
   786→
   787→static structure
   788→
   789→raw inspection
   790→
   791→2. Pane-by-pane design
   792→A. Execution / Trace pane
   793→This should be the primary pane when stepping.
   794→
   795→Show:
   796→Current word
   797→
   798→Word kind
   799→
   800→Execution token / code address
   801→
   802→Current IP offset within the word
   803→
   804→Caller chain
   805→
   806→Current parsed token
   807→
   808→Interpreter state
   809→
   810→Small rolling trace
   811→
   812→Example
   813→Current word: INTERPRET
   814→Kind: colon
   815→xt/CFA: 0x1840
   816→IP: body + 5 cells
   817→
   818→Caller chain:
   819→  QUIT
   820→  INTERPRET
   821→  FIND
   822→  NUMBER?
   823→
   824→Current token:
   825→  "SQUARE"
   826→
   827→Interpreter state:
   828→  STATE = 0   (interpret)
   829→  BASE  = 10
   830→  HERE  = 0x2C80
   831→  LATEST = 0x2C68
   832→
   833→Next operations:
   834→  5: FIND
   835→  6: ?DUP
   836→  7: IF
   837→  8: EXECUTE
   838→Why this matters
   839→This pane answers the most common Forth questions:
   840→
   841→What word am I in?
   842→
   843→What does it think the next thing is?
   844→
   845→Am I interpreting or compiling?
   846→
   847→Did lookup fail, or did execution fail?
   848→
   849→B. Data Stack pane
   850→This should always be visible.
   851→
   852→Suggested format
   853→Use a vertical stack, with top-of-stack at the bottom.
   854→That visually matches “push goes down onto the top.”
   855→
   856→Data Stack (depth 4)
   857→  [0] 0x0003   3
   858→  [1] 0x0010   16
   859→  [2] 0x2A40   addr BUFFER
   860→  [3] 0x1840   xt INTERPRET   <- TOS
   861→Recommended features
   862→show index from bottom
   863→
   864→highlight TOS
   865→
   866→support display modes:
   867→
   868→decimal
   869→
   870→hex
   871→
   872→signed
   873→
   874→symbolic
   875→
   876→optional inferred type badges:
   877→
   878→val
   879→
   880→addr
   881→
   882→xt
   883→
   884→flag
   885→
   886→Nice extra
   887→A mini stack history:
   888→
   889→Previous:
   890→  before DUP:   [ 5 ]
   891→  after  DUP:   [ 5 5 ]
   892→That makes stack-effect bugs much easier to see.
   893→
   894→C. Return Stack pane
   895→Treat this as equally important.
   896→
   897→Suggested format
   898→Return Stack (depth 3)
   899→  [0] 0x1012   return to QUIT + 2
   900→  [1] 0x1846   return to INTERPRET + 6
   901→  [2] 0x1A10   loop frame / DO..LOOP   <- TOP
   902→Recommended decoding
   903→Try to classify each entry:
   904→
   905→return address into colon body
   906→
   907→loop frame
   908→
   909→explicit user temporary pushed by >R
   910→
   911→unknown raw cell
   912→
   913→Why it matters
   914→A lot of hard-to-find Forth bugs are:
   915→
   916→broken control flow
   917→
   918→bad loop state
   919→
   920→accidental return-stack corruption
   921→
   922→A raw hex view is not enough. The symbolic decode is the useful part.
   923→
   924→D. Selected Object pane
   925→This is the inspector on the right side.
   926→When the user clicks a word, a stack item, a dictionary row, or a memory cell, it updates.
   927→
   928→Use tabs:
   929→
   930→Word
   931→
   932→Memory
   933→
   934→Disasm
   935→
   936→Regions
   937→
   938→Source
   939→
   940→3. Word tab mockup
   941→This is the most important tab for learning.
   942→
   943→Example: colon definition
   944→Name: SQUARE
   945→Kind: colon definition
   946→Header addr: 0x2C10
   947→Name addr:   0x2C10
   948→xt/CFA addr: 0x2C18
   949→Body/PFA:    0x2C1A
   950→
   951→Flags:
   952→  immediate: no
   953→  hidden:    no
   954→  compile-only: no
   955→
   956→Link:
   957→  previous -> DUP (0x2BF0)
   958→
   959→Execution:
   960→  code field -> DOCOL
   961→
   962→Body:
   963→  [0] 0x1100  xt DUP
   964→  [1] 0x1120  xt *
   965→  [2] 0x1004  xt EXIT
   966→Example: constant
   967→Name: TEN
   968→Kind: constant
   969→Header addr: 0x2C30
   970→Flags: none
   971→
   972→Link:
   973→  previous -> SQUARE
   974→
   975→Execution:
   976→  code field -> DOCON
   977→
   978→Body:
   979→  [0] 0x000A   10
   980→Example: variable
   981→Name: COUNT
   982→Kind: variable
   983→Execution:
   984→  code field -> DOVAR
   985→
   986→Body:
   987→  [0] 0x0000   current contents
   988→Example: primitive
   989→Name: DUP
   990→Kind: primitive
   991→Execution:
   992→  native entry -> 0x0810
   993→Machine code bytes:
   994→  81 32 90 ...
   995→4. Memory tab mockup
   996→This should have two synchronized views:
   997→
   998→cell view
   999→
  1000→byte view
  1001→
  1002→Cell-oriented view
  1003→This is the default for stacks and threaded bodies.
  1004→
  1005→Address   Cell       Meaning
  1006→0x2C18    0x0800     DOCOL
  1007→0x2C1A    0x1100     xt DUP
  1008→0x2C1C    0x1120     xt *
  1009→0x2C1E    0x1004     xt EXIT
  1010→Byte-oriented view
  1011→Use for headers, names, flags, and raw corruption analysis.
  1012→
  1013→0x2C10:  F0 2B 06 53 51 55 41 52 45 00 00 08 00 11 20 11
  1014→0x2C20:  04 10 ...
  1015→Good UI behavior
  1016→Clicking a cell highlights:
  1017→
  1018→corresponding bytes
  1019→
  1020→meaning
  1021→
  1022→cross-links to the referenced word
  1023→
  1024→So clicking 0x1100 would jump to DUP.
  1025→
  1026→5. Disassembly tab
  1027→This depends on word kind.
  1028→
  1029→For colon definitions
  1030→Show a decoded threaded listing, not raw CPU instructions.
  1031→
  1032→SQUARE
  1033→  +0  DUP
  1034→  +1  *
  1035→  +2  EXIT
  1036→For branch-heavy definitions:
  1037→
  1038→ABS
  1039→  +0  DUP
  1040→  +1  0<
  1041→  +2  ?BRANCH  -> +5
  1042→  +3  NEGATE
  1043→  +4  EXIT
  1044→  +5  EXIT
  1045→For primitives
  1046→Show native disassembly:
  1047→
  1048→DUP
  1049→  0x0810  LOAD T0, [DSP]
  1050→  0x0812  STORE T0, [DSP-1]
  1051→  0x0814  DEC DSP
  1052→  0x0816  NEXT
  1053→Why separate this tab
  1054→Colon words want word-level decode.
  1055→Primitives want machine-level decode.
  1056→Trying to force both into one view becomes confusing.
  1057→
  1058→6. Regions tab / memory map
  1059→This is where your web UI can really shine.
  1060→
  1061→You want a visual memory map with live pointers.
  1062→
  1063→Example region bar
  1064→0x0000 +----------------------------------------------------------------------+ 0x7FFF
  1065→       | Kernel | Primitives | Dictionary >>>>>>> | Free | <<< RStack | <<< DStack |
  1066→       +----------------------------------------------------------------------+
  1067→                ^                   ^              ^      ^            ^
  1068→                |                   |              |      |            |
  1069→             DOCOL              LATEST/HERE      gap     RP           SP
  1070→Or vertically:
  1071→
  1072→0x0000  Kernel / low runtime
  1073→0x0800  Primitive code
  1074→0x1000  Dictionary start
  1075→0x2C80  HERE / latest compiled word
  1076→0x5000  Free space
  1077→0x6800  Return stack
  1078→0x7400  Data stack
  1079→0x7FFF  RAM end
  1080→Show for each region
  1081→start
  1082→
  1083→end
  1084→
  1085→current pointer
  1086→
  1087→growth direction
  1088→
  1089→percent full
  1090→
  1091→high-water mark
  1092→
  1093→warning threshold
  1094→
  1095→Color suggestions
  1096→kernel/primitives: dark gray
  1097→
  1098→dictionary: blue
  1099→
  1100→free: neutral
  1101→
  1102→return stack: orange
  1103→
  1104→data stack: green
  1105→
  1106→invalid/overlap danger: red
  1107→
  1108→7. Dictionary pane
  1109→This should be a sortable/filterable list.
  1110→
  1111→Columns
  1112→Addr	Name	Kind	Flags	Link	xt/CFA	Size
  1113→0x2BF0	DUP	prim	-	0x2BD0	0x0810	4
  1114→0x2C10	SQUARE	colon	-	0x2BF0	DOCOL	3
  1115→0x2C30	TEN	const	-	0x2C10	DOCON	1
  1116→Filters
  1117→primitives only
  1118→
  1119→colon definitions only
  1120→
  1121→immediate words
  1122→
  1123→hidden words
  1124→
  1125→recently compiled
  1126→
  1127→contains name
  1128→
  1129→wordlist / vocabulary
  1130→
  1131→Optional secondary view
  1132→A link-chain graph:
  1133→
  1134→LATEST
  1135→  -> TEN
  1136→  -> SQUARE
  1137→  -> DUP
  1138→  -> DROP
  1139→  -> ...
  1140→This is very useful for teaching redefinition and shadowing.
  1141→
  1142→8. Compile-event log
  1143→For Forth, this is more useful than a generic log console.
  1144→
  1145→Example
  1146→[compile] : SQUARE
  1147→[compile]   append xt DUP
  1148→[compile]   append xt *
  1149→[compile]   append xt EXIT
  1150→[compile] ;  finalize word SQUARE at 0x2C10
  1151→
  1152→[interpret] token "5" -> number
  1153→[interpret] push 5
  1154→[interpret] token "SQUARE" -> found word
  1155→[execute] SQUARE
  1156→Why it matters
  1157→This explains:
  1158→
  1159→what was compiled
  1160→
  1161→what was executed immediately
  1162→
  1163→when immediate words ran
  1164→
  1165→why the dictionary changed
  1166→
  1167→It is especially useful when debugging the text interpreter and compiler.
  1168→
  1169→9. Breakpoints and watchpoints UI
  1170→A Forth debugger should support more than address breakpoints.
  1171→
  1172→Useful breakpoint types
  1173→By word
  1174→break on entry to INTERPRET
  1175→
  1176→break on entry to ;
  1177→
  1178→break on exit from SQUARE
  1179→
  1180→By event
  1181→break when new word is created
  1182→
  1183→break when STATE changes
  1184→
  1185→break when dictionary grows past threshold
  1186→
  1187→break on unknown token
  1188→
  1189→break on stack underflow
  1190→
  1191→break on return stack misuse
  1192→
  1193→By address / cell
  1194→break when cell 0x2C18 changes
  1195→
  1196→break when HERE changes
  1197→
  1198→break on write to variable COUNT
  1199→
  1200→UI mockup
  1201→Breakpoints
  1202→[x] on word entry: INTERPRET
  1203→[ ] on word exit:  QUIT
  1204→[x] on STATE change
  1205→[x] on stack underflow
  1206→[ ] on address write: 0x2C18
  1207→10. Concrete first-version mock UI
  1208→Here is a more detailed textual wireframe.
  1209→
  1210→+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
  1211→| Run  Pause  Step Word  Step Prim  Step Out  Reset  Follow IP  Show Cells  Show Bytes  Dark Mode |
  1212→+-------------------------------+------------------------------+-----------------------------------+
  1213→| EXECUTION                     | STACKS                       | SELECTED WORD                     |
  1214→|-------------------------------|------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
  1215→| Current word: INTERPRET       | Data Stack (depth 3)         | Name: SQUARE                      |
  1216→| Kind: colon                   | [0] 0x0005  5                | Kind: colon                       |
  1217→| xt: 0x1840                    | [1] 0x0005  5                | Header: 0x2C10                    |
  1218→| IP: +5                        | [2] 0x2C10  xt SQUARE <-TOS  | Link -> DUP                       |
  1219→|                               |                              | Flags: none                       |
  1220→| Caller chain:                 | Return Stack (depth 2)       | Code field: DOCOL                 |
  1221→| QUIT                          | [0] return QUIT + 2          | Body:                             |
  1222→| INTERPRET                     | [1] return INTERPRET + 6     |   +0 DUP                          |
  1223→| FIND                          |                              |   +1 *                            |
  1224→|                               |                              |   +2 EXIT                         |
  1225→| Token: "SQUARE"               |                              |                                   |
  1226→| STATE: 0                      |                              |                                   |
  1227→| BASE: 10                      |                              |                                   |
  1228→| HERE: 0x2C80                  |                              |                                   |
  1229→+-------------------------------+------------------------------+-----------------------------------+
  1230→| DICTIONARY                                                                                       |
  1231→| Addr    Name        Kind     Flags      Link    xt/CFA    Size                                   |
  1232→| 2BF0    DUP         prim     -          2BD0    0810      1                                      |
  1233→| 2C10    SQUARE      colon    -          2BF0    DOCOL     3                                      |
  1234→| 2C30    TEN         const    -          2C10    DOCON     1                                      |
  1235→+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
  1236→| COMPILE / EXECUTION LOG                                                                          |
  1237→| [interpret] token "5" -> push 5                                                                  |
  1238→| [interpret] token "SQUARE" -> execute                                                            |
  1239→| [execute] enter SQUARE                                                                           |
  1240→| [execute] DUP                                                                                    |
  1241→| [execute] *                                                                                      |
  1242→| [execute] EXIT                                                                                   |
  1243→+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
  1244→| REGIONS                                                                                          |
  1245→| Kernel | Primitives | Dictionary>>>>>>>>>>> | Free Space | <<Return Stack | <<Data Stack         |
  1246→|         0x0800      0x1000...0x2C80         0x2C81...67FF  6800...73FF       7400...7FFF         |
  1247→| CPU: PC=0x0810 SP=0x7F20 RP=0x6FF8 | Forth: IP=0x2C1C W=0x1100 | warnings: none                 |
  1248→+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
  1249→11. What to emphasize visually in memory
  1250→You asked specifically how to show different memory regions, usage, and conventions. For Forth, these are the most important conventions to make visible.
  1251→
  1252→A. Dictionary grows one way, stacks grow the other
  1253→This is one of the classic Forth realities.
  1254→
  1255→Show this graphically, not just numerically.
  1256→
  1257→Bad:
  1258→
  1259→HERE=0x2C80
  1260→
  1261→SP=0x7400
  1262→
  1263→Good:
  1264→
  1265→a bar with arrows:
  1266→
  1267→Dictionary >>>
  1268→
  1269→<<< Return Stack
  1270→
  1271→<<< Data Stack
  1272→
  1273→That makes collision risk intuitive.
  1274→
  1275→B. Header fields should be named explicitly
  1276→Do not make the user mentally decode raw bytes.
  1277→
  1278→For each word, show:
  1279→
  1280→header address
  1281→
  1282→link
  1283→
  1284→name/flags
  1285→
  1286→code field
  1287→
  1288→body start
  1289→
  1290→Even if your internal layout differs from a classic fig-Forth style layout, present a normalized conceptual display.
  1291→
  1292→C. Distinguish code, threaded code, and data
  1293→A single memory renderer is not enough.
  1294→
  1295→You want distinct renderers for:
  1296→
  1297→native primitive
  1298→
  1299→colon body
  1300→
  1301→constant body
  1302→
  1303→variable body
  1304→
  1305→CREATE/DOES> body
  1306→
  1307→Each one should have different icons/colors.
  1308→
  1309→For example:
  1310→
  1311→primitive = chip icon
  1312→
  1313→colon = list icon
  1314→
  1315→constant = number icon
  1316→
  1317→variable = box icon
  1318→
  1319→created object = cube icon
  1320→
  1321→D. Show symbolic targets for cells
  1322→Any cell that points into a known object should get a symbolic label.
  1323→
  1324→Instead of just:
  1325→
  1326→0x1100
  1327→show:
  1328→
  1329→0x1100   xt DUP
  1330→Instead of:
  1331→
  1332→0x2C1C
  1333→show:
  1334→
  1335→body of SQUARE + 1 cell
  1336→This makes the debugger feel Forth-aware rather than merely hex-aware.
  1337→
  1338→12. Minimal viable implementation order
  1339→To keep this practical, I would build the UI in this order.
  1340→
  1341→Phase 1: core live debugging
  1342→current word
  1343→
  1344→data stack
  1345→
  1346→return stack
  1347→
  1348→dictionary list
  1349→
  1350→selected-word inspector
  1351→
  1352→region map with growth directions
  1353→
  1354→Phase 2: semantic decoding
  1355→classify words by kind
  1356→
  1357→decode colon bodies into word lists
  1358→
  1359→symbolic return-stack decoding
  1360→
  1361→execution trace
  1362→
  1363→Phase 3: power features
  1364→breakpoints on word entry/exit
  1365→
  1366→compile-event log
  1367→
  1368→watchpoints on memory/HERE/STATE
  1369→
  1370→source/token view
  1371→
  1372→linked raw byte/cell panes
  1373→
  1374→Phase 4: teaching features
  1375→stack-effect overlays
  1376→
  1377→call graph
  1378→
  1379→word redefinition visualization
  1380→
  1381→vocabulary/search order pane
  1382→
  1383→animation of compilation into dictionary memory
  1384→
  1385→13. Recommended data model behind the UI
  1386→Since you are doing Yew/Rust/WASM, it helps to structure the debugger state semantically.
  1387→
  1388→Core runtime snapshot
  1389→struct ForthSnapshot {
  1390→    cpu: CpuState,
  1391→    vm: VmState,
  1392→    regions: Vec<MemoryRegion>,
  1393→    data_stack: Vec<CellValue>,
  1394→    return_stack: Vec<ReturnEntry>,
  1395→    dictionary: Vec<WordInfo>,
  1396→    current_word: Option<WordRef>,
  1397→    trace: Vec<TraceEvent>,
  1398→    compile_log: Vec<CompileEvent>,
  1399→}
  1400→Word metadata
  1401→struct WordInfo {
  1402→    name: String,
  1403→    header_addr: u32,
  1404→    link_addr: Option<u32>,
  1405→    flags: WordFlags,
  1406→    kind: WordKind,
  1407→    cfa_addr: u32,
  1408→    body_addr: Option<u32>,
  1409→    body_cells: Vec<u32>,
  1410→}
  1411→Word kinds
  1412→enum WordKind {
  1413→    Primitive,
  1414→    Colon,
  1415→    Constant,
  1416→    Variable,
  1417→    CreateDoes,
  1418→    Deferred,
  1419→    Unknown,
  1420→}
  1421→Return stack entries
  1422→enum ReturnEntry {
  1423→    ReturnToWord { word: String, offset_cells: usize },
  1424→    LoopFrame { info: String },
  1425→    UserTemp { raw: u32 },
  1426→    Unknown { raw: u32 },
  1427→}
  1428→That semantic layer is what enables a nice debugger.
  1429→
  1430→14. Best single design principle
  1431→If I had to reduce it to one rule:
  1432→
  1433→Show Forth concepts first, raw bytes second
  1434→So the user should first see:
  1435→
  1436→SQUARE = colon word
  1437→
  1438→body = DUP * EXIT
  1439→
  1440→return stack entry = return to INTERPRET + 6
  1441→
  1442→Then, with one click, they can drill into the bytes and addresses.
  1443→
  1444→That gives you both:
  1445→
  1446→a teaching/debugging tool
  1447→
  1448→a memory validation tool
  1449→
  1450→15. My recommendation for your first mock implementation
  1451→For version 1, I would build exactly these visible panels:
  1452→
  1453→Always visible
  1454→current word / caller chain
  1455→
  1456→data stack
  1457→
  1458→return stack
  1459→
  1460→selected word inspector
  1461→
  1462→dictionary table
  1463→
  1464→region usage bar
  1465→
  1466→CPU + VM register strip
  1467→
  1468→Hidden behind tabs
  1469→raw memory
  1470→
  1471→native disassembly
  1472→
  1473→compile log
  1474→
  1475→breakpoints
  1476→
  1477→That will already feel like a real Forth debugger, and it will map well to a small emulator and a web UI.
  1478→
  1479→If you want, next I can turn this into either:
  1480→
  1481→a more concrete Yew component breakdown, or
  1482→
  1483→a sample JSON/debug protocol between emulator and web UI.
  1484→
  1485→
  1486→
  1487→
  1488→
  1489→

file.content:
What does a Forth debugger look like?  What is important when looking at how words are stored in-memory?  I can add a web UI debugger (in Yew/Rust/WASM) that shows memory and registers.  How should I show the different memory regions/usage/conventions for Forth code and data?


Thought for 7s
You want a debugger/UI that helps you understand how Forth executes and how words live in memory, not just a generic CPU debugger. That means the design should make three things visible at once:

The machine state: registers, PC/IP, memory.

The Forth VM state: data stack, return stack, current word, next word, interpret/compile state.

The dictionary state: how words are laid out, linked, found, and executed.

That is what makes a Forth debugger feel different from a C or assembly debugger.

What a Forth debugger usually looks like
Historically, a Forth debugger is often word-oriented, not instruction-oriented. A typical debugger shows or lets you inspect:

the data stack

the return stack

the currently executing word

the input source / source location

the dictionary / word definition

optionally a memory dump and disassembly

For example, Gforth’s debugging and examining tools emphasize stack inspection, source-level stepping, and examining code/data rather than only raw CPU stepping. .s shows the data stack non-destructively, with the top of stack shown at the right; Gforth also documents return-stack concerns explicitly because misuse usually crashes the program. 

So the most useful mental model is:

A Forth debugger is a combined stack tracer + dictionary browser + optional machine debugger.

The most important views to include
1. Data stack
This is the first-class UI element in Forth.

Show:

depth

cells from bottom to top

top-of-stack clearly highlighted

multiple display formats:

signed decimal

unsigned

hex

address/symbolic if inside known regions

Why it matters:
Most Forth bugs are stack-effect bugs before they are “CPU bugs.”

Recommended UI:

vertical stack, top at bottom or top at right; just be consistent

include a small inferred tag:

value

addr

xt

flag

char

2. Return stack
This is equally important in Forth, but many tools under-emphasize it.

Show:

raw contents

symbolic interpretation when possible:

return address into a colon definition

loop parameters

temporary values if the program explicitly used >R R> R@

Why it matters:
In standard Forth systems, the return stack holds return addresses and loop-control state, and incorrect use often ends in a crash. 

Recommended UI:

separate pane from data stack

color-code entries:

return address

loop frame

user-pushed temporary

if an entry points into a word body, annotate:

return into FOO + 3 cells

3. Current execution view
This is the heart of a Forth debugger.

Show:

current instruction pointer / threaded IP

current word being executed

caller word

next few words to be executed

current source line if available

If you have indirect-threaded or token-threaded execution, raw machine PC is often less useful than the Forth IP stepping through word bodies.

Recommended UI:

breadcrumb:

MAIN -> PARSE-LINE -> INTERPRET -> FIND

current slot highlighted inside the word body

“step word”, “step primitive”, “step out”, “run to next colon word”

4. Dictionary browser
This is the most distinctive Forth-specific panel.

A Forth dictionary entry traditionally contains some form of:

link field: pointer to previous definition

name field

code field / code pointer field

parameter/body field

This terminology appears widely in Forth explanations: link field, name field, code field address (CFA), parameter field address (PFA), and body. 

Your UI should let the user click a word and see:

word name

flags:

immediate

hidden/smudged

compile-only if applicable

link to previous word

execution token / CFA

body/PFA

interpreted type:

primitive

colon definition

constant

variable

CREATEd word

DOES> word

This is where Forth becomes teachable.

What is important when looking at words in memory
The key question is not just “what bytes are here?” but:

How does this word participate in name lookup, compilation, and execution?

You want to make the following visible.

A. Header vs body
A word usually has a header and a body.

Header commonly includes:

link

name

flags/length

Body commonly includes:

code field / execution behavior

parameter data or compiled word list

This distinction is explicitly described in Forth references: the “head” is name/link information, while the “body” is code/data; colon definitions use the code field to point to the address interpreter, while other defining words arrange different runtime behavior. 

UI suggestion:
Use a structured expandable row like:

WORD: DUP
  Header
    Link: 0x1234 -> DROP
    Name: "DUP"
    Flags: immediate=0 hidden=0
  Execution
    CFA / xt: 0x87A0
    Kind: primitive
  Body
    <none or primitive code pointer>
For a colon definition:

WORD: SQUARE
  Header
    Link: ...
    Name: "SQUARE"
  Execution
    CFA: DOCOL
    Kind: colon
  Body
    0: DUP
    1: *
    2: EXIT
B. The link chain
Traditional Forth dictionaries are often organized as a linked list of definitions, with the newest word first and the link field pointing to the previous definition. JonesForth and several explanatory sources describe this structure directly. 

Why it matters:

explains name lookup

shows shadowing/redefinition

makes hidden/smudged words understandable

UI suggestion:
Show both:

linear memory order

search order / link-chain order

Those are not always identical in advanced systems.

C. Execution semantics
The same “word” name can correspond to different runtime forms:

primitive

colon definition

constant

variable

deferred word

CREATE ... DOES> instance

This matters more than raw bytes. A debugger should decode a word according to its defining semantics.

Example:

a CONSTANT’s body is data, not a sequence of called words

a colon definition body is a list of execution tokens / threaded addresses

a VARIABLE body is storage

a CREATEd word’s body is usually user data, while DOES> supplies runtime behavior

The Forth execution model and defining-word structure are central in “Under the Hood” style explanations. 

So in your UI, every word should have a kind and a custom renderer.

D. Immediate and hidden flags
These are crucial during compilation and debugging compiler behavior.

JonesForth explicitly discusses flag bits for hidden and immediate words in dictionary headers. 

Why it matters:

explains why a word executes during compilation

explains why some words do not appear in searches

helps debug the text interpreter/compiler

UI suggestion:
Display flags prominently, not buried in a raw byte dump.

E. Code field and parameter field boundaries
If you are teaching or debugging a threaded Forth, the most educational view is:

CFA / code field

PFA / body

current IP relative to the PFA

This helps the user answer:

Is the word executable?

What runtime code handles it?

Is the body code or data?

Where will >BODY land?

How to show memory regions in a web debugger
You said you can add a Yew/Rust/WASM debugger with memory and registers. For Forth, I would not begin with a single giant hex dump. Start with a memory map view, then drill down.

Recommended top-level regions
Show a region map like this:

Region	Purpose	Grows	Important indicators
Code / primitives	native code / kernel	fixed or upward	entry points, primitive table
Dictionary / heap	word headers + bodies	upward	HERE, latest word, free space
Data stack	parameters	downward or upward	SP, depth, underflow/overflow
Return stack	return addresses, loop frames	downward or upward	RP, nesting depth
User area	task-local vars	fixed	BASE, STATE, etc.
TIB / input buffer	source text	variable	parse pointer
PAD / scratch	transient formatting	variable	recent temporary usage
Variables/buffers	application data	variable	named blocks
These exact region boundaries vary across Forths, but the concepts—dictionary, data stack, return stack, input buffer, scratch area—are standard enough to make them excellent UI concepts. References discussing dictionary, input message buffer, pad, stacks, and execution model support this breakdown. 

Region-map UI
Use a horizontal or vertical bar:

0x000000 ├─ kernel/primitives
0x001000 ├─ dictionary start
0x003840 ├─ latest word / HERE
0x004000 ├─ free
0x007000 ├─ PAD / TIB / user area
0x008000 ├─ return stack top
0x009000 ├─ data stack top
0x00A000 └─ end RAM
Make each region clickable.

The most useful visualizations
1. Dual representation: semantic + raw
Every pane should support two modes:

semantic mode: word names, flags, fields, stack annotations

raw mode: hex bytes / cells

For example, dictionary browser:

[semantic]
FOO
  link -> BAR
  flags -> immediate
  cfa  -> DOCOL
  body -> DUP * EXIT
and

[raw]
001240: 34 12 83 46 4F 4F 00 ...
001248: A0 00
00124A: 91 10 77 02 E0 00
This is ideal for teaching and for validating your implementation.

2. Word-body disassembly view
For a selected word, show its body as decoded cells:

For colon definitions:

called word names

branches with target annotations

literals decoded

current IP highlighted

Example:

: ABS
  0: DUP
  1: 0<
  2: ?BRANCH -> 5
  3: NEGATE
  4: EXIT
  5: EXIT
For primitives:

native disassembly or pseudo-op view

For constants/variables:

show stored cell values

3. Search-order and vocabulary view
If you later support vocabularies or wordlists, include:

current search order

current compilation wordlist

latest word per wordlist

Without this, a lot of “why did FIND return that word?” becomes mysterious.

4. Stack-effect overlay
If you know or infer stack comments, display them while stepping.

Example:

DUP      ( x -- x x )
*        ( a b -- a*b )
This is not strictly required for a machine debugger, but it is hugely useful for a Forth debugger.

Best conventions for showing Forth memory
Prefer cells over bytes
Forth is naturally cell-oriented.

So the default memory dump for dictionary bodies and stacks should be in cells, with an option to switch to bytes.

For example:

Addr      Cell(hex)   Meaning
0x1200    0x08A0      CFA = DOCOL
0x1202    0x1310      xt DUP
0x1204    0x1422      xt *
0x1206    0x0810      xt EXIT
For name/header area, byte view is useful.
For code/data bodies, cell view is better.

Show growth directions
This matters a lot in Forth.

Always label:

dictionary grows up

stacks grow down or up depending on your implementation

Show collision risk visually:

free memory band between dictionary and stacks

warning when margin is small

This matters especially on small targets.

Show symbolic addresses
Instead of only 0x1234, annotate:

xt DUP

body of FOO + 2 cells

return to BAR + 1

TIB + 12

user variable STATE

That turns a memory monitor into a real debugger.

A practical UI layout for your Yew/Rust/WASM debugger
Here is the layout I would recommend.

Main layout
Left column: execution
current word

caller chain

step controls

source text / current token

interpret state:

STATE

BASE

current input pointer

Center column: stacks
data stack

return stack

optional floating stack later

Right column: selected memory object
Tabs:

Word

Memory

Disasm

Dictionary

Regions

Bottom strip: registers and status
CPU registers

machine PC/SP

Forth IP/W if applicable

breakpoints/watchpoints

cycle count if emulator supports it

Breakpoints and watchpoints that matter in Forth
A normal debugger breaks on addresses. A Forth debugger should also break on:

entry to a named word

exit from a named word

definition of a new word

changes to STATE

writes to HERE

dictionary overflow / stack near-collision

ABORT, THROW, or invalid word lookup

return-stack misuse

Very valuable watchpoints:

break when a particular header is created

break when a given variable or user variable changes

break when a dictionary cell is compiled

Pros and cons of different visualization styles
A. Raw hex-dump-first
Pros

closest to the machine

easiest to implement first

great for validating emulator correctness

Cons

bad for learning Forth

hides semantics of headers and threaded code

too much mental decoding

B. Semantic Forth-first
Pros

best for teaching

helps explain execution and compilation

easier to reason about bugs in words

Cons

requires decoders for multiple word kinds

can hide low-level corruption unless raw view exists

C. Split semantic/raw view
Pros

best overall

supports both teaching and implementation validation

scales from simple indirect-threaded Forth to richer systems

Cons

more UI work

For your use case, C is the clear winner.

A concrete set of conventions I would use
Dictionary pane
Show rows like:

Addr	Name	Kind	Flags	Link	CFA	Body
0x1200	DUP	primitive	-	0x11E0	0x0800	primitive code
0x1210	SQUARE	colon	-	0x1200	DOCOL	DUP * EXIT
0x1220	TEN	constant	-	0x1210	(CONST)	10
Memory-region pane
Show:

start/end address

current pointers:

HERE

LATEST

SP

RP

TIB

growth direction arrows

Stack pane
Show cells and symbolic decode:

Data stack
  [0]  0x0003   3
  [1]  0x1200   xt SQUARE   <- TOS
Word pane
Show selected word as:

Name: SQUARE
Kind: colon definition
Header address: 0x1210
Link: 0x1200 -> DUP
Flags: none
CFA: DOCOL
Body/PFA:
  0: DUP
  1: *
  2: EXIT
What matters most for small-target Forth systems
Since your target sounds resource-constrained and you are building your own environment, these are the most important conventions to expose:

Dictionary growth and free space

Data stack depth and high-water mark

Return stack depth and misuse

Header layout of each word

CFA/body decode

Whether a word is immediate or hidden

Current compile vs interpret mode

Exact location of HERE and latest word

If you only build the first version, build around those.

Recommended first milestone
Implement these panes first:

Data stack

Return stack

Current word / IP

Dictionary list

Selected-word inspector

Region map

That will already feel like a real Forth debugger.

Then add:

word breakpoints

source mapping

raw hex/cell dump

compile-event trace

Bottom line
A good Forth debugger should not look like “just memory and registers.” It should look like:

a stack debugger

a dictionary explorer

a word-level tracer

and only secondarily a machine debugger

The most important memory concepts to show are:

header vs body

link chain

CFA/xt and PFA/body

word kind

flags

dictionary growth

data stack / return stack separation

That combination will make your Yew/Rust/WASM debugger genuinely useful for learning, implementing, and debugging Forth. The overall emphasis on stack inspection, source/word-level stepping, dictionary structure, and header/body terminology is consistent with modern Gforth documentation and classic explanatory material on Forth internals. 

I can sketch a concrete mock UI layout next, including exact panes, labels, and a sample memory map for a minimal indirect-threaded Forth.


sure that would help

Below is a concrete mock debugger layout for a small Forth, especially a traditional threaded Forth on a constrained target. I will assume:

separate data stack and return stack

a dictionary growing upward

either:

indirect-threaded or direct-threaded code, or

a close equivalent where colon definitions are still visible as bodies of cells/tokens

a machine/debugger layer underneath with registers and raw memory

The goal is to let you understand, at a glance:

what word is executing

what the stacks contain

what the selected word looks like in memory

how close memory regions are to collision

what just got compiled or executed

1. High-level screen layout
A very practical first layout is a three-column + bottom strip arrangement.

+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Toolbar: [Run] [Pause] [Step Word] [Step Primitive] [Step Out] [Reset] [Compile Log] |
+-------------------------+--------------------------------+---------------------------+
| Execution / Trace       | Stacks                         | Selected Object           |
|                         |                                |                           |
| Current word            | Data Stack                     | [Word] [Memory] [Disasm] |
| Caller chain            | Return Stack                   | [Regions] [Source]       |
| Current token/IP        |                                |                           |
| Current input token     |                                | Details of selected word |
| STATE / BASE / HERE     |                                | or selected memory cell  |
|                         |                                |                           |
+-------------------------+--------------------------------+---------------------------+
| Dictionary / Search Order / Compile Events / Breakpoints                             |
+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| CPU registers | Forth VM registers | region usage bar | warnings/errors             |
+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
This is better than a single hex dump because it separates:

live execution

live stacks

static structure

raw inspection

2. Pane-by-pane design
A. Execution / Trace pane
This should be the primary pane when stepping.

Show:
Current word

Word kind

Execution token / code address

Current IP offset within the word

Caller chain

Current parsed token

Interpreter state

Small rolling trace

Example
Current word: INTERPRET
Kind: colon
xt/CFA: 0x1840
IP: body + 5 cells

Caller chain:
  QUIT
  INTERPRET
  FIND
  NUMBER?

Current token:
  "SQUARE"

Interpreter state:
  STATE = 0   (interpret)
  BASE  = 10
  HERE  = 0x2C80
  LATEST = 0x2C68

Next operations:
  5: FIND
  6: ?DUP
  7: IF
  8: EXECUTE
Why this matters
This pane answers the most common Forth questions:

What word am I in?

What does it think the next thing is?

Am I interpreting or compiling?

Did lookup fail, or did execution fail?

B. Data Stack pane
This should always be visible.

Suggested format
Use a vertical stack, with top-of-stack at the bottom.
That visually matches “push goes down onto the top.”

Data Stack (depth 4)
  [0] 0x0003   3
  [1] 0x0010   16
  [2] 0x2A40   addr BUFFER
  [3] 0x1840   xt INTERPRET   <- TOS
Recommended features
show index from bottom

highlight TOS

support display modes:

decimal

hex

signed

symbolic

optional inferred type badges:

val

addr

xt

flag

Nice extra
A mini stack history:

Previous:
  before DUP:   [ 5 ]
  after  DUP:   [ 5 5 ]
That makes stack-effect bugs much easier to see.

C. Return Stack pane
Treat this as equally important.

Suggested format
Return Stack (depth 3)
  [0] 0x1012   return to QUIT + 2
  [1] 0x1846   return to INTERPRET + 6
  [2] 0x1A10   loop frame / DO..LOOP   <- TOP
Recommended decoding
Try to classify each entry:

return address into colon body

loop frame

explicit user temporary pushed by >R

unknown raw cell

Why it matters
A lot of hard-to-find Forth bugs are:

broken control flow

bad loop state

accidental return-stack corruption

A raw hex view is not enough. The symbolic decode is the useful part.

D. Selected Object pane
This is the inspector on the right side.
When the user clicks a word, a stack item, a dictionary row, or a memory cell, it updates.

Use tabs:

Word

Memory

Disasm

Regions

Source

3. Word tab mockup
This is the most important tab for learning.

Example: colon definition
Name: SQUARE
Kind: colon definition
Header addr: 0x2C10
Name addr:   0x2C10
xt/CFA addr: 0x2C18
Body/PFA:    0x2C1A

Flags:
  immediate: no
  hidden:    no
  compile-only: no

Link:
  previous -> DUP (0x2BF0)

Execution:
  code field -> DOCOL

Body:
  [0] 0x1100  xt DUP
  [1] 0x1120  xt *
  [2] 0x1004  xt EXIT
Example: constant
Name: TEN
Kind: constant
Header addr: 0x2C30
Flags: none

Link:
  previous -> SQUARE

Execution:
  code field -> DOCON

Body:
  [0] 0x000A   10
Example: variable
Name: COUNT
Kind: variable
Execution:
  code field -> DOVAR

Body:
  [0] 0x0000   current contents
Example: primitive
Name: DUP
Kind: primitive
Execution:
  native entry -> 0x0810
Machine code bytes:
  81 32 90 ...
4. Memory tab mockup
This should have two synchronized views:

cell view

byte view

Cell-oriented view
This is the default for stacks and threaded bodies.

Address   Cell       Meaning
0x2C18    0x0800     DOCOL
0x2C1A    0x1100     xt DUP
0x2C1C    0x1120     xt *
0x2C1E    0x1004     xt EXIT
Byte-oriented view
Use for headers, names, flags, and raw corruption analysis.

0x2C10:  F0 2B 06 53 51 55 41 52 45 00 00 08 00 11 20 11
0x2C20:  04 10 ...
Good UI behavior
Clicking a cell highlights:

corresponding bytes

meaning

cross-links to the referenced word

So clicking 0x1100 would jump to DUP.

5. Disassembly tab
This depends on word kind.

For colon definitions
Show a decoded threaded listing, not raw CPU instructions.

SQUARE
  +0  DUP
  +1  *
  +2  EXIT
For branch-heavy definitions:

ABS
  +0  DUP
  +1  0<
  +2  ?BRANCH  -> +5
  +3  NEGATE
  +4  EXIT
  +5  EXIT
For primitives
Show native disassembly:

DUP
  0x0810  LOAD T0, [DSP]
  0x0812  STORE T0, [DSP-1]
  0x0814  DEC DSP
  0x0816  NEXT
Why separate this tab
Colon words want word-level decode.
Primitives want machine-level decode.
Trying to force both into one view becomes confusing.

6. Regions tab / memory map
This is where your web UI can really shine.

You want a visual memory map with live pointers.

Example region bar
0x0000 +----------------------------------------------------------------------+ 0x7FFF
       | Kernel | Primitives | Dictionary >>>>>>> | Free | <<< RStack | <<< DStack |
       +----------------------------------------------------------------------+
                ^                   ^              ^      ^            ^
                |                   |              |      |            |
             DOCOL              LATEST/HERE      gap     RP           SP
Or vertically:

0x0000  Kernel / low runtime
0x0800  Primitive code
0x1000  Dictionary start
0x2C80  HERE / latest compiled word
0x5000  Free space
0x6800  Return stack
0x7400  Data stack
0x7FFF  RAM end
Show for each region
start

end

current pointer

growth direction

percent full

high-water mark

warning threshold

Color suggestions
kernel/primitives: dark gray

dictionary: blue

free: neutral

return stack: orange

data stack: green

invalid/overlap danger: red

7. Dictionary pane
This should be a sortable/filterable list.

Columns
Addr	Name	Kind	Flags	Link	xt/CFA	Size
0x2BF0	DUP	prim	-	0x2BD0	0x0810	4
0x2C10	SQUARE	colon	-	0x2BF0	DOCOL	3
0x2C30	TEN	const	-	0x2C10	DOCON	1
Filters
primitives only

colon definitions only

immediate words

hidden words

recently compiled

contains name

wordlist / vocabulary

Optional secondary view
A link-chain graph:

LATEST
  -> TEN
  -> SQUARE
  -> DUP
  -> DROP
  -> ...
This is very useful for teaching redefinition and shadowing.

8. Compile-event log
For Forth, this is more useful than a generic log console.

Example
[compile] : SQUARE
[compile]   append xt DUP
[compile]   append xt *
[compile]   append xt EXIT
[compile] ;  finalize word SQUARE at 0x2C10

[interpret] token "5" -> number
[interpret] push 5
[interpret] token "SQUARE" -> found word
[execute] SQUARE
Why it matters
This explains:

what was compiled

what was executed immediately

when immediate words ran

why the dictionary changed

It is especially useful when debugging the text interpreter and compiler.

9. Breakpoints and watchpoints UI
A Forth debugger should support more than address breakpoints.

Useful breakpoint types
By word
break on entry to INTERPRET

break on entry to ;

break on exit from SQUARE

By event
break when new word is created

break when STATE changes

break when dictionary grows past threshold

break on unknown token

break on stack underflow

break on return stack misuse

By address / cell
break when cell 0x2C18 changes

break when HERE changes

break on write to variable COUNT

UI mockup
Breakpoints
[x] on word entry: INTERPRET
[ ] on word exit:  QUIT
[x] on STATE change
[x] on stack underflow
[ ] on address write: 0x2C18
10. Concrete first-version mock UI
Here is a more detailed textual wireframe.

+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Run  Pause  Step Word  Step Prim  Step Out  Reset  Follow IP  Show Cells  Show Bytes  Dark Mode |
+-------------------------------+------------------------------+-----------------------------------+
| EXECUTION                     | STACKS                       | SELECTED WORD                     |
|-------------------------------|------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Current word: INTERPRET       | Data Stack (depth 3)         | Name: SQUARE                      |
| Kind: colon                   | [0] 0x0005  5                | Kind: colon                       |
| xt: 0x1840                    | [1] 0x0005  5                | Header: 0x2C10                    |
| IP: +5                        | [2] 0x2C10  xt SQUARE <-TOS  | Link -> DUP                       |
|                               |                              | Flags: none                       |
| Caller chain:                 | Return Stack (depth 2)       | Code field: DOCOL                 |
| QUIT                          | [0] return QUIT + 2          | Body:                             |
| INTERPRET                     | [1] return INTERPRET + 6     |   +0 DUP                          |
| FIND                          |                              |   +1 *                            |
|                               |                              |   +2 EXIT                         |
| Token: "SQUARE"               |                              |                                   |
| STATE: 0                      |                              |                                   |
| BASE: 10                      |                              |                                   |
| HERE: 0x2C80                  |                              |                                   |
+-------------------------------+------------------------------+-----------------------------------+
| DICTIONARY                                                                                       |
| Addr    Name        Kind     Flags      Link    xt/CFA    Size                                   |
| 2BF0    DUP         prim     -          2BD0    0810      1                                      |
| 2C10    SQUARE      colon    -          2BF0    DOCOL     3                                      |
| 2C30    TEN         const    -          2C10    DOCON     1                                      |
+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| COMPILE / EXECUTION LOG                                                                          |
| [interpret] token "5" -> push 5                                                                  |
| [interpret] token "SQUARE" -> execute                                                            |
| [execute] enter SQUARE                                                                           |
| [execute] DUP                                                                                    |
| [execute] *                                                                                      |
| [execute] EXIT                                                                                   |
+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| REGIONS                                                                                          |
| Kernel | Primitives | Dictionary>>>>>>>>>>> | Free Space | <<Return Stack | <<Data Stack         |
|         0x0800      0x1000...0x2C80         0x2C81...67FF  6800...73FF       7400...7FFF         |
| CPU: PC=0x0810 SP=0x7F20 RP=0x6FF8 | Forth: IP=0x2C1C W=0x1100 | warnings: none                 |
+--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
11. What to emphasize visually in memory
You asked specifically how to show different memory regions, usage, and conventions. For Forth, these are the most important conventions to make visible.

A. Dictionary grows one way, stacks grow the other
This is one of the classic Forth realities.

Show this graphically, not just numerically.

Bad:

HERE=0x2C80

SP=0x7400

Good:

a bar with arrows:

Dictionary >>>

<<< Return Stack

<<< Data Stack

That makes collision risk intuitive.

B. Header fields should be named explicitly
Do not make the user mentally decode raw bytes.

For each word, show:

header address

link

name/flags

code field

body start

Even if your internal layout differs from a classic fig-Forth style layout, present a normalized conceptual display.

C. Distinguish code, threaded code, and data
A single memory renderer is not enough.

You want distinct renderers for:

native primitive

colon body

constant body

variable body

CREATE/DOES> body

Each one should have different icons/colors.

For example:

primitive = chip icon

colon = list icon

constant = number icon

variable = box icon

created object = cube icon

D. Show symbolic targets for cells
Any cell that points into a known object should get a symbolic label.

Instead of just:

0x1100
show:

0x1100   xt DUP
Instead of:

0x2C1C
show:

body of SQUARE + 1 cell
This makes the debugger feel Forth-aware rather than merely hex-aware.

12. Minimal viable implementation order
To keep this practical, I would build the UI in this order.

Phase 1: core live debugging
current word

data stack

return stack

dictionary list

selected-word inspector

region map with growth directions

Phase 2: semantic decoding
classify words by kind

decode colon bodies into word lists

symbolic return-stack decoding

execution trace

Phase 3: power features
breakpoints on word entry/exit

compile-event log

watchpoints on memory/HERE/STATE

source/token view

linked raw byte/cell panes

Phase 4: teaching features
stack-effect overlays

call graph

word redefinition visualization

vocabulary/search order pane

animation of compilation into dictionary memory

13. Recommended data model behind the UI
Since you are doing Yew/Rust/WASM, it helps to structure the debugger state semantically.

Core runtime snapshot
struct ForthSnapshot {
    cpu: CpuState,
    vm: VmState,
    regions: Vec<MemoryRegion>,
    data_stack: Vec<CellValue>,
    return_stack: Vec<ReturnEntry>,
    dictionary: Vec<WordInfo>,
    current_word: Option<WordRef>,
    trace: Vec<TraceEvent>,
    compile_log: Vec<CompileEvent>,
}
Word metadata
struct WordInfo {
    name: String,
    header_addr: u32,
    link_addr: Option<u32>,
    flags: WordFlags,
    kind: WordKind,
    cfa_addr: u32,
    body_addr: Option<u32>,
    body_cells: Vec<u32>,
}
Word kinds
enum WordKind {
    Primitive,
    Colon,
    Constant,
    Variable,
    CreateDoes,
    Deferred,
    Unknown,
}
Return stack entries
enum ReturnEntry {
    ReturnToWord { word: String, offset_cells: usize },
    LoopFrame { info: String },
    UserTemp { raw: u32 },
    Unknown { raw: u32 },
}
That semantic layer is what enables a nice debugger.

14. Best single design principle
If I had to reduce it to one rule:

Show Forth concepts first, raw bytes second
So the user should first see:

SQUARE = colon word

body = DUP * EXIT

return stack entry = return to INTERPRET + 6

Then, with one click, they can drill into the bytes and addresses.

That gives you both:

a teaching/debugging tool

a memory validation tool

15. My recommendation for your first mock implementation
For version 1, I would build exactly these visible panels:

Always visible
current word / caller chain

data stack

return stack

selected word inspector

dictionary table

region usage bar

CPU + VM register strip

Hidden behind tabs
raw memory

native disassembly

compile log

breakpoints

That will already feel like a real Forth debugger, and it will map well to a small emulator and a web UI.

If you want, next I can turn this into either:

a more concrete Yew component breakdown, or

a sample JSON/debug protocol between emulator and web UI.

content.content:
1→# AgentRail
     2→
     3→A Rust workflow CLI for keeping AI agents on track across fresh contexts.
     4→
     5→AgentRail solves a specific engineering pain point: **AI coding agents lose operational knowledge between contexts.** The agent knows the goal but forgets procedural details -- which API to call, which flags to pass, which files to reference, and how to validate the output. AgentRail provides structured handoffs, deterministic step execution, and in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) so agents succeed on the first attempt instead of improvising and failing 25% of the time.
     6→
     7→## The Problem
     8→
     9→When working with AI coding agents on multi-step projects:
    10→
    11→- **Context truncation** causes agents to lose procedural details mid-session
    12→- **Tool ambiguity** leads agents to pick the wrong approach for known tasks
    13→- **Execution drift** means agents creatively rewrite procedures that should be deterministic
    14→- **Session boundaries** force you to re-explain everything when a new context starts
    15→
    16→The result: you intervene manually on roughly 1 in 4 steps, re-explaining what the agent already figured out in a previous session.
    17→
    18→## How AgentRail Fixes This
    19→
    20→AgentRail maintains a **saga** -- a persistent, append-only record of a multi-step workflow. Each saga tracks steps with typed roles, enabling an orchestration loop:
    21→
    22→1. **Meta steps** prepare structured handoff packets with objectives, procedures, success patterns, and failure modes
    23→2. **Production steps** execute semantic work guided by those packets
    24→3. **Deterministic steps** run fully-specified jobs (TTS, ffmpeg, video concat) without agent involvement
    25→4. **Validation steps** check outputs against acceptance contracts
    26→
    27→Every step records its trajectory (state, action, result, reward). On the next run of a similar task, AgentRail retrieves successful trajectories and injects them into the agent's context -- **in-context reinforcement learning**. The agent learns from its own past successes without any weight updates.
    28→
    29→## Current Status
    30→
    31→**Phases 0-5 implemented.** The full ICRL loop is functional:
    32→
    33→- 13 CLI commands: `setup`, `init`, `add`, `status`, `next`, `begin`, `complete`, `plan`, `history`, `distill`, `run-loop`, `abort`, `help`
    34→- Maintenance mode: `agentrail add` for ad-hoc tasks, agents create steps from user messages
    35→- XSkill dual memory: skills (strategic workflow docs) + trajectories (per-run records)
    36→- ICRL injection: `next` shows skill procedures, failure modes, and past successful trajectories
    37→- Trajectory recording via `complete --reward --actions --failure-mode`
    38→- Skill distillation: `distill` generates skills from accumulated trajectories
    39→- Domain repos: register external skill/executor/validator repos
    40→- Shell executors and validators for deterministic step auto-execution
    41→- Orchestrator loop: `run-loop` auto-executes deterministic steps, pauses at agent steps
    42→- 41 integration tests, all passing
    43→
    44→## Quick Start
    45→
    46→```bash
    47→# Build and install
    48→cargo build --release
    49→sw-install -p .
    50→
    51→# Bootstrap a project (creates saga + CLAUDE.md + domain registration)
    52→cd ~/my-project
    53→agentrail setup --name my-project \
    54→  --plan "Build a web app with user auth" \
    55→  --domain /path/to/agentrail-domain-coding
    56→
    57→# Create the first step
    58→agentrail complete --summary "Initialized" \
    59→  --next-slug scaffold \
    60→  --next-prompt "Set up project structure" \
    61→  --next-role production \
    62→  --planned "implement: Build core logic" \
    63→  --planned "test: Write tests"
    64→
    65→# Start Claude Code
    66→claude "go"
    67→```
    68→
    69→Or use the interactive wizard:
    70→
    71→```bash
    72→bash /path/to/agentrail-rs/scripts/agentrail-wizard.sh
    73→```
    74→
