    75→For maintenance mode (ongoing bug fixes, ad-hoc tasks):
    76→
    77→```bash
    78→# Just tell Claude what to do -- no pre-planning needed
    79→claude "fix the login timeout bug in auth.rs"
    80→
    81→# Or pre-load tasks and process them
    82→agentrail add --slug fix-123 --prompt "Fix login timeout"
    83→agentrail add --slug fix-124 --prompt "Fix mobile layout"
    84→claude "go"   # does fix-123, stops
    85→claude "go"   # does fix-124, stops
    86→```
    87→
    88→See [Getting Started](docs/getting-started.md) for the full walkthrough,
    89→and [Maintenance Mode](docs/maintenance-mode.md) for ongoing task workflows.
    90→
    91→## Agent Workflow
    92→
    93→The intended workflow for an AI agent starting a fresh context:
    94→
    95→1. Run `agentrail next` -- outputs the plan, step list, current step prompt, context files, and instructions
    96→2. Read the prompt and any context files listed
    97→3. Run `agentrail begin` to mark the step as in-progress
    98→4. Do the work
    99→5. Run `agentrail complete --summary "..." --next-slug ... --next-prompt ...` to record what was done and set up the next step
   100→
   101→This ensures every fresh context starts with full awareness of what happened, what's next, and how to do it.
   102→
   103→## Architecture
   104→
   105→Cargo workspace with five crates:
   106→
   107→```
   108→crates/
   109→  agentrail-core/      Domain types: saga, step, trajectory, handoff packet, job spec, errors
   110→  agentrail-store/     File-based persistence against .agentrail/ directory
   111→  agentrail-cli/       CLI binary (agentrail) with clap derive
   112→  agentrail-exec/      Deterministic step executors (stub)
   113→  agentrail-validate/  Output validators and acceptance contracts (stub)
   114→```
   115→
   116→Dependency flow: `cli -> store, exec, validate -> core`
   117→
   118→All runtime data lives in `.agentrail/` (append-only):
   119→
   120→```
   121→.agentrail/
   122→  saga.toml                         Saga metadata
   123→  plan.md                           Evolving plan
   124→  steps/NNN-slug/
   125→    step.toml                       Step config (role, status, job spec, packet file)
   126→    prompt.md                       Instructions for this step
   127→    summary.md                      What was accomplished
   128→  trajectories/{task_type}/
   129→    run_NNN.json                    Individual ICRL trajectory records
   130→  sessions/{session-id}.jsonl       Claude Code conversation snapshots
   131→```
   132→
   133→## Key Concepts
   134→
   135→**Step Roles** define what kind of work a step involves:
   136→
   137→| Role | Description |
   138→|------|-------------|
   139→| Meta | Prepares ICRL packets and handoffs for the next production step |
   140→| Production | Executes semantic work using a prepared packet |
   141→| Deterministic | Fully specified, no agent needed (TTS, ffmpeg, etc.) |
   142→| Validation | Checks outputs against acceptance contracts |
   143→| Legacy | Untyped step (backward compatibility) |
   144→
   145→**ICRL Trajectories** are state/action/reward records stored per task type. When a task type recurs, successful trajectories are retrieved and injected into the agent's context so it can replicate what worked before.
   146→
   147→**Handoff Packets** are structured briefings from a meta agent to a production agent containing: objective, inputs, success patterns, common failures, procedure, and output contract.
   148→
   149→**Step Transitions** enforce a valid lifecycle: Pending -> InProgress -> Completed|Blocked.
   150→
   151→## Roadmap
   152→
   153→| Phase | Description |
   154→|-------|-------------|
   155→| 0 | Walking skeleton: working CLI with tests (done) |
   156→| 1 | Deterministic step execution (GenerateTts, ffmpeg, probe, concat) |
   157→| 2 | Meta handoff packet generation and rendering |
   158→| 3 | Trajectory retrieval and ICRL injection into prompts |
   159→| 4 | Hybrid orchestrator loop with auto-advance and escalation |
   160→
   161→## Lineage
   162→
   163→Evolved from [avoid-compaction](https://github.com/softwarewrighter/avoid-compaction), a saga-based context checkpoint tool. Core patterns (saga/step model, session snapshots, TOML persistence) carried forward; extended with ICRL trajectory storage, step role typing, deterministic job specs, and handoff packets.
   164→
   165→## Development
   166→
   167→```bash
   168→cargo test --workspace                    # run all tests
   169→cargo test -p agentrail-store             # run tests for one crate
   170→cargo test -p agentrail-store saga        # run tests matching a pattern
   171→cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings   # lint
   172→cargo fmt --check                         # format check
   173→```
   174→
   175→Pre-commit gate (all must pass):
   176→
   177→```bash
   178→cargo test && cargo clippy -- -D warnings && cargo fmt --check
   179→```
   180→
   181→TDD workflow: write failing test first, implement minimum logic, refactor.
   182→
   183→## Links
   184→
   185→- [Blog post: From avoid-compaction to AgentRail](https://software-wrighter-lab.github.io/2026/03/15/saw-reg-rs-avoid-compaction-agentrail/)
   186→- [Discord](https://discord.com/invite/Ctzk5uHggZ)
   187→
   188→## License
   189→
   190→MIT
   191→

1→[workspace]

     4→    "crates/agentrail-core",

1→# CLAUDE.md
     2→
     3→This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
     4→
     5→## Build & Test Commands
     6→
     7→```bash
     8→cargo test --workspace                    # run all tests
     9→cargo test -p agentrail-store             # run tests for one crate
    10→cargo test -p agentrail-store saga        # run tests matching "saga" in one crate
    11→cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings   # lint (treats warnings as errors)
    12→cargo fmt --check                         # format check
    13→cargo fmt                                 # auto-format
    14→```
    15→
    16→Pre-commit gate (all must pass): `cargo test && cargo clippy -- -D warnings && cargo fmt --check`
    17→
    18→## Two-Layer Architecture
    19→
    20→See `docs/architecture.md` for full details.
    21→
    22→**Layer 1 (this repo)**: Generic inference-time learning engine. Task-agnostic
    23→orchestration, dual-memory (skills + experiences), ICRL trajectory injection,
    24→distillation. Does NOT know about specific domains (TTS, ffmpeg, etc.).
    25→
    26→**Layer 2 (separate repos)**: Domain-specific knowledge. Skill documents,
    27→curated experiences, executor implementations, validators. See
    28→`docs/domain-repos.md` for the domain repo contract.
    29→
    30→## Crate Layout
    31→
    32→Cargo workspace (`edition = "2024"`) with five crates under `crates/`:
    33→
    34→- **agentrail-core** -- Domain types and error enum. All other crates depend on this. Key types: `SagaConfig`, `StepConfig`, `StepRole`, `Trajectory`, `HandoffPacket`, `JobSpec`. Error type: `agentrail_core::error::Error` with `Result<T>` alias.
    35→- **agentrail-store** -- File-based persistence against `.agentrail/`. Modules: `saga` (init/load/save), `step` (create/transition/list with NNN-slug dirs), `trajectory` (ICRL record save/retrieve), `session` (Claude Code JSONL snapshot).
    36→- **agentrail-cli** -- Binary crate (`agentrail`). 8 commands: init, status, next, begin, complete, plan, history, abort. Has `lib.rs` exporting `commands` module for testability.
    37→- **agentrail-exec** -- Deterministic step executors (stub; will become trait + shell executor routing to domain repos).
    38→- **agentrail-validate** -- Output validators (stub; will become trait + shell validator routing to domain repos).
    39→
    40→Dependency flow: `cli -> store, exec, validate -> core`
    41→
    42→## Key Concepts
    43→
    44→- **Dual memory (XSkill pattern)**: skills (strategic workflow docs per task category) + experiences (tactical per-run records). See `docs/dual-memory.md`.
    45→- **Step roles** (Meta, Production, Deterministic, Validation): orchestration loop. Meta prepares handoffs, production does semantic work, deterministic runs without agents, validation checks outputs.
    46→- **ICRL injection**: `agentrail next` retrieves successful experiences for the step's task_type and injects them into the prompt output.
    47→- **Step transitions** enforce: Pending -> InProgress -> Completed|Blocked.
    48→- **Domain repos**: per-domain knowledge (skills, experiences, executors, validators) in separate repos. See `docs/domain-repos.md`.
    49→
    50→## Storage Layout
    51→
    52→All runtime data in `.agentrail/` (never `.avoid-compaction/`):
    53→```
    54→.agentrail/saga.toml
    55→.agentrail/plan.md
    56→.agentrail/steps/NNN-slug/{step.toml, prompt.md, summary.md}
    57→.agentrail/skills/{task_type}.toml          (planned)
    58→.agentrail/experiences/{task_type}/run_NNN.json  (planned)
    59→.agentrail/trajectories/{task_type}/run_NNN.json (existing, backward compat)
    60→.agentrail/domains.toml                     (planned)
    61→.agentrail/sessions/{session-id}.jsonl
    62→```
    63→
    64→## Key Documentation
    65→
    66→- `docs/architecture.md` -- Two-layer design, layer interaction, domain discovery
    67→- `docs/dual-memory.md` -- Skills and experiences schemas, retrieval strategy, distillation
    68→- `docs/domain-repos.md` -- Domain repo structure, executor/validator interfaces
    69→- `docs/research-foundations.md` -- Research papers and how they map to the architecture
    70→- `docs/implementation-plan.md` -- Phased roadmap (replaces continue-from-bootstrap.md phases)
    71→- `docs/continue-from-bootstrap.md` -- Original bootstrapping handoff (historical)
    72→
    73→## Development Practices
    74→
    75→- TDD: write failing test first, implement minimum logic, refactor.
    76→- Test pattern: integration tests using `tempfile::tempdir()`.
    77→- Evolved from [avoid-compaction](https://github.com/softwarewrighter/avoid-compaction).
    78→- Layer 1 must remain task-agnostic. No domain-specific imports in agentrail-rs.
    79→

1→pub mod error;
     2→
     3→use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};
     4→use std::fmt;
     5→
     6→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
     7→// Saga model (evolved from avoid-compaction)
     8→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
     9→
    10→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
    11→pub struct SagaConfig {
    12→    pub name: String,
    13→    pub status: SagaStatus,
    14→    pub current_step: u32,
    15→    pub created_at: String,
    16→    pub plan_file: String,
    17→}
    18→
    19→#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Serialize, Deserialize)]
    20→#[serde(rename_all = "lowercase")]
    21→pub enum SagaStatus {
    22→    Active,
    23→    Completed,
    24→}
    25→
    26→impl fmt::Display for SagaStatus {
    27→    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
    28→        match self {
    29→            SagaStatus::Active => write!(f, "active"),
    30→            SagaStatus::Completed => write!(f, "completed"),
    31→        }
    32→    }
    33→}
    34→
    35→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    36→// Step model (extended with role and execution mode)
    37→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    38→
    39→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
    40→pub struct StepConfig {
    41→    pub number: u32,
    42→    pub slug: String,
    43→    pub status: StepStatus,
    44→    pub description: String,
    45→    pub role: StepRole,
    46→    pub context_files: Vec<String>,
    47→    pub created_at: String,
    48→    #[serde(default)]
    49→    pub completed_at: Option<String>,
    50→    #[serde(default)]
    51→    pub transcript_file: Option<String>,
    52→    /// For deterministic steps: the job spec to execute
    53→    #[serde(default)]
    54→    pub job_spec: Option<JobSpec>,
    55→    /// For agentic steps: the handoff packet path
    56→    #[serde(default)]
    57→    pub packet_file: Option<String>,
    58→    /// Task type key for skill/experience retrieval (e.g., "tts", "ffmpeg-concat")
    59→    #[serde(default)]
    60→    pub task_type: Option<String>,
    61→}
    62→
    63→#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Serialize, Deserialize)]
    64→#[serde(rename_all = "lowercase")]
    65→pub enum StepStatus {
    66→    Pending,
    67→    #[serde(rename = "in-progress")]
    68→    InProgress,
    69→    Completed,
    70→    Blocked,
    71→}
    72→
    73→impl fmt::Display for StepStatus {
    74→    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
    75→        match self {
    76→            StepStatus::Pending => write!(f, "pending"),
    77→            StepStatus::InProgress => write!(f, "in-progress"),
    78→            StepStatus::Completed => write!(f, "completed"),
    79→            StepStatus::Blocked => write!(f, "blocked"),
    80→        }
    81→    }
    82→}
    83→
    84→/// What role does this step play in the orchestration loop?
    85→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Default, PartialEq, Serialize, Deserialize)]
    86→#[serde(rename_all = "lowercase")]
    87→pub enum StepRole {
    88→    /// Meta agent: prepares ICRL packet / handoff for the next production step
    89→    Meta,
    90→    /// Production agent: executes semantic work using a prepared packet
    91→    Production,
    92→    /// Deterministic: fully specified, no agent needed
    93→    Deterministic,
    94→    /// Validation: check outputs, record results
    95→    Validation,
    96→    /// Legacy: untyped step (backward compat with avoid-compaction)
    97→    #[default]
    98→    Legacy,
    99→}
   100→
   101→impl fmt::Display for StepRole {
   102→    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
   103→        match self {
   104→            StepRole::Meta => write!(f, "meta"),
   105→            StepRole::Production => write!(f, "production"),
   106→            StepRole::Deterministic => write!(f, "deterministic"),
   107→            StepRole::Validation => write!(f, "validation"),
   108→            StepRole::Legacy => write!(f, "legacy"),
   109→        }
   110→    }
   111→}
   112→
   113→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   114→// Deterministic job spec
   115→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   116→
   117→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
   118→pub struct JobSpec {
   119→    pub kind: String,
   120→    pub params: serde_json::Value,
   121→}
   122→
   123→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   124→// ICRL trajectory model
   125→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   126→
   127→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
   128→pub struct Trajectory {
   129→    pub task_type: String,
   130→    pub state: serde_json::Value,
   131→    pub action: String,
   132→    pub result: String,
   133→    pub reward: i8,
   134→    pub timestamp: String,
   135→}
   136→
   137→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   138→// Meta handoff packet
   139→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   140→
   141→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
   142→pub struct HandoffPacket {
   143→    pub task_id: String,
   144→    pub objective: String,
   145→    #[serde(default)]
   146→    pub inputs: serde_json::Value,
   147→    #[serde(default)]
   148→    pub success_patterns: Vec<String>,
   149→    #[serde(default)]
   150→    pub common_failures: Vec<String>,
   151→    #[serde(default)]
   152→    pub procedure: Vec<String>,
   153→    #[serde(default)]
   154→    pub output_contract: OutputContract,
   155→}
   156→
   157→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Default, Serialize, Deserialize)]
   158→pub struct OutputContract {
   159→    #[serde(default)]
   160→    pub required_files: Vec<String>,
   161→    #[serde(default)]
   162→    pub acceptance_checks: Vec<String>,
   163→}
   164→
   165→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   166→// Skill model (XSkill dual-memory: strategic workflow docs per task category)
   167→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   168→
   169→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
   170→pub struct Skill {
   171→    pub task_type: String,
   172→    #[serde(default = "default_version")]
   173→    pub version: u32,
   174→    #[serde(default)]
   175→    pub updated_at: String,
   176→    #[serde(default)]
   177→    pub distilled_from: u32,
   178→    #[serde(default)]
   179→    pub procedure: Procedure,
   180→    #[serde(default)]
   181→    pub success_patterns: Vec<String>,
   182→    #[serde(default)]
   183→    pub common_failures: Vec<FailureMode>,
   184→    #[serde(default)]
   185→    pub output_contract: OutputContract,
   186→}
   187→
   188→fn default_version() -> u32 {
   189→    1
   190→}
   191→
   192→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Default, Serialize, Deserialize)]
   193→pub struct Procedure {
   194→    #[serde(default)]
   195→    pub summary: String,
   196→    #[serde(default)]
   197→    pub steps: Vec<String>,
   198→}
   199→
   200→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
   201→pub struct FailureMode {
   202→    pub mode: String,
   203→    pub description: String,
   204→    #[serde(default)]
   205→    pub frequency: u32,
   206→}
   207→
   208→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   209→// Utilities (from avoid-compaction)
   210→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   211→
   212→/// Read input that could be literal text, a file path, or "-" for stdin.
   213→pub fn read_input(value: &str) -> error::Result<String> {
   214→    if value == "-" {
   215→        use std::io::Read;
   216→        let mut buf = String::new();
   217→        std::io::stdin().read_to_string(&mut buf)?;
   218→        Ok(buf)
   219→    } else if std::path::Path::new(value).is_file() {
   220→        Ok(std::fs::read_to_string(value)?)
   221→    } else {
   222→        Ok(value.to_string())
   223→    }
   224→}
   225→
   226→/// Truncate a string to `max` characters, appending "..." if truncated.
   227→pub fn truncate(s: &str, max: usize) -> String {
   228→    if s.len() <= max {
   229→        s.to_string()
   230→    } else {
   231→        format!("{}...", &s[..max.saturating_sub(3)])
   232→    }
   233→}
   234→
   235→/// Get current timestamp as YYYYMMDDTHHMMSS string.
   236→pub fn timestamp() -> String {
   237→    chrono::Local::now().format("%Y%m%dT%H%M%S").to_string()
   238→}
   239→
   240→/// Get current timestamp as ISO 8601 string.
   241→pub fn timestamp_iso() -> String {
   242→    chrono::Local::now().format("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S").to_string()
   243→}
   244→

1→use std::path::PathBuf;
     2→
     3→#[derive(Debug, thiserror::Error)]
     4→pub enum Error {
     5→    #[error("No saga found at {path}/.agentrail/")]
     6→    SagaNotFound { path: PathBuf },
     7→
     8→    #[error("Saga already exists at {path}/.agentrail/")]
     9→    SagaAlreadyExists { path: PathBuf },
    10→
    11→    #[error("Invalid step transition: {from} -> {to}")]
    12→    InvalidStepTransition { from: String, to: String },
    13→
    14→    #[error("No current step found")]
    15→    NoCurrentStep,
    16→
    17→    #[error("Saga is already complete")]
    18→    SagaComplete,
    19→
    20→    #[error("No steps defined yet")]
    21→    NoSteps,
    22→
    23→    #[error("Multiple flags cannot read from stdin")]
    24→    MultipleStdin,
    25→
    26→    #[error("Job execution failed: {0}")]
    27→    JobFailed(String),
    28→
    29→    #[error("Validation failed: {0}")]
    30→    ValidationFailed(String),
    31→
    32→    #[error("{0}")]
    33→    Other(String),
    34→
    35→    #[error(transparent)]
    36→    Io(#[from] std::io::Error),
    37→
    38→    #[error("TOML parse error: {0}")]
    39→    TomlDeserialize(#[from] toml::de::Error),
    40→
    41→    #[error("TOML serialize error: {0}")]
    42→    TomlSerialize(#[from] toml::ser::Error),
    43→
    44→    #[error("JSON error: {0}")]
    45→    Json(#[from] serde_json::Error),
    46→}
    47→
    48→pub type Result<T> = std::result::Result<T, Error>;
    49→

1→# web-tml24c
     2→
     3→Web UI for [Tiny Macro Lisp](https://github.com/sw-vibe-coding/tml24c) on COR24. Browser-based Lisp REPL running on the COR24 emulator via Rust, Yew, and WebAssembly.
     4→
     5→Write Lisp expressions, select a prelude tier, and evaluate — all in the browser.
     6→
     7→**[Live Demo](https://sw-vibe-coding.github.io/web-tml24c/)**
     8→
     9→![web-tml24c screenshot](images/screenshot.png?ts=1774674005000)
    10→
    11→## Features
    12→
    13→- **5 prelude tiers**: Bare, Minimal, Standard, Full, Scheme
    14→- **17 embedded demos**: TCO, macros, lazy sequences, error handling, anaphoric macros, bottles of beer, LED blink, and more — grouped by prelude in the dropdown
    15→- **Dual view modes**: CLI (Enter to eval, interleaved output) and Split (semi-transparent overlay for multi-line code)
    16→- **Configurable stack**: 3 KB (hardware default) or 8 KB (full EBR)
    17→- **Floating hardware panel**: LED D2, Switch S2 (clickable), with live memory gauges for heap, symbols, string pool, and stack (green/yellow/red)
    18→- **Pause/Resume/Reset/Clear** controls
    19→- **COR24 emulator**: Full 24-bit RISC CPU running in WebAssembly with instant UART TX
    20→
    21→## Related
    22→
    23→- [tml24c](https://github.com/sw-vibe-coding/tml24c) — The Lisp implementation (C)
    24→- [tc24r](https://github.com/sw-vibe-coding/tc24r) — COR24 C compiler (Rust)
    25→- [cor24-rs](https://github.com/sw-embed/cor24-rs) — COR24 assembler and emulator (Rust)
    26→- [web-tc24r](https://github.com/sw-vibe-coding/web-tc24r) — Web UI for tc24r compiler
    27→
    28→## Development
    29→
    30→```bash
    31→./scripts/serve.sh          # dev server with hot reload on port 9135
    32→./scripts/build-all.sh      # recompile tml24c + build pages/ for GitHub Pages
    33→```
    34→
    35→## License
    36→
    37→MIT
    38→

1→[package]
     2→name = "web-tml24c"
     3→version = "0.1.0"
     4→edition = "2024"
     5→authors = ["Michael A Wright"]
     6→license = "MIT"
     7→description = "Browser-based Tiny Macro Lisp REPL on COR24"
     8→
     9→[lib]
    10→crate-type = ["cdylib", "rlib"]
    11→
    12→[dependencies]
    13→cor24-emulator = { path = "../../sw-embed/cor24-rs", default-features = false }
    14→cor24-isa = { path = "../../sw-embed/cor24-rs/isa", features = ["serde"] }
    15→yew = { version = "0.21", features = ["csr"] }
    16→wasm-bindgen = "0.2"
    17→web-sys = { version = "0.3", features = ["console", "HtmlTextAreaElement", "HtmlInputElement", "HtmlSelectElement", "HtmlElement", "KeyboardEvent"] }
    18→gloo = "0.11"
    19→console_error_panic_hook = "0.1"
    20→
    21→[profile.release]
    22→opt-level = "z"
    23→lto = true
    24→

1→use clap::{Parser, Subcommand};
     2→use std::path::PathBuf;
     3→use std::process::ExitCode;
     4→use std::sync::LazyLock;
     5→
     6→use agentrail_cli::commands;
     7→
     8→static VERSION: LazyLock<String> = LazyLock::new(|| {
     9→    let version = env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION");
    10→    let commit = env!("BUILD_COMMIT");
    11→    let commit_full = env!("BUILD_COMMIT_FULL");
    12→    let timestamp = env!("BUILD_TIMESTAMP");
    13→    let host = env!("BUILD_HOST");
    14→    format!(
    15→        "{version}\n\
    16→         Copyright (c) 2026 Michael A Wright\n\
    17→         License: MIT\n\
    18→         Repository: https://github.com/sw-vibe-coding/agentrail-rs\n\
    19→         \n\
    20→         Build Information:\n\
    21→         {}\
    22→         {}\
    23→         {}",
    24→        if !host.is_empty() {
    25→            format!("  Host: {host}\n")
    26→        } else {
    27→            String::new()
    28→        },
    29→        if !commit.is_empty() {
    30→            format!("  Commit: {commit} ({commit_full})\n")
    31→        } else {
    32→            String::new()
    33→        },
    34→        if !timestamp.is_empty() {
    35→            format!("  Timestamp: {timestamp}\n")
    36→        } else {
    37→            String::new()
    38→        },
    39→    )
    40→});
    41→
    42→#[derive(Parser)]
    43→#[command(name = "agentrail", version = &**VERSION)]
    44→#[command(about = "Workflow CLI for keeping AI agents on track")]
    45→struct Cli {
    46→    /// Path to the project directory (default: current directory)
    47→    #[arg(long, default_value = ".")]
    48→    saga: PathBuf,
    49→
    50→    #[command(subcommand)]
    51→    command: Commands,
    52→}
    53→
    54→#[derive(Subcommand)]
    55→enum Commands {
    56→    /// Initialize a new saga
    57→    Init {
    58→        /// Name for the saga
    59→        #[arg(long)]
    60→        name: String,
    61→        /// Plan: file path, literal text, or "-" for stdin
    62→        #[arg(long)]
    63→        plan: String,
    64→    },
    65→    /// Bootstrap a project: create saga, CLAUDE.md, and register domain
    66→    Setup {
    67→        /// Name for the saga
    68→        #[arg(long)]
    69→        name: String,
    70→        /// Plan: file path, literal text, or "-" for stdin
    71→        #[arg(long)]
    72→        plan: String,
    73→        /// Path to a domain repo to register
    74→        #[arg(long)]
    75→        domain: Option<String>,
    76→    },
    77→    /// Add a step to the saga (for maintenance mode or ad-hoc tasks)
    78→    Add {
    79→        /// Step slug (short name)
    80→        #[arg(long)]
    81→        slug: String,
    82→        /// Step prompt: text, file path, or "-" for stdin
    83→        #[arg(long)]
    84→        prompt: String,
    85→        /// Step role (production, deterministic, validation, meta)
    86→        #[arg(long, default_value = "production")]
    87→        role: String,
    88→        /// Task type for skill/trajectory lookup
    89→        #[arg(long)]
    90→        task_type: Option<String>,
    91→    },
    92→    /// Show current saga state
    93→    Status,
    94→    /// Output current step prompt and context for a fresh agent session
    95→    Next,
    96→    /// Mark the current step as in-progress
    97→    Begin,
    98→    /// Complete current step, optionally define next step
    99→    Complete {
   100→        /// Summary: text, file path, or "-" for stdin
   101→        #[arg(long)]
   102→        summary: Option<String>,
   103→        /// Slug for the next step
   104→        #[arg(long)]
   105→        next_slug: Option<String>,
   106→        /// Prompt for the next step: text, file path, or "-" for stdin
   107→        #[arg(long)]
   108→        next_prompt: Option<String>,
   109→        /// Context file paths for next step
   110→        #[arg(long, value_delimiter = ',')]
   111→        next_context: Vec<String>,
   112→        /// Role for the next step (meta, production, deterministic, validation)
   113→        #[arg(long, default_value = "legacy")]
   114→        next_role: String,
   115→        /// Task type for the next step (e.g., "tts", "ffmpeg-concat")
   116→        #[arg(long)]
   117→        next_task_type: Option<String>,
   118→        /// Planned future steps, each "slug: description"
   119→        #[arg(long)]
   120→        planned: Vec<String>,
   121→        /// Mark the saga as complete
   122→        #[arg(long)]
   123→        done: bool,
   124→        /// Reward for trajectory recording (-1, 0, or 1; default: 1)
   125→        #[arg(long)]
   126→        reward: Option<i8>,
   127→        /// Actions taken (for trajectory recording; defaults to summary)
   128→        #[arg(long)]
   129→        actions: Option<String>,
   130→        /// Failure mode identifier (for trajectory on failure)
   131→        #[arg(long)]
   132→        failure_mode: Option<String>,
   133→    },
   134→    /// View or update the saga plan
   135→    Plan {
   136→        /// Update plan: file path, literal text, or "-" for stdin
   137→        #[arg(long)]
   138→        update: Option<String>,
   139→    },
   140→    /// Show all step summaries
   141→    History,
   142→    /// Distill trajectories into a skill document
   143→    Distill {
   144→        /// Task type to distill (e.g., "tts", "ffmpeg-concat")
   145→        task_type: String,
   146→    },
   147→    /// Auto-execute deterministic steps, pause at agent steps
   148→    RunLoop,
   149→    /// Mark current step as blocked
   150→    Abort {
   151→        /// Reason for blocking
   152→        #[arg(long)]
   153→        reason: Option<String>,
   154→    },
   155→}
   156→
   157→fn main() -> ExitCode {
   158→    let cli = Cli::parse();
   159→    match dispatch(&cli.saga, cli.command) {
   160→        Ok(code) => ExitCode::from(code),
   161→        Err(e) => {
   162→            eprintln!("Error: {e}");
   163→            ExitCode::from(1)
   164→        }
   165→    }
   166→}
   167→
   168→fn dispatch(saga_path: &std::path::Path, command: Commands) -> agentrail_core::error::Result<u8> {
   169→    match command {
   170→        Commands::Init { name, plan } => commands::init::run(saga_path, &name, &plan).map(|_| 0),
   171→        Commands::Setup { name, plan, domain } => {
   172→            commands::setup::run(saga_path, &name, &plan, domain.as_deref()).map(|_| 0)
   173→        }
   174→        Commands::Add {
   175→            slug,
   176→            prompt,
   177→            role,
   178→            task_type,
   179→        } => commands::add::run(saga_path, &slug, &prompt, &role, task_type.as_deref()).map(|_| 0),
   180→        Commands::Status => commands::status::run(saga_path).map(|_| 0),
   181→        Commands::Next => commands::next::run(saga_path),
   182→        Commands::Begin => commands::begin::run(saga_path).map(|_| 0),
   183→        Commands::Complete {
   184→            summary,
   185→            next_slug,
   186→            next_prompt,
   187→            next_context,
   188→            next_role,
   189→            next_task_type,
   190→            planned,
   191→            done,
   192→            reward,
   193→            actions,
   194→            failure_mode,
   195→        } => {
   196→            let args = commands::complete::CompleteArgs {
   197→                summary: summary.as_deref(),
   198→                next_slug: next_slug.as_deref(),
   199→                next_prompt: next_prompt.as_deref(),
   200→                next_context,
   201→                next_role: &next_role,
   202→                next_task_type: next_task_type.as_deref(),
   203→                planned,
   204→                done,
   205→                reward,
   206→                actions: actions.as_deref(),
   207→                failure_mode: failure_mode.as_deref(),
   208→            };
   209→            commands::complete::run(saga_path, &args).map(|_| 0)
   210→        }
   211→        Commands::Distill { task_type } => commands::distill::run(saga_path, &task_type).map(|_| 0),
   212→        Commands::RunLoop => commands::run_loop::run(saga_path),
   213→        Commands::Plan { update } => commands::plan::run(saga_path, update.as_deref()).map(|_| 0),
   214→        Commands::History => commands::history::run(saga_path).map(|_| 0),
   215→        Commands::Abort { reason } => commands::abort::run(saga_path, reason.as_deref()).map(|_| 0),
   216→    }
   217→}
   218→

1→# Architecture: Two-Layer Design
     2→
     3→AgentRail separates concerns into two independent layers with a clean
     4→interface between them. This document describes the architecture, the
     5→reasoning behind it, and how the layers interact.
     6→
     7→## Layer 1: Generic Inference-Time Learning Engine
     8→
     9→**Lives in**: `agentrail-rs` (this repo)
    10→
    11→Layer 1 is the task-agnostic orchestration and memory layer. It manages
    12→workflow state, dual-memory storage, trajectory retrieval, and prompt
    13→injection. It does not know about any specific domain (TTS, video, Rust
    14→builds, web deploys, etc.).
    15→
    16→### Responsibilities
    17→
    18→- **Workflow state machine**: sagas, steps, transitions, session snapshots
    19→- **Dual memory (XSkill pattern)**: skills (strategic workflow docs per task
    20→  category) and experiences (tactical per-run records with rewards)
    21→- **ICRL injection**: retrieve relevant skills and experiences, format them
    22→  into agent prompts via `agentrail next`
    23→- **Distillation**: analyze batches of experiences, update/generate skill
    24→  documents (`agentrail distill`)
    25→- **Abstract execution**: route job specs to domain executors by `kind`,
    26→  without importing domain-specific code
    27→- **Abstract validation**: evaluate outputs against named validator contracts,
    28→  without knowing what the validators check internally
    29→- **Domain registry**: discover and load domain repos from configuration
    30→
    31→### What Layer 1 knows about
    32→
    33→- Sagas, steps, step roles, step transitions
    34→- Skills (structured workflow documents per task_type)
    35→- Experiences (state/action/result/reward + trigger conditions + failure modes)
    36→- Executor trait: `fn execute(job: &JobSpec) -> Result<ExecutionResult>`
    37→- Validator trait: `fn validate(contract: &str, context: &Value) -> Result<ValidationResult>`
    38→- Task types as opaque strings (e.g., "tts", "ffmpeg-concat") -- meaningful
    39→  only to domain repos
    40→
    41→### What Layer 1 does NOT know about
    42→
    43→- How TTS works, which API to call, what ffmpeg flags to use
    44→- Domain-specific validation logic (duration checks, file format checks)
    45→- Any concrete tool, service, or external system
    46→
    47→## Layer 2: Domain-Specific Knowledge Repos
    48→
    49→**Lives in**: separate per-domain GitHub repos (e.g., `agentrail-domain-media`)
    50→
    51→Layer 2 contains all domain-specific knowledge: skill documents, curated
    52→experience libraries, executor implementations, and validator implementations.
    53→Each domain is an independent, versionable, shareable repo.
    54→
    55→### Responsibilities
    56→
    57→- **Skill documents**: authored and distilled procedure docs for each task type
    58→  in the domain (e.g., "how to generate TTS audio", "how to concatenate videos")
    59→- **Experience libraries**: curated successful trajectories and known failure
    60→  modes, stored in the standard experience format
    61→- **Executor implementations**: concrete code that runs deterministic steps
    62→  (shell scripts, Rust binaries, API calls)
    63→- **Validator implementations**: concrete checks (file exists, duration > 0,
    64→  format is valid, output matches contract)
    65→- **Task-type knowledge graphs** (optional): directed graphs of expected tool
    66→  chains for structured reward signals
    67→
    68→### Standard domain repo structure
    69→
    70→```
    71→agentrail-domain-{name}/
    72→  domain.toml           # metadata: name, task types, executor kinds, validator kinds
    73→  skills/
    74→    {task_type}.toml     # skill document per task type
    75→  experiences/
    76→    {task_type}/
    77→      run_NNN.json       # curated experience records
    78→  executors/
    79→    {kind}.sh            # or {kind}.rs, or {kind}.py -- executor implementations
    80→  validators/
    81→    {check_name}.sh      # or .rs, .py -- validator implementations
    82→  graphs/
    83→    {task_type}.toml     # optional: expected tool chain graph
    84→```
    85→
    86→### Example domains
    87→
    88→- `agentrail-domain-media` -- TTS generation, ffmpeg operations, video
    89→  compositing, audio normalization, duration probing
    90→- `agentrail-domain-rust` -- cargo test, clippy, build patterns, release
    91→  packaging
    92→- `agentrail-domain-web` -- deploy, lighthouse audit, screenshot validation
    93→- `agentrail-domain-content` -- blog post generation, markdown validation,
    94→  image optimization
    95→
    96→## Layer Interaction
    97→
    98→```
    99→Agent prompt
   100→    |
   101→    v
   102→+---------------------------+
   103→| Layer 1: agentrail-rs     |
   104→|                           |
   105→| agentrail next            |
   106→|   1. Load step config     |
   107→|   2. Check task_type      |
   108→|   3. Retrieve skill doc   |  <-- from domain repo or local store
   109→|   4. Retrieve top-N       |  <-- experiences matching task_type
   110→|      successful experiences
   111→|   5. Format into prompt   |
   112→|   6. Output to agent      |
   113→+---------------------------+
   114→    |
   115→    | (abstract interfaces)
   116→    v
   117→+---------------------------+
   118→| Layer 2: domain repo      |
   119→|                           |
   120→| Provides:                 |
   121→|   - Skill documents       |
   122→|   - Experience records    |
   123→|   - Executor binaries     |
   124→|   - Validator checks      |
   125→+---------------------------+
   126→```
   127→
   128→### Domain discovery
   129→
   130→Layer 1 discovers domains through `.agentrail/domains.toml`:
   131→
   132→```toml
   133→[[domain]]
   134→name = "media"
   135→path = "/path/to/agentrail-domain-media"
   136→# or: repo = "https://github.com/user/agentrail-domain-media"
   137→
   138→[[domain]]
   139→name = "rust"
   140→path = "/path/to/agentrail-domain-rust"
   141→```
   142→
   143→When `agentrail next` encounters a step with `task_type = "tts"`, it:
   144→
   145→1. Looks up which domain provides the "tts" task type (from domain.toml manifests)
   146→2. Loads the skill document from that domain's `skills/tts.toml`
   147→3. Loads experiences from both the domain's curated library and the local
   148→   saga's experience store
   149→4. Injects both into the prompt output
   150→
   151→### Data flow on completion
   152→
   153→When `agentrail complete` records a step:
   154→
   155→1. The experience record is saved to the local saga store (`.agentrail/experiences/`)
   156→2. Optionally, the experience can be contributed back to the domain repo
   157→   (manual curation or `agentrail contribute` command)
   158→3. `agentrail distill <task_type>` reads all experiences and updates the skill doc
   159→
   160→## Research Foundations
   161→
   162→This architecture is informed by specific research:
   163→
   164→| Concept | Source | How it maps |
   165→|---------|--------|-------------|
   166→| Dual memory (skills + experiences) | XSkill (arXiv 2603.12056) | Layer 1 stores and retrieves both types |
   167→| In-context RL from trajectories | Decision Transformer, Reflexion, Voyager (MLF-02a) | Layer 1 injects experiences as trajectory examples |
   168→| Knowledge graphs as reward models | arXiv 2601.15160 (MLF-03a) | Layer 2 domain repos can define tool-chain graphs |
   169→| Inference-time > weight updates | Sleepy Coder experiment | Layer 1 focuses entirely on context engineering |
   170→| Accumulation/inference loop | XSkill two-phase design | `distill` = accumulation, `next` = inference |
   171→
   172→## Why Not Weight-Based Learning Here?
   173→
   174→The Sleepy Coder experiment (https://software-wrighter-lab.github.io/2026/02/12/sleepy-coder-when-fine-tuning-fails/)
   175→showed that LoRA fine-tuning on a capable base model (Qwen2.5-Coder-1.5B)
   176→could not improve beyond baseline (73.3%) and often caused catastrophic
   177→forgetting (dropping to 60%). The Share algorithm prevented forgetting but
   178→could not push past baseline.
   179→
   180→The lesson: for models that already handle a task reasonably well, the
   181→ceiling is inference-time context quality, not weight adjustment. Layers 1
   182→and 2 maximize context quality. Weight-based approaches (LoRA, shared
   183→subspaces, mHC, Engram) are deferred to separate future layers that can
   184→consume the same experience data as training signal.
   185→

1→# Dual Memory: Skills and Experiences
     2→
     3→This document describes the XSkill-inspired dual-memory system that
     4→replaces the single trajectory type in the original design.
     5→
     6→## Motivation
     7→
     8→The original agentrail-rs design had a single `Trajectory` type for all
     9→learned knowledge. XSkill research (arXiv 2603.12056) demonstrated through
    10→ablation studies that two distinct memory types are both necessary and
    11→complementary:
    12→
    13→- **Skills** capture strategic, reusable workflow knowledge
    14→- **Experiences** capture tactical, situation-specific lessons
    15→
    16→Neither alone is sufficient. Skills without experiences lack grounding in
    17→real execution. Experiences without skills lack generalized procedure.
    18→
    19→## Skills
    20→
    21→A skill is a structured workflow document for an entire task category.
    22→Skills are distilled from multiple experiences and represent the current
    23→best-known procedure for a task type.
    24→
    25→### Schema
    26→
    27→```toml
    28→# .agentrail/skills/tts.toml (or from domain repo: skills/tts.toml)
    29→
    30→task_type = "tts"
    31→version = 3
    32→updated_at = "2026-03-20T10:00:00"
    33→distilled_from = 12  # number of experiences analyzed
    34→
    35→[procedure]
    36→summary = "Generate TTS audio from a narration script using VoxCPM"
    37→steps = [
    38→    "Read the narration script from the specified path",
    39→    "Call VoxCPM Gradio API at the configured service URL",
    40→    "Pass the script text and reference voice file",
    41→    "Save the output WAV to the specified path",
    42→    "Validate: file exists and duration > 0",
    43→]
    44→
    45→[success_patterns]
    46→patterns = [
    47→    "Always use the Gradio client, not raw HTTP",
    48→    "Reference voice file must be the 17-second sample",
    49→    "Output WAV sample rate should be 24kHz",
    50→]
    51→
    52→[common_failures]
    53→failures = [
    54→    { mode = "wrong_api", description = "Calling HTTP endpoint instead of Gradio client", frequency = 4 },
    55→    { mode = "missing_reference", description = "Forgetting to pass reference voice file", frequency = 2 },
    56→    { mode = "wrong_sample_rate", description = "Not specifying 24kHz output", frequency = 1 },
    57→]
    58→
    59→[output_contract]
    60→required_files = ["output.wav"]
    61→acceptance_checks = ["file-exists", "duration-positive"]
    62→```
    63→
    64→### Lifecycle
    65→
    66→1. **Seeded manually**: a human or meta agent writes the initial skill doc
    67→2. **Refined by distillation**: `agentrail distill <task_type>` updates the
    68→   skill based on accumulated experiences
    69→3. **Versioned**: each distillation increments the version
    70→4. **Shareable**: skill docs in domain repos can be used across projects
    71→
    72→### Storage
    73→
    74→Skills live in two places:
    75→- **Local**: `.agentrail/skills/{task_type}.toml` (project-specific overrides)
    76→- **Domain repo**: `skills/{task_type}.toml` (shared baseline)
    77→
    78→Local takes precedence over domain repo. Distillation updates the local copy.
    79→
    80→## Experiences
    81→
    82→An experience is a single execution record with rich context. It extends
    83→the original `Trajectory` type with trigger conditions and failure
    84→documentation.
    85→
    86→### Schema
    87→
    88→```json
    89→{
    90→    "task_type": "tts",
    91→    "run_id": "run_007",
    92→    "timestamp": "2026-03-20T10:30:00",
    93→    "trigger": {
    94→        "step_role": "deterministic",
    95→        "inputs": {
    96→            "script_path": "work/scripts/03-1.txt",
    97→            "service_url": "http://localhost:7860"
    98→        }
    99→    },
   100→    "actions": [
   101→        { "tool": "gradio_client", "params": { "endpoint": "/tts", "script": "..." } },
   102→        { "tool": "file_write", "params": { "path": "work/audio/03-1.wav" } }
   103→    ],
   104→    "result": {
   105→        "status": "success",
   106→        "outputs": { "audio_file": "work/audio/03-1.wav", "duration_seconds": 4.2 }
   107→    },
   108→    "reward": 1,
   109→    "failure_mode": null,
   110→    "notes": null
   111→}
   112→```
   113→
   114→On failure:
   115→
   116→```json
   117→{
   118→    "task_type": "tts",
   119→    "run_id": "run_008",
   120→    "timestamp": "2026-03-20T11:00:00",
   121→    "trigger": {
   122→        "step_role": "deterministic",
   123→        "inputs": { "script_path": "work/scripts/04-1.txt" }
   124→    },
   125→    "actions": [
   126→        { "tool": "curl", "params": { "url": "http://localhost:7860/tts" } }
   127→    ],
   128→    "result": {
   129→        "status": "failure",
   130→        "error": "Connection refused"
   131→    },
   132→    "reward": -1,
   133→    "failure_mode": "wrong_api",
   134→    "notes": "Used curl instead of Gradio client; server only accepts Gradio protocol"
   135→}
   136→```
   137→
   138→### Storage
   139→
   140→Experiences live in two places:
   141→- **Local**: `.agentrail/experiences/{task_type}/run_NNN.json`
   142→- **Domain repo**: `experiences/{task_type}/run_NNN.json` (curated library)
   143→
   144→Retrieval merges both sources. Local experiences are always saved.
   145→Contributing to the domain repo is a deliberate curation step.
   146→
   147→## Retrieval Strategy
   148→
   149→When `agentrail next` runs for a step with a known `task_type`:
   150→
   151→1. **Load skill**: find the skill doc (local override > domain repo baseline)
   152→2. **Load experiences**: merge local + domain repo experiences for the task type
   153→3. **Filter**: keep only reward > 0 (successes)
   154→4. **Rank**: most recent first (recency bias captures tool/API changes)
   155→5. **Limit**: top N experiences (default: 3)
   156→6. **Format**: inject skill procedure + experience examples into prompt output
   157→
   158→### Prompt injection format
   159→
   160→```
   161→=== SKILL: tts ===
   162→Procedure:
   163→  1. Read the narration script from the specified path
   164→  2. Call VoxCPM Gradio API at the configured service URL
   165→  ...
   166→
   167→Known failure modes:
   168→  - wrong_api: Calling HTTP endpoint instead of Gradio client (seen 4 times)
   169→  ...
   170→
   171→=== RECENT SUCCESSFUL EXPERIENCES ===
   172→
   173→[run_007] 2026-03-20
   174→  Actions: gradio_client(/tts) -> file_write(work/audio/03-1.wav)
   175→  Result: success, duration=4.2s
   176→  Reward: +1
   177→
   178→[run_005] 2026-03-19
   179→  Actions: gradio_client(/tts) -> file_write(work/audio/02-1.wav)
   180→  Result: success, duration=3.8s
   181→  Reward: +1
   182→```
   183→
   184→## Distillation
   185→
   186→The `agentrail distill <task_type>` command implements XSkill's
   187→accumulation phase:
   188→
   189→1. Load all experiences for the task type (successes and failures)
   190→2. Group by success/failure
   191→3. Extract common action patterns from successes
   192→4. Extract and count failure modes from failures
   193→5. Generate or update the skill document:
   194→   - Procedure steps from successful action sequences
   195→   - Success patterns from commonalities
   196→   - Failure modes with frequencies
   197→   - Output contract from successful result patterns
   198→6. Increment skill version, update timestamp
   199→
   200→Distillation is run manually or on a schedule (e.g., after every N new
   201→experiences). It is the "accumulation phase" of the XSkill two-phase loop.
   202→
   203→## Backward Compatibility
   204→
   205→The existing `Trajectory` type and `trajectories/` storage continue to work.
   206→Migration path:
   207→
   208→- Existing trajectories are readable as minimal experiences (reward field
   209→  maps directly, other fields default to null/empty)
   210→- New experiences written to `experiences/` directory
   211→- Existing `retrieve_successes()` function continues to work against old data
   212→- Skill documents are purely additive (no existing data to migrate)
   213→

1→# Domain Repository Specification
     2→
     3→This document defines the contract that domain-specific knowledge repos
     4→must follow to be usable with agentrail-rs (Layer 1).
     5→
     6→## Purpose
     7→
     8→Domain repos contain all task-specific knowledge: how to execute tasks,
     9→how to validate outputs, what good execution looks like, and what common
    10→failures to avoid. They are independent, versionable, and shareable.
    11→
    12→Separating domain knowledge from the orchestration engine means:
    13→- agentrail-rs stays generic and task-agnostic
    14→- Domain experts maintain their own knowledge repos
    15→- Multiple projects can share the same domain repo
    16→- Domains can evolve independently of the orchestration engine
    17→
    18→## Repository Structure
    19→
    20→```
    21→agentrail-domain-{name}/
    22→  domain.toml
    23→  skills/
    24→    {task_type}.toml
    25→  experiences/
    26→    {task_type}/
    27→      run_NNN.json
    28→  executors/
    29→    {kind}.sh          (or .rs, .py, or a Cargo crate)
    30→  validators/
    31→    {check_name}.sh    (or .rs, .py)
    32→  graphs/
    33→    {task_type}.toml   (optional)
    34→  README.md
    35→```
    36→
    37→## domain.toml
    38→
    39→The manifest file that Layer 1 reads to discover what the domain provides.
    40→
    41→```toml
    42→[domain]
    43→name = "media"
    44→description = "Audio/video production: TTS, ffmpeg, compositing, probing"
    45→version = "0.1.0"
    46→
    47→[[task_types]]
    48→name = "tts"
    49→description = "Text-to-speech audio generation"
    50→executor = "tts-voxcpm"
    51→validators = ["file-exists", "duration-positive"]
    52→
    53→[[task_types]]
    54→name = "ffmpeg-concat"
    55→description = "Concatenate video/audio files with ffmpeg"
    56→executor = "ffmpeg-concat"
    57→validators = ["file-exists", "duration-matches"]
    58→
    59→[[task_types]]
    60→name = "video-composite"
    61→description = "Composite image + audio into video"
    62→executor = "ffmpeg-composite"
    63→validators = ["file-exists", "resolution-check"]
    64→
    65→[[task_types]]
    66→name = "probe"
    67→description = "Probe media file for duration and format info"
    68→executor = "ffprobe"
    69→validators = []
    70→```
    71→
    72→## Executors
    73→
    74→Executors are invoked by Layer 1 when running deterministic steps. Layer 1
    75→passes the `JobSpec` (kind + params as JSON) to the executor.
    76→
    77→### Shell executor interface
    78→
    79→```bash
    80→#!/bin/bash
    81→# executors/tts-voxcpm.sh
    82→# Receives job params as JSON on stdin
    83→# Exit 0 on success, non-zero on failure
    84→# Write result JSON to stdout
    85→
    86→set -euo pipefail
    87→PARAMS=$(cat)
    88→
    89→SCRIPT_PATH=$(echo "$PARAMS" | jq -r '.script_path')
    90→OUTPUT_WAV=$(echo "$PARAMS" | jq -r '.output_wav')
    91→SERVICE_URL=$(echo "$PARAMS" | jq -r '.service_url')
    92→REFERENCE=$(echo "$PARAMS" | jq -r '.reference_voice')
    93→
    94→# Execute the task
    95→python3 tts/client.py \
    96→    --script "$SCRIPT_PATH" \
    97→    --output "$OUTPUT_WAV" \
    98→    --service "$SERVICE_URL" \
    99→    --reference "$REFERENCE"
   100→
   101→# Output result
   102→echo "{\"status\": \"success\", \"output_file\": \"$OUTPUT_WAV\"}"
   103→```
   104→
   105→### Rust executor interface (future)
   106→
   107→For performance-critical executors, a domain repo can include a Cargo crate
   108→that implements the `Executor` trait from `agentrail-core`:
   109→
   110→```rust
   111→pub trait Executor: Send + Sync {
   112→    fn kind(&self) -> &str;
   113→    fn execute(&self, params: &serde_json::Value) -> Result<ExecutionResult>;
   114→}
   115→
   116→pub struct ExecutionResult {
   117→    pub status: ExecutionStatus,
   118→    pub outputs: serde_json::Value,
   119→    pub error: Option<String>,
   120→}
   121→```
   122→
   123→## Validators
   124→
   125→Validators check outputs against acceptance criteria. Layer 1 invokes them
   126→by name, passing context as JSON.
   127→
   128→### Shell validator interface
   129→
   130→```bash
   131→#!/bin/bash
   132→# validators/duration-positive.sh
   133→# Receives context JSON on stdin (contains file paths, expected values)
   134→# Exit 0 if valid, exit 1 if invalid
   135→# Write result JSON to stdout
   136→
   137→set -euo pipefail
   138→CONTEXT=$(cat)
   139→
   140→FILE=$(echo "$CONTEXT" | jq -r '.file')
   141→DURATION=$(ffprobe -v error -show_entries format=duration \
   142→    -of default=noprint_wrappers=1:nokey=1 "$FILE")
   143→
   144→if (( $(echo "$DURATION > 0" | bc -l) )); then
   145→    echo "{\"valid\": true, \"duration\": $DURATION}"
   146→    exit 0
   147→else
   148→    echo "{\"valid\": false, \"error\": \"duration is zero or negative\"}"
   149→    exit 1
   150→fi
   151→```
   152→
   153→## Knowledge Graphs (Optional)
   154→
   155→Domain repos can optionally define expected tool chains as directed graphs.
   156→These enable structured reward signals (MLF-03a: KG as implicit reward model).
   157→
   158→```toml
   159→# graphs/tts.toml
   160→
   161→[graph]
   162→task_type = "tts"
   163→
   164→[[graph.edges]]
   165→from = "read_script"
   166→to = "call_tts_api"
   167→required = true
   168→
   169→[[graph.edges]]
   170→from = "call_tts_api"
   171→to = "save_output"
   172→required = true
   173→
   174→[[graph.edges]]
   175→from = "save_output"
   176→to = "validate_duration"
   177→required = true
   178→
   179→[[graph.edges]]
   180→from = "validate_duration"
   181→to = "validate_format"
   182→required = false
   183→```
   184→
   185→When recording an experience, Layer 1 can compare the actual action sequence
   186→against the expected graph to compute a structured reward:
   187→- All required edges followed: reward = +1
   188→- Required edge skipped: reward = -1, failure_mode = "skipped_{edge}"
   189→- Optional edge skipped: no penalty
   190→
   191→## Discovery and Registration
   192→
   193→Layer 1 discovers domain repos through `.agentrail/domains.toml` in the
   194→project directory:
   195→
   196→```toml
   197→[[domain]]
   198→name = "media"
   199→path = "/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/agentrail-domain-media"
   200→
   201→[[domain]]
   202→name = "rust"
   203→path = "/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/agentrail-domain-rust"
   204→```
   205→
   206→On `agentrail next`, Layer 1:
   207→1. Reads `domains.toml`
   208→2. For each domain, reads `domain.toml` to discover task types
   209→3. Matches the current step's `task_type` to a domain
   210→4. Loads skill doc and experiences from that domain
   211→5. Merges with local project skills/experiences (local takes precedence)
   212→
   213→## Contributing Experiences Back
   214→
   215→Experiences recorded in the local saga can be contributed to the domain repo:
   216→
   217→```bash
   218→agentrail contribute <task_type> --to media
   219→```
   220→
   221→This copies local experiences to the domain repo's `experiences/` directory.
   222→Domain repo maintainers curate (accept/reject/edit) contributed experiences
   223→before committing them. This is a manual process by design -- experience
   224→quality matters more than quantity.
   225→
   226→## Creating a New Domain Repo
   227→
   228→```bash
   229→mkdir agentrail-domain-mytools
   230→cd agentrail-domain-mytools
   231→mkdir skills experiences executors validators graphs
   232→
   233→cat > domain.toml << 'EOF'
   234→[domain]
   235→name = "mytools"
   236→description = "Description of this domain"
   237→version = "0.1.0"
   238→
   239→[[task_types]]
   240→name = "my-task"
   241→description = "What this task does"
   242→executor = "my-executor"
   243→validators = ["my-check"]
   244→EOF
   245→```
   246→
   247→Then write skill docs, executors, and validators following the interfaces
   248→above.
   249→

1→#!/usr/bin/env bash
     2→set -euo pipefail
     3→
     4→# Build pages/ for GitHub Pages deployment.
     5→# Run this before committing pages/ changes.
     6→
     7→SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
     8→PROJECT_DIR="$(dirname "$SCRIPT_DIR")"
     9→TML_DIR="$PROJECT_DIR/../../sw-vibe-coding/tml24c"
    10→TC24R="$PROJECT_DIR/../../sw-vibe-coding/tc24r/components/cli/target/release/tc24r"
    11→
    12→# 1. Recompile all REPL variants from tml24c
    13→echo "=== Compiling REPL variants ==="
    14→for v in bare minimal standard full scheme; do
    15→  echo "  repl-$v..."
    16→  "$TC24R" "$TML_DIR/src/repl-$v.c" -I "$TML_DIR/src" -o "$PROJECT_DIR/asm/repl-$v.s"
    17→done
    18→
    19→# 2. Build WASM into dist/ (gitignored), then sync to pages/
    20→#    rsync --exclude preserves .nojekyll in pages/ across rebuilds
    21→echo "=== Building pages/ ==="
    22→cd "$PROJECT_DIR"
    23→mkdir -p pages
    24→touch pages/.nojekyll
    25→trunk build --release --public-url /web-tml24c/ -d dist
    26→rsync -a --delete --exclude='.nojekyll' dist/ pages/
    27→
    28→echo "=== Done ==="
    29→echo "Pages built in: $PROJECT_DIR/pages/"
    30→echo "To preview locally: ./scripts/serve.sh"
    31→echo "To deploy: git add pages/ && git commit && git push"
    32→

1→# Architecture: web-tml24c
     2→
     3→## Decision: Rust/Yew with cor24-rs library (not Emscripten)
     4→
     5→### Approach
     6→
     7→Use **cor24-rs** (assembler + emulator) as a Rust library dependency. The tml24c
     8→C interpreter is pre-compiled to COR24 assembly via tc24r, then assembled and
     9→executed in the browser-based COR24 emulator. Lisp I/O flows through the
    10→emulated UART.
    11→
    12→### Why not Emscripten?
    13→
    14→Compiling tml24c C code directly to WASM via Emscripten would work but defeats
    15→the purpose: users want to see Lisp running *on COR24*. The cor24-rs emulator
    16→provides instruction tracing, register visibility, and memory inspection — all
    17→essential for the Phase 3 execution trace feature.
    18→
    19→### Why not a Rust rewrite of the Lisp core?
    20→
    21→Unnecessary. The C code compiles to COR24 via tc24r already. A Rust rewrite
    22→would duplicate effort and diverge from the canonical tml24c implementation.
    23→
    24→## Pipeline
    25→
    26→```
    27→Build time:
    28→  tml24c (C) --[tc24r]--> tml24c.s (COR24 assembly)
    29→
    30→Browser runtime:
    31→  tml24c.s --[cor24-rs assembler]--> binary
    32→  binary   --[cor24-rs emulator]-->  running COR24 CPU
    33→  User Lisp input --> UART RX --> interpreter --> UART TX --> output display
    34→```
    35→
    36→## Stack
    37→
    38→- **Frontend**: Yew (Rust WASM framework), same as cor24-rs
    39→- **Build tool**: Trunk (WASM bundler)
    40→- **CPU/Assembler**: cor24-emulator crate (path dependency to cor24-rs)
    41→- **ISA types**: cor24-isa crate
    42→
    43→## Project Structure
    44→
    45→```
    46→web-tml24c/
    47→├── Cargo.toml          # Workspace root
    48→├── Trunk.toml          # WASM build config
    49→├── index.html          # Entry point
    50→├── src/
    51→│   ├── main.rs         # Yew app entry
    52→│   └── repl.rs         # REPL component
    53→├── docs/
    54→│   └── architecture.md # This file
    55→└── asm/
    56→    └── tml24c.s        # Pre-compiled Lisp interpreter (from tc24r)
    57→```
    58→
    59→## Component Design
    60→
    61→### REPL Component
    62→
    63→- Text input area for Lisp expressions
    64→- Eval button (sends input to emulated UART)
    65→- Output area (reads from emulated UART output buffer)
    66→- The COR24 emulator runs the tml24c interpreter binary
    67→- Each eval: write Lisp text to UART RX, run CPU until output appears on UART TX
    68→
    69→### Future: Execution Trace (Phase 3)
    70→
    71→The cor24-rs `TraceBuffer` provides a ring buffer of the last 200 executed
    72→instructions with before/after register state. This will power the visual
    73→execution trace showing how the Lisp evaluator steps through COR24 instructions.
    74→

1→# Maintenance Mode
     2→
     3→Maintenance mode is an open-ended saga for ongoing bug fixes, feature
     4→requests, and ad-hoc tasks. Unlike a normal saga with a fixed set of
     5→planned steps, a maintenance saga runs indefinitely. Each session
     6→processes one task, then the user decides what's next.
     7→
     8→## Setup
     9→
    10→```bash
    11→cd ~/my-project
    12→agentrail setup --name "my-project-maint" \
    13→  --plan "Ongoing maintenance: bug fixes, features, improvements"
    14→```
    15→
    16→Then replace the generated CLAUDE.md with the maintenance-mode template
    17→below, or add a maintenance section to your existing CLAUDE.md.
    18→
    19→## How It Works
    20→
    21→1. User adds tasks via `agentrail add`:
    22→   ```bash
    23→   agentrail add --slug fix-parser-bug \
    24→     --prompt "Fix the off-by-one error in parser.c line 42"
    25→   agentrail add --slug add-dark-mode \
    26→     --prompt "Add dark mode toggle to the settings page"
    27→   ```
    28→
    29→2. User starts Claude:
    30→   ```bash
    31→   claude "go"
    32→   ```
    33→
    34→3. Agent runs `agentrail next`, sees the next pending task, does it,
    35→   runs `agentrail complete`, stops.
    36→
    37→4. If no pending tasks, agent asks the user what to do.
    38→
    39→5. User adds more tasks and runs `claude "go"` again. Repeat.
    40→
    41→## Adding Tasks
    42→
    43→### From the command line (before starting Claude)
    44→
    45→```bash
    46→# Simple task
    47→agentrail add --slug fix-login \
    48→  --prompt "Fix the login timeout issue reported in issue #42"
    49→
    50→# With task type for skill injection
    51→agentrail add --slug refactor-api \
    52→  --prompt "Refactor the API module to use async/await" \
    53→  --task-type rust-clippy-fix
    54→
    55→# Multiple tasks at once
    56→agentrail add --slug fix-css --prompt "Fix mobile layout breakpoint"
    57→agentrail add --slug add-tests --prompt "Add tests for user service"
    58→agentrail add --slug update-deps --prompt "Update all dependencies"
    59→```
    60→
    61→### From inside a Claude session
    62→
    63→The agent can also add tasks during a session if it discovers more
    64→work while doing the current task:
    65→
    66→```bash
    67→agentrail add --slug fix-related-bug \
    68→  --prompt "Found related bug in auth module while fixing login"
    69→```
    70→
    71→This adds it to the backlog without disrupting the current step.
    72→
    73→## The `agentrail add` Command
    74→
    75→```
    76→agentrail add --slug <slug> --prompt <text> [--role <role>] [--task-type <type>]
    77→```
    78→
    79→- Adds a step at the end of the current step list
    80→- If the saga's current step is completed/blocked, auto-advances to
    81→  the new step
    82→- If the saga is marked complete, reopens it as active
    83→- Default role is `production`
    84→
    85→## CLAUDE.md Template for Maintenance Mode
    86→
    87→```markdown
    88→# CLAUDE.md
    89→
    90→This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when
    91→working with code in this repository.
    92→
    93→## CRITICAL: AgentRail Maintenance Protocol (MUST follow exactly)
    94→
    95→This project uses AgentRail in maintenance mode (ongoing tasks).
    96→
    97→### 1. START (do this FIRST)
    98→```bash
    99→agentrail next
   100→```
   101→
   102→**If there is a pending step**: proceed to step 2.
   103→
   104→**If there are NO pending steps**: check if the user's message
   105→describes a task. If yes, create it:
   106→```bash
   107→agentrail add --slug <slug-from-description> \
   108→  --prompt "<user's task description>"
   109→```
   110→Then proceed to step 2. If the user just said "go" with no task
   111→description, ask: "No pending tasks. What should I work on?"
   112→
   113→### 2. BEGIN
   114→```bash
   115→agentrail begin
   116→```
   117→
   118→### 3. WORK
   119→Do the task described in the step prompt. Do NOT ask permission.
   120→
   121→If you discover additional work needed while doing this task, add
   122→it to the backlog (do NOT do it now):
   123→```bash
   124→agentrail add --slug <related-task> --prompt "<description>"
   125→```
   126→
   127→### 4. COMMIT
   128→Commit code changes with git.
   129→
   130→### 5. COMPLETE
   131→```bash
   132→agentrail complete --summary "what you accomplished" --reward 1
   133→```
   134→
   135→### 6. STOP
   136→Do NOT continue working after complete. The user will start a new
   137→session when ready.
   138→```
   139→
   140→## Example Workflows
   141→
   142→### User describes task directly (simplest)
   143→
   144→```bash
   145→# User tells Claude what to do -- agent creates the step itself
   146→claude "fix the login timeout bug in auth.rs"
   147→# Agent: runs agentrail next (no pending steps)
   148→#        runs agentrail add --slug fix-login-timeout --prompt "fix the login timeout bug in auth.rs"
   149→#        runs agentrail begin
   150→#        does the work
   151→#        runs agentrail complete
   152→#        stops
   153→
   154→claude "add dark mode to the settings page"
   155→# same flow: agent creates step, does it, completes, stops
   156→```
   157→
   158→### Pre-load a batch of tasks
   159→
   160→```bash
   161→# Monday: user adds tasks from issue tracker
   162→agentrail add --slug fix-123 --prompt "Fix login timeout (issue #123)"
   163→agentrail add --slug fix-124 --prompt "Fix mobile layout (issue #124)"
   164→agentrail add --slug feat-125 --prompt "Add export CSV button (issue #125)"
   165→
   166→# Process them one at a time
   167→claude "go"   # does fix-123, stops
   168→claude "go"   # does fix-124, stops
   169→claude "go"   # does feat-125, stops
   170→```
   171→
   172→### Mixed: some pre-loaded, some ad-hoc
   173→
   174→```bash
   175→# Pre-load known tasks
   176→agentrail add --slug fix-130 --prompt "Fix email notifications"
   177→agentrail add --slug fix-131 --prompt "Fix date formatting"
   178→
   179→# Process pre-loaded
   180→claude "go"   # does fix-130
   181→claude "go"   # does fix-131
   182→
   183→# Ad-hoc: user just describes what they need
   184→claude "the CSV export is missing the header row"
   185→claude "refactor the database module to use connection pooling"
   186→```
   187→
   188→## Transitioning Between Modes
   189→
   190→### From roadmap saga to maintenance
   191→When a planned saga completes (agent runs `--done`), start a new
   192→maintenance saga:
   193→
   194→```bash
   195→agentrail init --name "my-project-maint" \
   196→  --plan "Ongoing maintenance"
   197→```
   198→
   199→### From maintenance to roadmap
   200→If a big feature needs multi-step planning, start a new saga:
   201→
   202→```bash
   203→agentrail init --name "v2-rewrite" \
   204→  --plan "Rewrite the backend. Phase 1: ..."
   205→```
   206→
   207→The `.agentrail/` directory keeps history from all sagas (trajectories
   208→and skills persist across saga reinitializations).
   209→
   210→## Tips
   211→
   212→- **Keep tasks small**: one task = one session = one commit. Break
   213→  large tasks into multiple `agentrail add` calls.
   214→- **Use task types**: even in maintenance mode, setting `--task-type`
   215→  enables skill injection and trajectory learning.
   216→- **Batch add**: add several tasks at once, then process them with
   217→  sequential `claude "go"` sessions.
   218→- **Trajectories accumulate**: every completed maintenance task adds
   219→  to the trajectory history. Run `agentrail distill` periodically
   220→  to update skills.
   221→

1→# Getting Started
     2→
     3→This guide walks you through setting up AgentRail on a project for the
     4→first time, from installation through your first agent-driven session.
     5→
     6→## Prerequisites
     7→
     8→- Rust toolchain (for building agentrail)
     9→- [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code) (or another AI coding agent)
    10→- [sw-install](https://github.com/softwarewrighter/sw-install) (optional,
    11→  for installing to PATH)
    12→
    13→## Step 1: Install AgentRail
    14→
    15→```bash
    16→# Clone the repo
    17→git clone https://github.com/sw-vibe-coding/agentrail-rs
    18→cd agentrail-rs
    19→
    20→# Build release binary
    21→cargo build --release
    22→
    23→# Install to PATH (via sw-install)
    24→sw-install -p .
    25→
    26→# Verify
    27→agentrail --version
    28→```
    29→
    30→## Step 2: Set Up Your Project
    31→
    32→### Option A: Interactive Wizard (recommended)
    33→
    34→The wizard walks you through project setup with prompts:
    35→
    36→```bash
    37→cd ~/my-project
    38→bash /path/to/agentrail-rs/scripts/agentrail-wizard.sh
    39→```
    40→
    41→It asks for:
    42→1. Project name
    43→2. Plan (what needs to be done, multi-line)
    44→3. Domain repo path (optional, for skill injection)
    45→4. First step (slug, description, role, task type)
    46→5. Planned future steps
    47→
    48→Then it runs `agentrail setup` and creates the first step automatically.
    49→
    50→### Option B: One Command
    51→
    52→```bash
    53→cd ~/my-project
    54→agentrail setup --name my-project --plan "Build a web app with user auth"
    55→```
    56→
    57→This creates:
    58→- `.agentrail/` directory with saga, steps, trajectories, sessions
    59→- `CLAUDE.md` with the AgentRail session protocol (tells Claude how
    60→  to use agentrail commands)
    61→
    62→To also register a domain repo for skill injection:
    63→
    64→```bash
    65→agentrail setup --name my-project \
    66→  --plan "Build a web app" \
    67→  --domain /path/to/agentrail-domain-coding
    68→```
    69→
    70→### Option C: Step by Step
    71→
    72→```bash
    73→cd ~/my-project
    74→
    75→# Initialize the saga
    76→agentrail init --name my-project --plan "Build a web app with user auth"
    77→
    78→# Create CLAUDE.md (copy the template from below)
    79→# Register a domain repo (optional)
    80→```
    81→
    82→## Step 3: Create the First Step
    83→
    84→After setup, create the first step that tells the agent what to do:
    85→
    86→```bash
    87→agentrail complete --summary "Project initialized" \
    88→  --next-slug scaffold \
    89→  --next-prompt "Set up the project structure: create directories, config files, and a hello-world main" \
    90→  --next-role production \
    91→  --next-task-type rust-project-init
    92→```
    93→
    94→To plan ahead (recommended):
    95→
    96→```bash
    97→agentrail complete --summary "Project initialized" \
    98→  --next-slug scaffold \
    99→  --next-prompt "Set up the project structure" \
   100→  --next-role production \
   101→  --planned "implement: Build the core logic" \
   102→  --planned "test: Write tests" \
   103→  --planned "docs: Write documentation"
   104→```
   105→
   106→Verify with:
   107→
   108→```bash
   109→agentrail next
   110→```
   111→
   112→You should see the plan, step list, current step prompt, and any
   113→matching skill docs.
   114→
   115→## Step 4: Start Claude Code
   116→
   117→```bash
   118→cd ~/my-project
   119→claude "go"
   120→```
   121→
   122→The agent reads CLAUDE.md, follows the 6-step protocol:
   123→1. `agentrail next` -- sees the plan, step, prompt, skills
   124→2. `agentrail begin` -- marks step as in-progress
   125→3. Does the work described in the prompt
   126→4. Commits code with git
   127→5. `agentrail complete` -- records trajectory, advances to next step
   128→6. Stops
   129→
   130→When the session ends, start a new one for the next step:
   131→
   132→```bash
   133→claude "go"
   134→```
   135→
   136→## Step 5: Repeat
   137→
   138→Each `claude "go"` session handles one step. The agent picks up where
   139→the previous one left off, seeing the full history and any accumulated
   140→skill knowledge.
   141→
   142→After several steps, you can distill trajectories into skill docs:
   143→
   144→```bash
   145→agentrail distill rust-project-init
   146→```
   147→
   148→## CLAUDE.md Template
   149→
   150→If you used `agentrail setup`, this was created automatically. If not,
   151→create `CLAUDE.md` in your project root with this content:
   152→
   153→```markdown
   154→# CLAUDE.md
   155→
   156→This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when
   157→working with code in this repository.
   158→
   159→## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)
   160→
   161→This project uses AgentRail. Every session follows this exact sequence:
   162→
   163→### 1. START (do this FIRST, before anything else)
   164→agentrail next
   165→
   166→### 2. BEGIN (immediately after reading the next output)
   167→agentrail begin
   168→
   169→### 3. WORK (do what the step prompt says)
   170→Do NOT ask "want me to proceed?". The step prompt IS your instruction.
   171→
   172→### 4. COMMIT (after the work is done)
   173→Commit your code changes with git.
   174→
   175→### 5. COMPLETE (LAST thing, after committing)
   176→agentrail complete --summary "what you accomplished" \
   177→  --reward 1 --actions "tools and approach used"
   178→
   179→### 6. STOP (after complete, DO NOT continue working)
   180→Do NOT make further code changes after complete.
   181→Any changes after complete are untracked.
   182→```
   183→
   184→Add project-specific sections below the protocol (build commands,
   185→related projects, available task types, etc.).
   186→
   187→## Using a Domain Repo
   188→
   189→Domain repos provide reusable skills across projects. The
   190→[agentrail-domain-coding](https://github.com/sw-vibe-coding/agentrail-domain-coding)
   191→repo has skills for Rust, C, Lisp, and web development.
   192→
   193→### Register in your project
   194→
   195→```bash
   196→# Clone the domain repo
   197→git clone https://github.com/sw-vibe-coding/agentrail-domain-coding
   198→
   199→# Register it (creates .agentrail/domains.toml)
   200→agentrail setup --name my-project --plan "..." \
   201→  --domain /path/to/agentrail-domain-coding
   202→```
   203→
   204→Or manually create `.agentrail/domains.toml`:
   205→
   206→```toml
   207→[[domain]]
   208→name = "coding"
   209→path = "/path/to/agentrail-domain-coding"
   210→```
   211→
   212→### How it works
   213→
   214→When a step has a `task_type` (e.g., `rust-project-init`), `agentrail
   215→next` looks for a matching skill doc:
   216→1. First in `.agentrail/skills/` (local, project-specific)
   217→2. Then in registered domain repos
   218→
   219→The skill procedure, success patterns, and failure modes are injected
   220→into the agent's prompt automatically.
   221→
   222→## Working on Existing Projects
   223→
   224→For an existing project that doesn't have a saga yet:
   225→
   226→```bash
   227→cd ~/existing-project
   228→
   229→# The wizard works on existing projects too
   230→bash /path/to/agentrail-rs/scripts/agentrail-wizard.sh
   231→
   232→# Or one command
   233→agentrail setup --name existing-project \
   234→  --plan "Add feature X to the existing codebase"
   235→```
   236→
   237→AgentRail adds `.agentrail/` and `CLAUDE.md` without touching your
   238→existing code.
   239→
   240→## After a Saga Completes
   241→
   242→When all steps are done:
   243→
   244→- **More multi-step work**: start a new saga with `agentrail init`
   245→- **Quick one-off fix**: just use `claude` normally without agentrail
   246→- **Add more steps**: `agentrail add --slug fix-bug --prompt "Fix X"`
   247→  (auto-reopens the saga)
   248→
   249→## Maintenance Mode
   250→
   251→For ongoing work without a fixed endpoint (bug fixes, features,
   252→improvements), use maintenance mode:
   253→
   254→```bash
   255→# Just tell Claude what to do -- no pre-planning needed
   256→claude "fix the login timeout bug in auth.rs"
   257→claude "add dark mode to the settings page"
   258→claude "update all dependencies"
   259→```
   260→
   261→The agent creates the step itself via `agentrail add`, does the work,
   262→completes, and stops. Or pre-load a batch:
   263→
   264→```bash
   265→agentrail add --slug fix-123 --prompt "Fix login timeout"
   266→agentrail add --slug fix-124 --prompt "Fix mobile layout"
   267→claude "go"   # does fix-123
   268→claude "go"   # does fix-124
   269→```
   270→
   271→See `docs/maintenance-mode.md` for the full guide and CLAUDE.md template.
   272→
   273→See `docs/user-guide.md` for details.
   274→
   275→## Example: Real Project Setup
   276→
   277→Here is how the [tml24c](https://github.com/sw-vibe-coding/tml24c)
   278→project (Tiny Macro Lisp for COR24) was bootstrapped:
   279→
   280→```bash
   281→cd ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/tml24c
   282→
   283→# Setup with coding domain
   284→agentrail setup --name tml24c \
   285→  --plan "Build a Tiny Macro Lisp that compiles to COR24 assembly" \
   286→  --domain ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/agentrail-domain-coding
   287→
   288→# First step: research and planning
   289→agentrail complete --summary "Project initialized" \
   290→  --next-slug research-and-plan \
   291→  --next-prompt "Read docs/research.txt, study COR24 toolchain, create architecture and design docs" \
   292→  --next-role production \
   293→  --planned "scaffold: Set up C source structure" \
   294→  --planned "lexer: Implement tokenizer" \
   295→  --planned "reader: Implement S-expression reader" \
   296→  --planned "eval-core: Implement eval with core special forms" \
   297→  --planned "gc: Implement mark-sweep GC" \
   298→  --planned "compiler: Compile to COR24 assembly"
   299→
   300→# Start working
   301→claude "go"
   302→```
   303→
   304→## Troubleshooting
   305→
   306→**Agent doesn't follow the protocol**: make sure CLAUDE.md is in the
   307→project root and the protocol section says CRITICAL/MUST at the top.
   308→
   309→**Agent asks "want me to proceed?"**: the CLAUDE.md should say "Do NOT
   310→ask. The step prompt IS your instruction."
   311→
   312→**Agent keeps working after complete**: the `complete` command prints
   313→a STOP message. Ensure CLAUDE.md has step 6 (STOP).
   314→
   315→**Skills not appearing**: check that the step has `--next-task-type`
   316→set and that the domain repo is registered in `.agentrail/domains.toml`.
   317→
   318→**Duplicate steps**: fixed in current version. `complete` advances to
   319→existing planned steps instead of creating duplicates.
   320→
