1→# MakerLisp COR24 — Assembly Emulator
     2→
     3→A browser-based educational emulator for the
     4→[MakerLisp](https://makerlisp.com) COR24 (C-Oriented RISC, 24-bit)
     5→architecture. Written in Rust and compiled to WebAssembly.
     6→
     7→**[Live Demo](https://sw-embed.github.io/cor24-rs/)**
     8→
     9→### Assembler Tab
    10→![COR24 Assembler Tab](images/assembler-tab.png?ts=1774129001000)
    11→
    12→### C Tab
    13→![COR24 C Pipeline Tab](images/c-tab.png?ts=1774129001000)
    14→
    15→### Rust Tab
    16→![COR24 Rust Pipeline Tab](images/rust-tab.png?ts=1774129001000)
    17→
    18→### CLI Pipeline Demos
    19→
    20→![Assembler UART Hello](docs/vhs/asm-uart-hello.gif?ts=1774129001000)
    21→
    22→![Rust Pipeline UART Hello](docs/vhs/uart-hello.gif?ts=1774129001000)
    23→
    24→## Features
    25→
    26→- **Interactive Assembly Editor** — Write and edit COR24 assembly code
    27→- **Step-by-Step Execution** — Debug your code instruction by instruction
    28→- **Multi-Region Memory Viewer** — Program, Stack, and I/O regions with change heatmaps
    29→- **CLI Debugger** (`cor24-dbg`) — GDB-like command-line debugger with breakpoints, UART, and LED/button I/O
    30→- **LGO File Loader** — Load programs assembled with the reference `as24` toolchain
    31→- **Built-in Examples** — Learn from pre-loaded example programs
    32→- **Challenges** — Test your assembly skills with programming challenges
    33→- **ISA Reference** — Instruction set documentation and memory map
    34→
    35→## COR24 Architecture
    36→
    37→COR24 is a 24-bit RISC soft CPU for Lattice MachXO FPGAs, designed for
    38→embedded systems education. 32 operations encode into 211 instruction
    39→forms (1, 2, or 4 bytes).
    40→
    41→- **3 General-Purpose Registers**: r0, r1, r2 (24-bit)
    42→- **5 Special-Purpose Registers**:
    43→  - r3 = fp (frame pointer)
    44→  - r4 = sp (stack pointer, init 0xFEEC00)
    45→  - r5 = z (always zero; usable only in compare instructions)
    46→  - r6 = iv (interrupt vector)
    47→  - r7 = ir (interrupt return address)
    48→- **Single Condition Flag**: C (set by compare instructions)
    49→- **16 MB Address Space**: 1 MB SRAM + 3 KB EBR (stack) + memory-mapped I/O
    50→- **Variable-Length Instructions**: 1, 2, or 4 bytes
    51→
    52→### Supported Instructions
    53→
    54→| Category | Instructions |
    55→|----------|-------------|
    56→| Arithmetic | `add`, `sub`, `mul` |
    57→| Logic | `and`, `or`, `xor` |
    58→| Shifts | `shl`, `sra`, `srl` |
    59→| Compare | `ceq`, `cls`, `clu` |
    60→| Branch | `bra`, `brf`, `brt` |
    61→| Jump | `jmp`, `jal` |
    62→| Load | `la`, `lc`, `lcu`, `lb`, `lbu`, `lw` |
    63→| Store | `sb`, `sw` |
    64→| Stack | `push`, `pop` |
    65→| Move | `mov`, `sxt`, `zxt` |
    66→
    67→## Examples & Demos
    68→
    69→This project has two sets of examples, matching the two tabs in the web UI:
    70→
    71→- **[Assembler Examples](docs/assembler-examples.md)** — 11 hand-written COR24 assembly programs.
    72→  Available in the web UI's **Assembler** tab (click Examples → pick one → Assemble → Run)
    73→  and via `cor24-dbg` on the command line (see `scripts/demo-cli-*.sh`).
    74→
    75→- **[Rust Pipeline Demos](docs/rust-pipeline-demos.md)** — 12 Rust programs compiled through the
    76→  Rust → MSP430 → COR24 cross-compilation pipeline.
    77→  Available in the web UI's **Rust** tab (pick example → Compile → Translate → Assemble → Run)
    78→  and via CLI scripts in `rust-to-cor24/demos/` (see `run-demo.sh`, per-demo `run.sh`, `generate-all.sh`).
    79→
    80→```bash
    81→# Assembler example via CLI debugger
    82→scripts/demo-cli-hello-world.sh
    83→
    84→# Rust pipeline demo via CLI
    85→rust-to-cor24/demos/run-demo.sh demo_add
    86→rust-to-cor24/demos/run-demo.sh demo_echo_v2 --uart-input 'hello!'
    87→```
    88→
    89→For an overview of all the binaries and how they fit together, see **[docs/eli5.md](docs/eli5.md)**.
    90→
    91→## Building
    92→
    93→### Prerequisites
    94→
    95→- [Rust](https://rustup.rs/) (1.75+)
    96→- [Trunk](https://trunkrs.dev/) (`cargo install trunk`)
    97→- wasm32-unknown-unknown target (`rustup target add wasm32-unknown-unknown`)
    98→
    99→### Development
   100→
   101→```bash
   102→# Serve locally with hot reload (port 7401)
   103→./serve.sh
   104→
   105→# Or directly:
   106→trunk serve --port 7401
   107→
   108→# Open http://localhost:7401/cor24-rs/
   109→```
   110→
   111→### Production Build
   112→
   113→```bash
   114→# Build optimized WASM to pages/
   115→trunk build --release
   116→```
   117→
   118→## Project Structure
   119→
   120→```
   121→cor24-rs/
   122→├── src/
   123→│   ├── cpu/           # CPU emulator core
   124→│   │   ├── decode_rom.rs  # Instruction decode ROM (from hardware)
   125→│   │   ├── encode.rs      # Instruction encoding tables
   126→│   │   ├── executor.rs    # Instruction execution engine
   127→│   │   ├── instruction.rs # Opcode definitions
   128→│   │   └── state.rs       # CPU state (registers, memory regions, I/O)
   129→│   ├── emulator.rs    # EmulatorCore — shared controller for CLI and Web
   130→│   ├── assembler.rs   # Two-pass assembler
   131→│   ├── loader.rs      # LGO file loader (as24 output format)
   132→│   ├── challenge.rs   # Challenge definitions
   133→│   ├── wasm.rs        # WASM bindings (WasmCpu wraps EmulatorCore)
   134→│   └── app.rs         # Yew web application
   135→├── cli/               # CLI debugger (cor24-dbg)
   136→├── components/        # Reusable Yew UI components
   137→├── tests/programs/    # Assembly test programs (.s files)
   138→├── scripts/           # Demo and build scripts
   139→├── styles/            # CSS stylesheets
   140→└── pages/             # Built WASM output (GitHub Pages)
   141→```

1→# User Guide
     2→
     3→This guide covers how to use AgentRail to manage AI agent workflows,
     4→from initial setup through dogfooding on real projects.
     5→
     6→## Installation
     7→
     8→```bash
     9→# Build from source (release mode)
    10→cargo build --release
    11→
    12→# Install via sw-install
    13→sw-install -p /path/to/agentrail-rs
    14→
    15→# Verify
    16→agentrail --version
    17→```
    18→
    19→## Core Concepts
    20→
    21→**Saga**: a persistent, append-only record of a multi-step project.
    22→Lives in `.agentrail/` inside your project directory.
    23→
    24→**Step**: a discrete unit of work within a saga. Steps have roles
    25→(Production, Deterministic, Validation, Meta, Legacy), statuses
    26→(Pending, InProgress, Completed, Blocked), and optional task types.
    27→
    28→**Skill**: a strategic workflow document for a task category. Describes
    29→procedure, success patterns, and known failure modes. Loaded from
    30→`.agentrail/skills/` or from a domain repo.
    31→
    32→**Trajectory**: a per-run record (state, action, result, reward) stored
    33→per task type. Injected into agent prompts by `agentrail next` so
    34→agents learn from past successes.
    35→
    36→**Domain repo**: a separate repository containing skills, executors,
    37→and validators for a specific domain (e.g., coding, media production).
    38→
    39→## Setting Up a New Project
    40→
    41→### 1. Initialize the saga
    42→
    43→```bash
    44→cd ~/my-project
    45→agentrail init --name my-project --plan "Build feature X. Phase 1: scaffold. Phase 2: implement. Phase 3: test."
    46→```
    47→
    48→This creates `.agentrail/` with `saga.toml`, `plan.md`, and empty
    49→`steps/`, `trajectories/`, `sessions/` directories.
    50→
    51→### 2. Register a domain (optional)
    52→
    53→If you have a domain repo with skills, create `.agentrail/domains.toml`:
    54→
    55→```toml
    56→[[domain]]
    57→name = "coding"
    58→path = "/path/to/agentrail-domain-coding"
    59→```
    60→
    61→### 3. Create the first step
    62→
    63→```bash
    64→agentrail complete --summary "Project initialized" \
    65→  --next-slug scaffold \
    66→  --next-prompt "Set up the project structure, create initial files" \
    67→  --next-role production \
    68→  --next-task-type rust-project-init
    69→```
    70→
    71→### 4. Plan ahead with --planned
    72→
    73→```bash
    74→agentrail complete --summary "Initialized" \
    75→  --next-slug scaffold \
    76→  --next-prompt "Set up project structure" \
    77→  --planned "implement: Build the core logic" \
    78→  --planned "test: Write integration tests" \
    79→  --planned "docs: Write documentation"
    80→```
    81→
    82→This creates step 1 (scaffold) plus placeholder steps 2-4. When step 1
    83→completes, `agentrail complete` automatically advances to the existing
    84→step 2 instead of creating a duplicate.
    85→
    86→### 5. Add CLAUDE.md to your project
    87→
    88→Create a `CLAUDE.md` in your project root with the AgentRail session
    89→protocol. This tells Claude Code to follow the workflow automatically.
    90→
    91→```markdown
    92→## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)
    93→
    94→### 1. START (do this FIRST, before anything else)
    95→agentrail next
    96→
    97→### 2. BEGIN (immediately after reading the next output)
    98→agentrail begin
    99→
   100→### 3. WORK (do what the step prompt says)
   101→Do NOT ask "want me to proceed?". The step prompt IS your instruction.
   102→
   103→### 4. COMMIT (after the work is done)
   104→Commit your code changes with git.
   105→
   106→### 5. COMPLETE (LAST thing, after committing)
   107→agentrail complete --summary "what you accomplished" \
   108→  --reward 1 --actions "tools and approach used"
   109→
   110→### 6. STOP (after complete, DO NOT continue working)
   111→Do NOT make further code changes after complete.
   112→Any changes after complete are untracked.
   113→```
   114→
   115→## Running a Session
   116→
   117→### Starting Claude Code
   118→
   119→```bash
   120→cd ~/my-project
   121→claude "go"
   122→```
   123→
   124→The positional argument `"go"` starts an interactive session with "go"
   125→as the initial message. Claude reads CLAUDE.md, follows the protocol,
   126→and executes the current step.
   127→
   128→### What the agent does
   129→
   130→1. Runs `agentrail next` -- sees plan, steps, prompt, skills, trajectories
   131→2. Runs `agentrail begin` -- marks step as in-progress
   132→3. Does the work described in the prompt
   133→4. Commits code with git
   134→5. Runs `agentrail complete` -- records trajectory, advances to next step
   135→6. Stops (does not continue working)
   136→
   137→### Multiple projects in parallel
   138→
   139→Open separate terminals for each project:
   140→
   141→```bash
   142→# Terminal 1
   143→cd ~/project-a && claude "go"
   144→
   145→# Terminal 2
   146→cd ~/project-b && claude "go"
   147→
   148→# Terminal 3
   149→cd ~/project-c && claude "go"
   150→```

1→# Web TML24C — Feature Plan 2→ 3→## Context 4→ 5→The tml24c CLI Lisp interpreter has grown significantly: 4 prelude tiers (bare/minimal/standard/full), 41 built-in primitives, strings, quasiquote, lazy sequences, tail-call optimization, variadic functions, anaphoric macros, 15 demo programs, configurable 3K/8K stack, and hardware I/O (LED, switch, UART). The web UI currently exposes almost none of this — it embeds one stale assembly binary with a basic REPL. This plan brings the web UI to parity with the CLI. 6→ 7→## Architecture 8→ 9→``` 10→Build time: repl-{bare,minimal,standard,full}.c --[tc24r]--> asm/repl-{bare,minimal,standard,full}.s 11→Runtime: User selects prelude tier + stack size 12→ → assemble selected .s → load into EmulatorCore → set SP → run 13→ → UART I/O between browser and emulated Lisp REPL 14→ → LED/Switch state rendered as widgets 15→ → Heap/Stack usage displayed as gauges 16→``` 17→ 18→All 4 assembly files are embedded via `include_str!`. Only the selected one is assembled at runtime. Switching prelude = hard_reset + assemble new binary + set SP + resume. 19→ 20→--- 21→ 22→## Phase 1: Multi-Binary Prelude Switching 23→ 24→### 1.1 Compile all 4 REPL variants 25→ 26→```bash 27→tc24r src/repl-bare.c -I src -o asm/repl-bare.s 28→tc24r src/repl-minimal.c -I src -o asm/repl-minimal.s 29→tc24r src/repl-standard.c -I src -o asm/repl-standard.s 30→tc24r src/repl-full.c -I src -o asm/repl-full.s 31→``` 32→ 33→Remove the stale `asm/tml24c.s` (test harness, not a REPL variant). 34→ 35→### 1.2 Configuration types (`src/config.rs`) 36→ 37→```rust 38→#[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)] 39→pub enum PreludeTier { Bare, Minimal, Standard, Full } 40→ 41→#[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)] 42→pub enum StackSize { ThreeKb, EightKb } 43→ 44→impl StackSize { 45→ pub fn initial_sp(self) -> u32 { 46→ match self { 47→ Self::ThreeKb => 0xFEEC00, // default, matches MachXO hardware 48→ Self::EightKb => 0xFF0000, // full EBR window 49→ } 50→ } 51→} 52→``` 53→ 54→### 1.3 Assembly embedding 55→ 56→```rust 57→const ASM_BARE: &str = include_str!("../asm/repl-bare.s"); 58→const ASM_MINIMAL: &str = include_str!("../asm/repl-minimal.s"); 59→const ASM_STANDARD: &str = include_str!("../asm/repl-standard.s"); 60→const ASM_FULL: &str = include_str!("../asm/repl-full.s"); 61→``` 62→ 63→### 1.4 Repl component rework 64→ 65→Add messages: `SetPrelude(PreludeTier)`, `SetStack(StackSize)`, `Reset`. 66→ 67→`load_binary(tier, stack)`: 68→1. `emulator.hard_reset()` 69→2. Assemble the selected tier's assembly source 70→3. Load bytes into emulator memory 71→4. `emulator.set_reg(4, stack.initial_sp())` — register 4 = SP 72→5. `emulator.set_pc(0)`, `emulator.resume()` 73→6. Start tick loop 74→ 75→### 1.5 Toolbar 76→ 77→Prelude dropdown (Bare / Minimal / Standard / Full), Stack dropdown (3 KB / 8 KB), Reset button. Changing prelude or stack triggers full reload. 78→ 79→Default: **Standard** prelude, **3 KB** stack. 80→ 81→--- 82→ 83→## Phase 2: Demo Programs 84→ 85→### 2.1 Demo metadata (`src/demos.rs`) 86→ 87→```rust 88→pub struct Demo { 89→ pub title: &'static str, 90→ pub description: &'static str, 91→ pub source: &'static str, // include_str from tml24c/demos/ 92→ pub prelude: PreludeTier, 93→ pub stack: StackSize, 94→ pub category: &'static str, 95→} 96→``` 97→ 98→### 2.2 Demo inventory 99→ 100→| Demo | Category | Prelude | Stack | Description | 101→|------|----------|---------|-------|-------------| 102→| tco | Language | Minimal | 8K | Tail-call optimization: countdown, sum, fib | 103→| macros | Language | Standard | 3K | let, cond, and, or macros | 104→| quasiquote | Language | Standard | 3K | Quasiquote template expansion | 105→| variadic | Language | Standard | 3K | Rest parameters `(lambda args ...)` | 106→| strings | Language | Standard | 3K | String type: length, append, ref | 107→| mutation | Language | Standard | 3K | set!, closures, memoization, atoms | 108→| multiline | Language | Standard | 3K | Multi-line balanced-paren input | 109→| threading | Language | Full | 3K | `->` and `->>` pipeline macros | 110→| lazy | Language | Full | 8K | Lazy sequences, infinite fibonacci | 111→| anaphora | Language | Full | 3K | Unhygienic macros: aif, awhen, aand | 112→| utilities | Language | Full | 3K | partial, juxt, doseq, dotimes | 113→| bottles | Application | Standard | 3K | 99 Bottles (macro + TCO, 5 verses) | 114→| bottles2 | Application | Full | 8K | 99 Bottles (trampoline, full 99) | 115→| bottles4 | Application | Standard | 3K | 99 Bottles (functional map/for-each) | 116→| blink | Hardware | Standard | 3K | LED blink via memory-mapped I/O | 117→ 118→### 2.3 Demo loading behavior 119→ 120→`Msg::LoadDemo(index)`: 121→1. Set input textarea to demo source (strip `;;` comment header) 122→2. Auto-select required prelude tier and stack size 123→3. Reload emulator if config changed 124→4. User clicks Eval to run (no auto-eval) 125→ 126→### 2.4 Multi-expression handling 127→ 128→Demos contain multiple expressions separated by newlines. All bytes are queued to UART at once. The interpreter reads and evaluates them one line at a time. Prompt detection (`output.ends_with("> ")` with empty TX queue) already handles this. 129→ 130→Demos ending with `(exit)` trigger `StopReason::Halted`. Show "Program finished." instead of generic "Halted." 131→ 132→--- 133→ 134→## Phase 3: Hardware Widgets & Memory Visualization 135→ 136→### 3.1 LED D2 widget (`src/hardware.rs`) 137→ 138→- Visual: 28px circle, dark gray when off, red radial-gradient glow when on 139→- State from: `emulator.get_led()` (bit 0) 140→- Updated after each tick batch (via `BatchResult.led_changed`) 141→- Pure display, no user interaction 142→ 143→CSS reference (cor24-rs): 144→```css 145→.led-on { background: radial-gradient(circle at 30% 30%, #ff5555, #ff0000, #cc0000); 146→ box-shadow: 0 0 15px #ff0000, 0 0 30px rgba(255,0,0,0.3); } 147→.led-off { background: #333; box-shadow: inset 0 2px 4px rgba(0,0,0,0.5); } 148→``` 149→ 150→### 3.2 Switch S2 widget 151→ 152→- Visual: 28px rounded rectangle, gray when released, cyan glow when pressed 153→- Click toggles: `emulator.set_button_pressed(!current)` 154→- State from: `emulator.get_button()` 155→- Blink demo uses this for LED-follows-button patterns 156→ 157→### 3.3 Memory gauges 158→ 159→**Heap usage**: Find `_heap_next` symbol address from `AssemblyResult.labels` HashMap. Read with `emulator.read_word(addr)`. Display: `"Heap: {used}/{HEAP_SIZE} cells"` as a bar. 160→ 161→**Stack depth**: `initial_sp - emulator.get_reg(4)` bytes. Display: `"Stack: {depth}/{total} bytes"` as a bar. 162→ 163→**SRAM usage** (optional, periodic): Count non-zero 16-byte blocks in `emulator.sram()`. Expensive — run every N ticks, not every tick. 164→ 165→### 3.4 Layout 166→ 167→``` 168→+------------------------------------------------------------------+ 169→| Tiny Macro Lisp — COR24 [Prelude▾][Stack▾][Demo▾] [⟳] 🐙| 170→+------------------------------------------------------------------+ 171→| | D2 [●] S2 [▭] | 172→| OUTPUT | | 173→| > (+ 1 2) | Heap [████░░░░] 234/4096 | 174→| 3 | Stack [██░░░░░░] 96/3072 | 175→| > | | 176→| | PRELUDE: Standard | 177→| | 41 primitives | 178→| | ~35 prelude functions | 179→| | [▸ Reference...] | 180→+-----------------------------------+------------------------------+ 181→| INPUT | 182→| ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ | 183→| │ (map (lambda (x) (* x x)) (list 1 2 3 4 5)) │ | 184→| └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ | 185→| [Eval] Ready. | 186→+------------------------------------------------------------------+ 187→| MIT License · © 2026 Michael A Wright · COR24-TB · abc1234 · ... | 188→+------------------------------------------------------------------+ 189→``` 190→ 191→--- 192→ 193→## Phase 4: Documentation Panels 194→ 195→### 4.1 Documentation content (`src/docs.rs`) 196→ 197→Static data organized by tier. Each entry: name, signature, one-line description, optional example. 198→ 199→**Built-in primitives** (41, always available): 200→- Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` 201→- Comparison: `<`, `=` 202→- List: `cons`, `car`, `cdr`, `list`, `null?`, `pair?` 203→- Type: `atom?`, `eq?`, `not`, `number?`, `string?`, `fn?` 204→- I/O: `display`, `print`, `println`, `newline`, `peek`, `poke`, `delay` 205→- String: `string-length`, `string-ref`, `string-append`, `string=?`, `number->string`, `symbol->string`, `string->symbol` 206→- Meta: `apply`, `eval`, `macroexpand`, `gensym` 207→- GC: `gc`, `heap-used`, `heap-size` 208→- Control: `exit` 209→ 210→**Special forms** (8): `quote`, `if`, `define`, `set!`, `lambda`, `defmacro`, `begin`, quasiquote/unquote/splicing 211→ 212→**Minimal prelude** (6): `>`, `>=`, `<=`, `zero?`, `positive?`, `negative?` 213→ 214→**Standard prelude** (adds ~35): `map`, `filter`, `reduce`, `foldr`, `length`, `append`, `reverse`, `nth`, `when`, `unless`, `let`, `and`, `or`, `cond`, `->`, `->>`, `abs`, `min`, `max`, `cadr`/`caddr`/`caar`/`cdar`, `identity`, `complement`, `compose`, `->str`, `str`, `for-each`, `assoc`, `get`, I/O constants (`IO-LED`, `set-leds`, etc.) 215→ 216→**Full prelude** (adds ~25): `constantly`, `every?`, `some`, `none?`, `range`, `repeat`, `take`, `drop`, `zip`, `flatten`, `partial`, `juxt`, `doseq`, `dotimes`, `trampoline`, `lazy-cons`/`lazy-car`/`lazy-cdr`/`lazy-take`/`lazy-map`/`lazy-filter`, `iterate`, `lazy-range`, `take-while`, `drop-while`, `aif`, `awhen`, `aand` 217→ 218→### 4.2 Collapsible reference panel 219→ 220→In the right sidebar, below hardware widgets. Shows functions available at the current prelude tier. Clickable examples load into the input area. Sections: Primitives, Special Forms, Prelude Functions. 221→ 222→### 4.3 Per-demo documentation 223→ 224→When a demo is loaded, show its description and what features it demonstrates. This comes from the demo's comment header. 225→ 226→--- 227→ 228→## Phase 5: Chrome — Header, Footer, GitHub Corner 229→ 230→### 5.1 GitHub corner (octocat SVG) 231→ 232→Absolute-positioned SVG in top-right corner linking to `https://github.com/user/web-tml24c`. Match web-tc24r pattern: 80x80 SVG, `fill: var(--accent)`, `color: var(--bg)`. 233→ 234→### 5.2 Footer 235→ 236→```html 237→
238→ MIT License · © 2026 Michael A Wright · 239→ COR24-TB · 240→ {git_sha} · {build_timestamp} · 241→ Demos 242→
243→``` 244→ 245→Font: 0.75em, color: `var(--dim)`, links: `var(--accent)`. 246→ 247→### 5.3 Font & contrast improvements 248→ 249→Current web-tml24c uses small fonts (11px labels, 13px code). Target: 250→- Output panel: **15px** monospace, color `#a6e3a1` (green) on `#1e1e2e` 251→- Input textarea: **15px** monospace, color `#cdd6f4` on `#282840` 252→- Panel headers: **13px** uppercase, color `#bac2de` (brighter than current `#6c7086`) 253→- Status text: **13px**, color `#bac2de` 254→- Toolbar labels: **14px**, semi-bold, color `#cdd6f4` 255→ 256→--- 257→ 258→## File Structure 259→ 260→``` 261→web-tml24c/ 262→├── asm/ 263→│ ├── repl-bare.s # NEW: compiled from tml24c/src/repl-bare.c 264→│ ├── repl-minimal.s # NEW: compiled from tml24c/src/repl-minimal.c 265→│ ├── repl-standard.s # NEW: compiled from tml24c/src/repl-standard.c 266→│ └── repl-full.s # NEW: compiled from tml24c/src/repl-full.s 267→├── src/ 268→│ ├── main.rs # Entry point (unchanged) 269→│ ├── lib.rs # App shell: toolbar, layout, GitHub corner, footer 270→│ ├── config.rs # NEW: PreludeTier, StackSize enums 271→│ ├── repl.rs # REWORK: multi-binary, hardware polling, demo loading 272→│ ├── demos.rs # NEW: demo metadata + embedded source 273→│ ├── docs.rs # NEW: documentation content per tier 274→│ ├── hardware.rs # NEW: LED, Switch Yew components 275→│ └── memory.rs # NEW: heap/stack gauge components 276→├── index.html # Updated CSS variables, larger fonts, higher contrast 277→├── Cargo.toml 278→├── Trunk.toml 279→└── docs/ 280→ ├── architecture.md 281→ └── plan.md # This file 282→``` 283→ 284→## Build Process 285→ 286→```bash 287→# 1. Compile all REPL variants (from tml24c repo) 288→cd ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/tml24c 289→for v in bare minimal standard full; do 290→ tc24r src/repl-$v.c -I src -o ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/web-tml24c/asm/repl-$v.s 291→done 292→ 293→# 2. Build WASM 294→cd ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/web-tml24c 295→trunk build # dev 296→trunk build --release # production (opt-level z, LTO) 297→ 298→# 3. Serve 299→trunk serve # port 9135 300→``` 301→ 302→## Verification 303→ 304→1. **Prelude switching**: Select each tier from dropdown, verify prompt appears, eval `(+ 1 2)` returns 3 in all modes 305→2. **Stack config**: Select 8K, run `tco.l24` demo (deep recursion) — should not crash 306→3. **Demos**: Load each demo, click Eval, verify output matches CLI (`just eval demos/X.l24`) 307→4. **LED widget**: Load `blink.l24`, click Eval — LED should toggle on/off 308→5. **Switch widget**: Click S2, run `(s2-pressed?)` — should return `t` 309→6. **Memory gauges**: Eval expressions, verify heap gauge increases; run recursive code, verify stack gauge responds 310→7. **Documentation**: Switch preludes, verify reference panel updates to show correct function set 311→8. **Footer**: Verify copyright, license, links, git SHA, timestamp all render 312→ 313→## Sequencing 314→ 315→Phase 1 (multi-binary) is prerequisite for all others. 316→Phase 2 (demos) and Phase 3 (hardware) can run in parallel after Phase 1. 317→Phase 4 (docs) and Phase 5 (chrome) can run in parallel after Phase 1. 318→ 319→Recommended: **1 → 2+3 parallel → 4+5 parallel → 6**. 320→ 321→--- 322→ 323→## Phase 6: Prelude Editor & Custom Preludes (Future) 324→ 325→### 6.1 Scheme prelude tier 326→ 327→tml24c will add a Scheme-flavored prelude with different naming conventions 328→and examples. The web UI needs to support dynamically-added prelude tiers 329→without hardcoding the list. 330→ 331→### 6.2 Prelude editor tab/dialog 332→ 333→A dedicated panel for viewing, editing, and creating custom preludes: 334→- View the source of any built-in prelude (read-only for built-ins) 335→- Create new custom preludes in a text editor 336→- Name/rename custom preludes 337→- Export prelude to `.l24` text file (download) 338→- Import prelude from `.l24` text file (upload) 339→- Save custom preludes to browser localStorage 340→ 341→### 6.3 Custom prelude execution 342→ 343→Custom preludes use the **bare** REPL binary. The prelude text is fed 344→via UART before the user's input, exactly like `cat prelude.l24 | cor24-run`. 345→The bare binary has no compiled-in prelude, so it evaluates the UART 346→input as Lisp definitions. 347→ 348→### 6.4 Per-prelude demo sets 349→ 350→Each prelude tier (including Scheme and custom) may have different 351→applicable demos. Demo metadata should include a list of compatible 352→preludes rather than a single required tier. 353→

1→[package]
     2→name = "cor24-isa"
     3→version = "0.1.0"
     4→edition = "2024"
     5→description = "COR24 ISA definitions: opcodes, encoding tables, branch constants"
     6→license = "MIT"
     7→
     8→[features]
     9→default = []
    10→serde = ["dep:serde"]
    11→
    12→[dependencies]
    13→serde = { version = "1.0", features = ["derive"], optional = true }

1→//! COR24 instruction opcodes and formats.
     2→
     3→/// COR24 instruction opcodes (5-bit, from decoded ROM)
     4→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
     5→#[cfg_attr(feature = "serde", derive(serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize))]
     6→#[repr(u8)]
     7→pub enum Opcode {
     8→    AddReg = 0x00, // add ra,rb
     9→    AddImm = 0x01, // add ra,dd
    10→    And = 0x02,    // and ra,rb
    11→    Bra = 0x03,    // bra dd
    12→    Brf = 0x04,    // brf dd
    13→    Brt = 0x05,    // brt dd
    14→    Ceq = 0x06,    // ceq ra,rb
    15→    Cls = 0x07,    // cls ra,rb (compare less signed)
    16→    Clu = 0x08,    // clu ra,rb (compare less unsigned)
    17→    Jal = 0x09,    // jal ra,(rb)
    18→    Jmp = 0x0A,    // jmp (ra)
    19→    La = 0x0B,     // la ra,dddddd
    20→    Lb = 0x0C,     // lb ra,dd(rb) (signed)
    21→    Lbu = 0x0D,    // lbu ra,dd(rb) (unsigned)
    22→    Lc = 0x0E,     // lc ra,dd (signed)
    23→    Lcu = 0x0F,    // lcu ra,dd (unsigned)
    24→    Lw = 0x10,     // lw ra,dd(rb)
    25→    Mov = 0x11,    // mov ra,rb or mov ra,c
    26→    Mul = 0x12,    // mul ra,rb
    27→    Or = 0x13,     // or ra,rb
    28→    Pop = 0x14,    // pop ra
    29→    Push = 0x15,   // push ra
    30→    Sb = 0x16,     // sb ra,dd(rb)
    31→    Shl = 0x17,    // shl ra,rb
    32→    Sra = 0x18,    // sra ra,rb
    33→    Srl = 0x19,    // srl ra,rb
    34→    Sub = 0x1A,    // sub ra,rb
    35→    SubSp = 0x1B,  // sub sp,dddddd
    36→    Sw = 0x1C,     // sw ra,dd(rb)
    37→    Sxt = 0x1D,    // sxt ra,rb
    38→    Xor = 0x1E,    // xor ra,rb
    39→    Zxt = 0x1F,    // zxt ra,rb
    40→    Invalid = 0xFF,
    41→}
    42→
    43→impl From<u8> for Opcode {
    44→    fn from(value: u8) -> Self {
    45→        match value {
    46→            0x00 => Opcode::AddReg,
    47→            0x01 => Opcode::AddImm,
    48→            0x02 => Opcode::And,
    49→            0x03 => Opcode::Bra,
    50→            0x04 => Opcode::Brf,
    51→            0x05 => Opcode::Brt,
    52→            0x06 => Opcode::Ceq,
    53→            0x07 => Opcode::Cls,
    54→            0x08 => Opcode::Clu,
    55→            0x09 => Opcode::Jal,
    56→            0x0A => Opcode::Jmp,
    57→            0x0B => Opcode::La,
    58→            0x0C => Opcode::Lb,
    59→            0x0D => Opcode::Lbu,
    60→            0x0E => Opcode::Lc,
    61→            0x0F => Opcode::Lcu,
    62→            0x10 => Opcode::Lw,
    63→            0x11 => Opcode::Mov,
    64→            0x12 => Opcode::Mul,
    65→            0x13 => Opcode::Or,
    66→            0x14 => Opcode::Pop,
    67→            0x15 => Opcode::Push,
    68→            0x16 => Opcode::Sb,
    69→            0x17 => Opcode::Shl,
    70→            0x18 => Opcode::Sra,
    71→            0x19 => Opcode::Srl,
    72→            0x1A => Opcode::Sub,
    73→            0x1B => Opcode::SubSp,
    74→            0x1C => Opcode::Sw,
    75→            0x1D => Opcode::Sxt,
    76→            0x1E => Opcode::Xor,
    77→            0x1F => Opcode::Zxt,
    78→            _ => Opcode::Invalid,
    79→        }
    80→    }
    81→}
    82→
    83→/// Decoded instruction with opcode and register operands
    84→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
    85→pub struct DecodedInstruction {
    86→    pub opcode: Opcode,
    87→    pub ra: u8,
    88→    pub rb: u8,
    89→}
    90→
    91→impl DecodedInstruction {
    92→    pub fn new(opcode: Opcode, ra: u8, rb: u8) -> Self {
    93→        Self {
    94→            opcode,
    95→            ra: ra & 0x07,
    96→            rb: rb & 0x07,
    97→        }
    98→    }
    99→
   100→    /// Decode from 12-bit ROM output
   101→    pub fn from_decoded(decoded: u16) -> Self {
   102→        let opcode = Opcode::from(((decoded >> 6) & 0x1F) as u8);
   103→        let ra = ((decoded >> 3) & 0x07) as u8;
   104→        let rb = (decoded & 0x07) as u8;
   105→        Self { opcode, ra, rb }
   106→    }
   107→}
   108→
   109→/// Instruction format type
   110→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
   111→pub enum InstructionFormat {
   112→    /// Single byte: opcode + registers encoded
   113→    SingleByte,
   114→    /// Two bytes: first byte + 8-bit immediate/offset
   115→    TwoBytes,
   116→    /// Four bytes: first byte + 24-bit address
   117→    FourBytes,
   118→}
   119→
   120→impl InstructionFormat {
   121→    /// Size in bytes
   122→    pub fn size(&self) -> usize {
   123→        match self {
   124→            InstructionFormat::SingleByte => 1,
   125→            InstructionFormat::TwoBytes => 2,
   126→            InstructionFormat::FourBytes => 4,
   127→        }
   128→    }
   129→}
   130→
   131→impl Opcode {
   132→    /// Get the instruction format for this opcode
   133→    pub fn format(&self) -> InstructionFormat {
   134→        match self {
   135→            Opcode::AddReg | Opcode::And | Opcode::Ceq | Opcode::Cls | Opcode::Clu
   136→            | Opcode::Jal | Opcode::Jmp | Opcode::Mov | Opcode::Mul | Opcode::Or
   137→            | Opcode::Pop | Opcode::Push | Opcode::Shl | Opcode::Sra | Opcode::Srl
   138→            | Opcode::Sub | Opcode::Sxt | Opcode::Xor | Opcode::Zxt => {
   139→                InstructionFormat::SingleByte
   140→            }
   141→            Opcode::AddImm | Opcode::Bra | Opcode::Brf | Opcode::Brt | Opcode::Lb
   142→            | Opcode::Lbu | Opcode::Lc | Opcode::Lcu | Opcode::Lw | Opcode::Sb
   143→            | Opcode::Sw => InstructionFormat::TwoBytes,
   144→            Opcode::La | Opcode::SubSp => InstructionFormat::FourBytes,
   145→            Opcode::Invalid => InstructionFormat::SingleByte,
   146→        }
   147→    }
   148→
   149→    /// Get mnemonic string for this opcode
   150→    pub fn mnemonic(&self) -> &'static str {
   151→        match self {
   152→            Opcode::AddReg | Opcode::AddImm => "add",
   153→            Opcode::And => "and",
   154→            Opcode::Bra => "bra",
   155→            Opcode::Brf => "brf",
   156→            Opcode::Brt => "brt",
   157→            Opcode::Ceq => "ceq",
   158→            Opcode::Cls => "cls",
   159→            Opcode::Clu => "clu",
   160→            Opcode::Jal => "jal",
   161→            Opcode::Jmp => "jmp",
   162→            Opcode::La => "la",
   163→            Opcode::Lb => "lb",
   164→            Opcode::Lbu => "lbu",
   165→            Opcode::Lc => "lc",
   166→            Opcode::Lcu => "lcu",
   167→            Opcode::Lw => "lw",
   168→            Opcode::Mov => "mov",
   169→            Opcode::Mul => "mul",
   170→            Opcode::Or => "or",
   171→            Opcode::Pop => "pop",
   172→            Opcode::Push => "push",
   173→            Opcode::Sb => "sb",
   174→            Opcode::Shl => "shl",
   175→            Opcode::Sra => "sra",
   176→            Opcode::Srl => "srl",
   177→            Opcode::Sub | Opcode::SubSp => "sub",
   178→            Opcode::Sw => "sw",
   179→            Opcode::Sxt => "sxt",
   180→            Opcode::Xor => "xor",
   181→            Opcode::Zxt => "zxt",
   182→            Opcode::Invalid => "???",
   183→        }
   184→    }
   185→}
   186→

1→//! COR24 branch range constants and helpers.
     2→//!
     3→//! Branch instructions (bra, brt, brf) use a 2-byte format:
     4→//!   byte 0: opcode
     5→//!   byte 1: signed 8-bit offset
     6→//!
     7→//! The offset is relative to branch_base = instruction_address + 4
     8→//! (accounting for the COR24 pipeline: 3 prefetch + 1 execute).
     9→
    10→/// Minimum branch offset (signed 8-bit).
    11→pub const BRANCH_OFFSET_MIN: i32 = -128;
    12→
    13→/// Maximum branch offset (signed 8-bit).
    14→pub const BRANCH_OFFSET_MAX: i32 = 127;
    15→
    16→/// Pipeline delay: branch base = instruction_address + this value.
    17→pub const BRANCH_PIPELINE_DELAY: u32 = 4;
    18→
    19→/// Maximum bytes a single instruction can occupy.
    20→pub const MAX_INSTRUCTION_BYTES: usize = 4;
    21→
    22→/// Maximum number of instructions between a branch and its target
    23→/// that guarantees a short branch is safe.
    24→///
    25→/// Calculated as: BRANCH_OFFSET_MAX / MAX_INSTRUCTION_BYTES = 127 / 4 = 31.
    26→/// This is conservative — most instructions are 1-2 bytes, so the actual
    27→/// reachable instruction count is usually higher.
    28→pub const MAX_SHORT_BRANCH_INSTRUCTIONS: usize = 31;
    29→
    30→/// Check if a short branch can reach from one instruction position to another.
    31→///
    32→/// Uses a conservative estimate: each instruction is at most 4 bytes.
    33→/// Returns true if the distance is guaranteed to be within range.
    34→pub fn can_short_branch(from_count: usize, to_count: usize) -> bool {
    35→    let distance = if from_count > to_count {
    36→        from_count - to_count
    37→    } else {
    38→        to_count - from_count
    39→    };
    40→    distance <= MAX_SHORT_BRANCH_INSTRUCTIONS
    41→}
    42→

1→//! Challenge system and self-test for COR24 emulator
     2→
     3→use crate::assembler::Assembler;
     4→use crate::cpu::executor::Executor;
     5→use crate::cpu::CpuState;
     6→
     7→/// A challenge for the user to complete
     8→#[derive(Clone)]
     9→pub struct Challenge {
    10→    pub id: usize,
    11→    pub name: String,
    12→    pub description: String,
    13→    pub initial_code: String,
    14→    pub hint: String,
    15→    pub validator: fn(&CpuState) -> bool,
    16→}
    17→
    18→/// Get all available challenges
    19→pub fn get_challenges() -> Vec<Challenge> {
    20→    vec![
    21→        Challenge {
    22→            id: 1,
    23→            name: "Load and Add".to_string(),
    24→            description: "Load the value 10 into r0, then add 5 to it. Result should be 15 in r0."
    25→                .to_string(),
    26→            initial_code: "; Load 10 into r0, add 5\n; Result: r0 = 15\n\n".to_string(),
    27→            hint: "Use 'lc r0,10' to load 10, then 'add r0,5' to add 5".to_string(),
    28→            validator: |cpu| cpu.get_reg(0) == 15,
    29→        },
    30→        Challenge {
    31→            id: 2,
    32→            name: "Compare and Branch".to_string(),
    33→            description: "Set r0 to 1 if 5 < 10 (signed), otherwise 0. Use cls and brt/brf."
    34→                .to_string(),
    35→            initial_code: "; Compare 5 < 10 and set r0 accordingly\n; Result: r0 = 1\n\n"
    36→                .to_string(),
    37→            hint: "Load values, use cls to compare, then mov r0,c to get the result".to_string(),
    38→            validator: |cpu| cpu.get_reg(0) == 1,
    39→        },
    40→        Challenge {
    41→            id: 3,
    42→            name: "Stack Operations".to_string(),
    43→            description: "Push values 1, 2, 3 onto the stack, then pop them into r0, r1, r2."
    44→                .to_string(),
    45→            initial_code: "; Push 1, 2, 3 then pop into r0, r1, r2\n; Result: r0=3, r1=2, r2=1\n\n"
    46→                .to_string(),
    47→            hint: "Remember LIFO order - last pushed is first popped".to_string(),
    48→            validator: |cpu| cpu.get_reg(0) == 3 && cpu.get_reg(1) == 2 && cpu.get_reg(2) == 1,
    49→        },
    50→        Challenge {
    51→            id: 4,
    52→            name: "Max of Two".to_string(),
    53→            description: "Set r0 to the maximum of r0=7 and r1=12 (without branching). Use mov ra,c!"
    54→                .to_string(),
    55→            initial_code: "; Find max of r0=7 and r1=12, store result in r0\n; Hint: Use COR24's mov ra,c feature\n; Result: r0 = 12\n\n        lc      r0,7\n        lc      r1,12\n\n        ; Your code here\n\nhalt:   bra     halt\n"
    56→                .to_string(),
    57→            hint: "cls sets C if r0 < r1. If true, you want r1. Use sub/add with C flag.".to_string(),
    58→            validator: |cpu| cpu.get_reg(0) == 12,
    59→        },
    60→        Challenge {
    61→            id: 5,
    62→            name: "Byte Sign Extension".to_string(),
    63→            description: "Load -50 (0xCE) as unsigned into r0, then sign-extend it. Result should be 0xFFFFCE."
    64→                .to_string(),

    66→                .to_string(),

    68→            validator: |cpu| cpu.get_reg(0) == 0xFFFFCE,
    69→        },
    70→    ]
    71→}

1→//! COR24 register definitions.
     2→
     3→/// Register names indexed by 3-bit register number.
     4→pub const REG_NAMES: [&str; 8] = ["r0", "r1", "r2", "fp", "sp", "z", "r6", "r7"];
     5→
     6→/// Get register name from 3-bit index.
     7→pub fn reg_name(reg: u8) -> &'static str {
     8→    REG_NAMES[(reg & 0x07) as usize]
     9→}
    10→
    11→/// Parse a register name to its 3-bit index.
    12→pub fn parse_register(s: &str) -> Option<u8> {
    13→    match s.to_lowercase().as_str() {
    14→        "r0" => Some(0),
    15→        "r1" => Some(1),
    16→        "r2" => Some(2),
    17→        "fp" => Some(3),
    18→        "sp" => Some(4),
    19→        "z" | "c" => Some(5),
    20→        "iv" => Some(6),
    21→        "ir" => Some(7),
    22→        _ => None,
    23→    }
    24→}

1→# CLAUDE.md
     2→
     3→This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
     4→
     5→## Build Commands
     6→
     7→**CRITICAL: NEVER run `trunk` commands directly.** Always use the shell scripts below. Running bare `trunk serve` or `trunk build` with wrong flags breaks the build (wrong port, missing `--release`, wrong `--public-url`). The scripts encode the correct arguments.
     8→
     9→```bash
    10→# Dev server with hot reload (http://localhost:7401/cor24-rs/)
    11→./serve.sh              # incremental build + serve
    12→./serve.sh --clean      # clean build + serve (use after strange build errors)
    13→
    14→# Production build (outputs to pages/)
    15→./build.sh              # incremental build
    16→./build.sh --clean      # clean build
    17→
    18→# Run tests (OK to run cargo directly for non-build commands)
    19→cargo test
    20→
    21→# Check compilation (OK to run cargo directly)
    22→cargo check
    23→cargo check --target wasm32-unknown-unknown   # checks WASM-only code too
    24→cargo clippy --target wasm32-unknown-unknown  # lint check
    25→```
    26→
    27→Prerequisites: Rust 1.75+, Trunk (`cargo install trunk`), `rustup target add wasm32-unknown-unknown`.
    28→
    29→## Commit Discipline
    30→
    31→**Commit early and often.** Each commit should do one thing. Do not accumulate large changesets.
    32→
    33→- Commit after each logical change: a bug fix, a new feature, a refactor, an extraction — each is its own commit.
    34→- Small commits enable cherry-picking, rebasing, and bisecting. Large commits make all of these painful.
    35→- If a task involves multiple steps (e.g., extract data to files, then update callers, then add a new feature), commit after each step.
    36→- Commit working code. Run `cargo check --target wasm32-unknown-unknown` before committing WASM changes.
    37→- Deployment commits (`pages/` updates via `./build.sh`) should be separate from code changes when practical.
    38→
    39→## Deployment
    40→
    41→The `pages/` directory contains pre-built production assets and is committed to git. GitHub Actions deploys from `pages/` on push to `main` — no CI build step, just upload.
    42→
    43→**To deploy changes to the live site:**
    44→1. `./build.sh --clean` — always use `--clean` to avoid stale cached WASM artifacts
    45→2. `git add pages/` and commit (separate from code changes)
    46→3. `git push`
    47→
    48→**IMPORTANT:** Use `./build.sh --clean`, not `./build.sh`. Incremental builds can serve stale code if `include_str!()` data files changed but Rust source didn't.
    49→
    50→## Architecture
    51→
    52→This is a browser-based COR24 CPU emulator written in Rust, compiled to WebAssembly via Trunk. The COR24 is a real 24-bit RISC architecture (C-Oriented RISC) designed for embedded systems education.
    53→
    54→### Workspace Structure
    55→
    56→- **`src/`** — Main application crate (`cor24-emulator`)
    57→- **`components/`** — Reusable Yew UI components library
    58→- **`rust-to-cor24/`** — Standalone CLI tool (not part of workspace). Pipeline: Rust → `rustc --target msp430-none-elf --emit asm` → MSP430 ASM → `msp430-to-cor24 --entry <func>` → COR24 ASM (with `bra <entry>` reset vector prologue at address 0). See `rust-to-cor24/README.md` for full pipeline documentation. Not compiled to WASM — used offline to generate pipeline examples shown in the Web UI's Rust tab.
    59→
    60→### Core Modules (src/)
    61→
    62→- **`cpu/`** — CPU emulator core
    63→  - `state.rs` — CPU state, memory (64KB subset of 24-bit address space), memory-mapped I/O (LED/switch at `0xFF0000`, UART at `0xFFFF00-02`)
    64→  - `executor.rs` — Instruction execution engine
    65→  - `decode_rom.rs` — Decode ROM extracted from actual FPGA Verilog hardware
    66→  - `encode.rs` — Instruction encoding tables
    67→  - `instruction.rs` — Opcode definitions, variable-length instructions (1/2/4 bytes)
    68→- **`assembler.rs`** — Two-pass assembler producing machine code from COR24 assembly
    69→- **`wasm.rs`** — `WasmCpu` wrapper exposing CPU to JavaScript/Yew via `wasm_bindgen`
    70→- **`app.rs`** — Main Yew `#[function_component(App)]` — all application state and UI logic. This is the largest file; it manages two independent CPU instances (assembler tab and Rust pipeline tab)
    71→- **`challenge.rs`** — Example programs and challenge definitions
    72→
    73→### UI Components (components/)
    74→
    75→Yew components: `Header`, `Sidebar`, `TabBar`, `ProgramArea`, `RegisterPanel`, `MemoryViewer`, `Modal`, `Collapsible`, `RustPipeline`. The `RustPipeline` component implements a wizard-driven 3-column view showing the Rust→MSP430 ASM→COR24 ASM→Machine Code pipeline with pre-built examples.
    76→
    77→### Key Patterns
    78→
    79→- **Two CPU instances**: `app.rs` maintains separate `WasmCpu` state for the Assembler tab and Rust Pipeline tab
    80→- **Animated run with stop**: Uses `Rc<Cell<bool>>` for stop flags and `Rc<Cell<u8>>` for switch state to ensure immediate visibility across async closures (Yew state updates are deferred)
    81→- **Hardware-accurate I/O**: Matches COR24-TB test board — single LED (D2) and button (S2) using bit 0 of `IO_LEDSWDAT` (`0xFF0000`). Reference hardware docs are in `references/COR24-TB/`
    82→- **Conditional compilation**: `app.rs` and `wasm.rs` are `#[cfg(target_arch = "wasm32")]` only; `cpu/`, `assembler`, and `challenge` modules compile on native targets for `cargo test`
    83→- **`build.rs`**: Embeds git SHA, build timestamp, and hostname into the binary via env vars
    84→
    85→### CSS
    86→
    87→Two stylesheet files in `styles/`: `asm-game.css` (component styles) and `layout.css` (page structure). Referenced in `index.html` via Trunk's `data-trunk` attributes.
    88→
    89→### Reference Materials
    90→
    91→`references/COR24-TB/` contains the actual hardware documentation: Verilog source (including `cor24_io.v` for I/O address decoding), demo C programs (blinky, sieve, etc.), and FPGA project files. The decode ROM in `decode_rom.rs` was extracted from `cor24_cpu.v` using `scripts/extract_decode_rom.py`.
    92→

     4→
     5→## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)
     6→
     7→This project uses AgentRail. Every session follows this exact sequence:
     8→
     9→### 1. START (do this FIRST, before anything else)
    10→```bash
    11→agentrail next
    12→```
    13→Read the output carefully. It tells you your current step, prompt, skill docs, and past trajectories.
    14→
    15→### 2. BEGIN (immediately after reading the next output)
    16→```bash
    17→agentrail begin
    18→```
    19→
    20→### 3. WORK (do what the step prompt says)
    21→Do NOT ask the user "want me to proceed?" or "shall I start?". The step prompt IS your instruction. Execute it.
    22→
    23→### 4. COMMIT (after the work is done)
    24→Commit your code changes with git.
    25→
    26→### 5. COMPLETE (LAST thing, after committing)
    27→```bash
    28→agentrail complete --summary "what you accomplished" \
    29→  --reward 1 \
    30→  --actions "tools and approach used"
    31→```
    32→If the step failed: `--reward -1 --failure-mode "what went wrong"`
    33→If the saga is finished: add `--done`
    34→
    35→### 6. STOP (after complete, DO NOT continue working)
    36→Do NOT make any further code changes after running agentrail complete.
    37→Any changes after complete are untracked and invisible to the next session.
    38→If you see more work to do, it belongs in the NEXT step, not this session.
    39→
    40→Do NOT skip any of these steps. The next session depends on your trajectory recording.
    41→
    42→## Project: tf24a — Tiny Forth for COR24 in Assembler
    43→
    44→Clean-room DTC Forth for the COR24 24-bit RISC ISA. Assembler kernel, self-extending in Forth.
    45→
    46→### Tools
    47→- `cor24-run --run file.s [opts]` — assemble and run COR24 assembly
    48→- `cor24-run --run file.s --dump --speed 0 -n N` — run N instructions, dump state
    49→- `cor24-run --run file.s --terminal --echo --speed 0` — interactive UART session
    50→- `cor24-run --run file.s -u 'input' --speed 0 --dump` — feed UART input, dump state
    51→
    52→### COR24 Assembly Syntax
    53→- Labels on own line: `label:` (no inline `label: instr`)
    54→- Comments: `;` only (not `#`)
    55→- Decimal immediates (not hex): `la r0, -65280` not `la r0, 0xFF0100`
    56→- `.word label` — emits 24-bit address (one label per directive)
    57→- `.byte 72, 101, 108` — raw bytes (no string literals)
    58→- No `.align` directive; manually pad with `.byte 0`
    59→
    60→### Register Allocation (Frozen)
    61→- r0 = W (work register) / scratch
    62→- r1 = RSP (return stack pointer, SRAM ~0x0F0000 growing down)
    63→- r2 = IP (instruction pointer for threaded code)
    64→- sp = DSP (data stack, hardware push/pop in EBR)
    65→- fp = available as extra scratch
    66→
    67→### Key ISA Constraints
    68→- Only r0, r1, r2, sp support `add rX, imm8`; fp does NOT
    69→- push/pop only work with sp (1-byte instructions)
    70→- Branch offset ±127 bytes (signed 8-bit)
    71→- `jal r1,(r0)` sets r1=PC+1, jumps to r0 — conflicts with r1=RSP
    72→- Cell size = 3 bytes (24-bit words)
    73→
    74→### DTC Inner Interpreter
    75→```
    76→; NEXT (inline everywhere or as tail of each primitive):
    77→;   lw r0, 0(r2)    ; W = mem[IP] — fetch CFA from thread
    78→;   add r2, 3       ; IP += cell
    79→;   jmp (r0)        ; execute code at CFA
    80→```
    81→
    82→### UART I/O
    83→- Data register: address -65280 (0xFF0100)
    84→- Status register: address -65279 (0xFF0101)
    85→- TX busy: bit 7 of status
    86→- RX ready: bit 0 of status
    87→

1→; forth.s — tf24a Phase 1: Bootstrap, UART I/O, Stack Tests
     2→; COR24 DTC Forth bootstrap
     3→;
     4→; Register allocation (frozen):
     5→;   r0 = W (work/scratch)
     6→;   r1 = RSP (return stack pointer, grows down from 0x0F0000)
     7→;   r2 = IP (instruction pointer — unused in Phase 1 tests)
     8→;   sp = DSP (data stack, hardware push/pop in EBR)
     9→;   fp = limited scratch (many instructions don't support fp)
    10→;
    11→; UART: data at 0xFF0100 (-65280), status at 0xFF0101 (-65279)
    12→;   TX busy = status bit 7, RX ready = status bit 0
    13→
    14→; ============================================================
    15→; Entry point (address 0)
    16→; ============================================================
    17→_start:
    18→    ; sp is set by hardware (EBR at 0xFEEC00)
    19→    la r1, 983040       ; r1 = 0x0F0000 return stack base
    20→
    21→    ; ============================================================
    22→    ; Test 1: Data stack + UART — print "OK\n"
    23→    ; Push in reverse order (LIFO), pop and emit in order
    24→    ; ============================================================
    25→    lc r0, 10           ; '\n'
    26→    push r0
    27→    lc r0, 75           ; 'K'
    28→    push r0
    29→    lc r0, 79           ; 'O'
    30→    push r0
    31→
    32→    la r2, -65280       ; r2 = UART data (IP not needed in Phase 1)
    33→
    34→    ; Emit 'O' — poll TX busy, then write
    35→    pop r0
    36→    push r0             ; save byte while polling
    37→tx1:
    38→    lb r0, 1(r2)        ; status (sign-extended; bit 7 → negative)
    39→    cls r0, z           ; C = (status < 0) = TX busy
    40→    brt tx1
    41→    pop r0              ; restore byte
    42→    sb r0, 0(r2)        ; write to UART
    43→
    44→    ; Emit 'K'
    45→    pop r0
    46→    push r0
    47→tx2:
    48→    lb r0, 1(r2)
    49→    cls r0, z
    50→    brt tx2
    51→    pop r0
    52→    sb r0, 0(r2)
    53→
    54→    ; Emit '\n'
    55→    pop r0
    56→    push r0
    57→tx3:
    58→    lb r0, 1(r2)
    59→    cls r0, z
    60→    brt tx3
    61→    pop r0
    62→    sb r0, 0(r2)
    63→
    64→    ; ============================================================
    65→    ; Test 2: Return stack — push 42, clear r0, pop, emit '*'
    66→    ; ============================================================
    67→    lc r0, 42           ; 42 = ASCII '*'
    68→    add r1, -3          ; RSP -= cell
    69→    sw r0, 0(r1)        ; push to return stack
    70→
    71→    lc r0, 0            ; clear r0 to prove round-trip
    72→
    73→    lw r0, 0(r1)        ; pop from return stack
    74→    add r1, 3           ; RSP += cell
    75→
    76→    ; Emit '*' with TX polling
    77→    push r0
    78→tx4:
    79→    lb r0, 1(r2)
    80→    cls r0, z
    81→    brt tx4
    82→    pop r0
    83→    sb r0, 0(r2)
    84→
    85→    ; Emit '\n'
    86→    lc r0, 10
    87→    push r0
    88→tx5:
    89→    lb r0, 1(r2)
    90→    cls r0, z
    91→    brt tx5
    92→    pop r0
    93→    sb r0, 0(r2)
    94→
    95→    ; ============================================================
    96→    ; Halt
    97→    ; ============================================================
    98→halt:
    99→    bra halt
   100→
   101→; ============================================================
   102→; EMIT ( c -- ) : Write character to UART TX with busy-wait
   103→; Saves byte and IP (r2) on return stack
   104→; ============================================================
   105→do_emit:
   106→    pop r0              ; r0 = character from data stack
   107→    add r1, -3          ; save IP on return stack
   108→    sw r2, 0(r1)
   109→    add r1, -3          ; save byte on return stack
   110→    sw r0, 0(r1)
   111→emit_poll:
   112→    la r0, -65280       ; r0 = UART base
   113→    lb r0, 1(r0)        ; r0 = status (sign-extended; bit 7 → negative)
   114→    cls r0, z           ; C = (status < 0) = TX busy
   115→    brt emit_poll       ; loop while TX busy
   116→    lw r0, 0(r1)        ; restore byte
   117→    add r1, 3
   118→    la r2, -65280       ; r2 = UART base (temporarily clobber IP)
   119→    sb r0, 0(r2)        ; write byte to UART TX
   120→    lw r2, 0(r1)        ; restore IP
   121→    add r1, 3
   122→    ; NEXT
   123→    lw r0, 0(r2)        ; W = mem[IP]
   124→    add r2, 3           ; IP += cell
   125→    jmp (r0)            ; execute next word
   126→
   127→; ============================================================
   128→; KEY ( -- c ) : Read character from UART RX with busy-wait
   129→; Saves IP (r2) on return stack
   130→; ============================================================
   131→do_key:
   132→    add r1, -3          ; save IP on return stack
   133→    sw r2, 0(r1)
   134→key_poll:
   135→    la r0, -65280       ; r0 = UART base
   136→    lbu r0, 1(r0)       ; r0 = status byte
   137→    lcu r2, 1           ; r2 = bit 0 mask (RX ready)
   138→    and r0, r2          ; isolate bit 0
   139→    ceq r0, z           ; C = (bit0 == 0) = RX not ready
   140→    brt key_poll        ; loop until RX ready
   141→    la r0, -65280       ; reload UART base
   142→    lbu r0, 0(r0)       ; read byte (acknowledges RX)
   143→    push r0             ; push to data stack
   144→    lw r2, 0(r1)        ; restore IP
   145→    add r1, 3
   146→    ; NEXT
   147→    lw r0, 0(r2)        ; W = mem[IP]
   148→    add r2, 3           ; IP += cell
   149→    jmp (r0)            ; execute next word
   150→

     4→
     5→I once used Forth a long time ago and after that I read a book called "Threaded Interpetive Languages" and I've wanted to learn more by teaching.  I want to build a faithful implementation of Forth for a target ISA (COR24-TB, MakerLisp.com).  I have an emulator for the board, an assembler for this ISA, and a C compiler that emits assembler source.  How should I bootsrap Forth which is mostly implemented in itself.  What are the historical approaches?  What is the license for public implementations of Forth.  Should I port using a permissive license, if available, or just do a clean-room bootstrapped implementation?  Should I start with C (because it gives me malloc/free, printf, stdio.h, strings.h etc. -- I do not have a C runtime.  The entire C support is in .h and .c files.  No linker, no loader.  My target hardware (and emulator) have SRAM for code, locals, heap and EBR memory for the stack, and memory-mapped I/O.  I can generate assembler inline in C (for interrupt handlers to deal with specific interrupt registers).  The target ISA has 3 general purpose registers, a PC, frame pointer and stack pointer.  Is there good public documentation on how to implement Forth.  Which words are minimal for a dictionary.  How are more features added? what is the terminology for the base set of Forth words and add on feature sets?
     6→
     7→
     8→Thought for 15s
     9→You want a bootstrapping plan for a faithful Forth on COR24-TB, with attention to:
    10→
    11→historical ways Forth systems were brought up on new machines,
    12→
    13→whether to start in assembler vs C,
    14→
    15→how much of Forth must exist before Forth can extend itself,
    16→
    17→what the minimal dictionary / kernel words are,
    18→
    19→how standardized word sets are named,
    20→
    21→and what the license tradeoffs are if you port or borrow from existing systems.
    22→
    23→High-level recommendation
    24→For your hardware and toolchain, I would not start by porting a large hosted ANS Forth. I would build a small native kernel in assembler, possibly with a tiny amount of C only for optional host-side experiments, then let that kernel grow itself in Forth.
    25→
    26→That is the classic path, and it fits your constraints:
    27→
    28→no linker / loader,
    29→
    30→no real libc/runtime,
    31→
    32→tight control over stacks, code layout, and interrupt paths,
    33→
    34→memory-mapped I/O,
    35→
    36→only a few registers,
    37→
    38→desire for faithfulness and educational value.
    39→
    40→Historically, Forths are often brought up with a tiny nucleus in machine code, then the compiler, interpreter, control structures, defining words, and tools are layered on top in Forth itself. That is exactly the bootstrapping style described in the threaded-language tradition and reflected in small educational systems like JonesForth. Threaded-code systems represent definitions largely as lists of addresses of other words, which is why they bootstrap so naturally. 
    41→
    42→Architecture first: what you are really building
    43→A practical Forth for COR24 needs these layers:
    44→
    45→1. Inner interpreter / execution engine
    46→This is the heart of classic threaded Forth:
    47→
    48→fetch the next code field / execution token,
    49→
    50→dispatch to the word’s implementation,
    51→
    52→handle NEXT efficiently.
    53→
    54→This is where your ISA matters most.
    55→
    56→2. Data stack and return stack
    57→Forth normally uses two stacks:
    58→
    59→data stack for operands,
    60→
    61→return stack for control flow and temporary storage.
    62→
    63→Given your memory layout, I would strongly consider:
    64→
    65→EBR memory for return stack, if it is fast and deterministic,
    66→
    67→SRAM for dictionary/code and data stack,
    68→
    69→leave C-style call frames out of the core kernel if possible.
    70→
    71→3. Dictionary
    72→Each word needs metadata plus executable behavior:
    73→
    74→link to previous word,
    75→
    76→name,
    77→
    78→flags/immediacy/visibility,
    79→
    80→code field,
    81→
    82→parameter field.
    83→
    84→4. Text interpreter
    85→Read a token, search dictionary, either:
    86→
    87→execute it immediately in interpret state, or
    88→
    89→compile it in compile state,
    90→
    91→otherwise parse a number.
    92→
    93→5. Compiler
    94→Initially very small:
    95→
    96→append execution tokens / addresses to current definition,
    97→
    98→support : and ;,
    99→
   100→later add control structures and defining words.
   101→
   102→6. Outer environment
   103→terminal I/O,
   104→
   105→block or line input,
   106→
   107→error handling,
   108→
   109→optional persistence / image save.
   110→
   111→Historical bootstrap approaches
   112→There are several historically common approaches.
   113→
   114→Approach A: Assembler kernel, then self-extension
   115→This is the classic and the one I recommend.
   116→
   117→You write in assembler:
   118→
   119→the stacks,
   120→
   121→dictionary layout,
   122→
   123→inner interpreter,
   124→
   125→a small set of primitives,
   126→
   127→: and ; support,
   128→
   129→tokenizer and number parser,
   130→
   131→enough I/O to talk to a console.
   132→
   133→Then you load additional Forth source that defines higher-level words in Forth.
   134→
   135→Why this is good for COR24
   136→
   137→faithful to how many Forths were actually built,
   138→
   139→minimal assumptions,
   140→
   141→direct control over registers and memory,
   142→
   143→no dependency on a C runtime model that fights Forth.
   144→
   145→Downside
   146→
   147→more initial assembler work,
   148→
   149→debugging the first 1000 lines can be painful.
   150→
   151→Approach B: Portable C kernel, then Forth on top
   152→This is the pForth style: a portable ANS Forth kernel written in C, intended to be ported broadly. pForth explicitly describes itself as a portable ANS Forth based on an ANSI C kernel. 
   153→
   154→Why people do this
   155→
   156→easier portability,
   157→
   158→easier development on hosted systems,
   159→
   160→easier testing before bare-metal adaptation.
   161→
   162→Why I would not make this your primary path
   163→
   164→your C is freestanding and self-contained, not backed by a conventional loader/libc,
   165→
   166→Forth wants exact stack discipline and control over calling conventions,
   167→
   168→your machine has only a few general-purpose registers,
   169→
   170→C function calling and Forth threading may fight each other,
   171→
   172→if you do it in C first, you may accidentally implement a C-hosted interpreter instead of a faithful target Forth.
   173→
   174→Approach C: Meta-compiler / cross-compiler from another Forth
   175→Another classic method is to use an existing Forth on a host machine to generate a target image. This is elegant and historically important, but it adds conceptual complexity early.
   176→
   177→I would delay this.
   178→It becomes attractive after you have:
   179→
   180→one working target kernel,
   181→
   182→a clear target image format,
   183→
   184→confidence in dictionary layout.
   185→
   186→Approach D: Subroutine-threaded Forth using native CALL/RET
   187→Instead of classic indirect threading, each colon definition compiles machine calls to primitives/words.
   188→
   189→Pros
   190→
   191→simpler model on some CPUs,
   192→
   193→better interoperability with native code,
   194→
   195→easier debugging in some cases.
   196→
   197→Cons
   198→
   199→can be less faithful to the classic “threaded interpretive” model you specifically want to learn from,
   200→
   201→depends heavily on COR24 call/return cost and return-stack behavior.
   202→
   203→For learning, I would start with indirect-threaded or direct-threaded style, then later experiment with subroutine threading.
   204→
   205→Which threading model to start with?
   206→The main historical families are:
   207→
   208→Indirect-threaded code (ITC)
   209→Parameter fields hold pointers to code fields; the inner interpreter dereferences more than once.
   210→
   211→Direct-threaded code (DTC)
   212→Compiled code points more directly at executable fragments.
   213→
   214→Subroutine-threaded code (STC)
   215→Definitions are machine call sequences.
   216→
   217→Token-threaded code
   218→Compact tokens index into a dispatch table.
   219→
   220→For a first faithful system, I would choose:
   221→
   222→Start with indirect-threaded or direct-threaded
   223→Why:
   224→
   225→aligns closely with the classic literature on threaded interpretive languages, 
   226→
   227→makes the dictionary structure obvious,
   228→
   229→keeps colon definitions compact,
   230→
   231→makes DOCOL, EXIT, LIT, and defining words conceptually clean.
   232→
   233→My bias for your target
   234→Because COR24 has only 3 GP registers, you may want to test both:
   235→
   236→ITC if simplicity of implementation matters most,
   237→
   238→DTC if you can shave a dereference and make NEXT cheaper.
   239→
   240→This is one of the few places where benchmarking on your emulator is worth doing very early.
   241→
   242→Bootstrapping plan I would use
   243→Phase 0 — Decide the target model
   244→Before coding, freeze these design decisions:
   245→
   246→cell size: likely one machine word on COR24,
   247→
   248→endianness,
   249→
   250→alignment rules,
   251→
   252→dictionary header layout,
   253→
   254→threaded model: ITC or DTC,
   255→
   256→separate return stack: yes,
   257→
   258→input source: UART / terminal,
   259→
   260→numeric formats: start with decimal only, maybe hex later.
   261→
   262→Write this down first. Forth implementations become much easier once the dictionary and execution token model are fixed.
   263→
   264→Phase 1 — Write the micro-kernel in assembler
   265→Implement only these machine-level pieces:
   266→
   267→Core runtime
   268→reset/startup
   269→
   270→memory map initialization
   271→
   272→stack pointers
   273→
   274→terminal I/O
   275→
   276→panic / error output
   277→
   278→Inner interpreter primitives
   279→NEXT
   280→
   281→DOCOL
   282→
   283→EXIT
   284→
   285→LIT
   286→
   287→branch primitive
   288→
   289→zero-branch primitive
   290→
   291→Stack primitives
   292→At minimum:
   293→
   294→DROP
   295→
   296→DUP
   297→
   298→SWAP
   299→
   300→OVER
   301→
   302→ROT can wait
   303→
   304→>R
   305→
   306→R>
   307→
   308→R@
   309→
   310→Arithmetic / logic
   311→At minimum:
   312→
   313→+
   314→
   315→-
   316→
   317→AND
   318→
   319→OR
   320→
   321→XOR
   322→
   323→=
   324→
   325→<
   326→
   327→0=
   328→
   329→maybe UM* later
   330→
   331→Memory access
   332→At minimum:
   333→
   334→@
   335→
   336→!
   337→
   338→C@
   339→
   340→C!
   341→
   342→,
   343→
   344→C,
   345→
   346→Dictionary/compiler support
   347→At minimum:
   348→
   349→CREATE or at least the machinery behind it
   350→
   351→:
   352→
   353→;
   354→
   355→,
   356→
   357→ALLOT
   358→
   359→HERE
   360→
   361→LATEST or equivalent internal variable
   362→
   363→FIND
   364→
   365→number parser
   366→
   367→EXECUTE
   368→
   369→Terminal / parsing
   370→At minimum:
   371→
   372→KEY
   373→
   374→EMIT
   375→
   376→token parser
   377→
   378→WORD-like mechanism or simpler equivalent
   379→
   380→This is enough to get to a very small self-hosting core.
   381→
   382→Phase 2 — Bring up the text interpreter
   383→Your first interactive milestone should be:
   384→
   385→type 1 2 + .
   386→
   387→define : SQUARE DUP * ;
   388→
   389→run 5 SQUARE .
   390→
   391→You are not “done” when primitives run. You are “done” when the system can:
   392→
   393→interpret tokens,
   394→
   395→compile new words,
   396→
   397→execute compiled definitions.
   398→
   399→That is the moment the bootstrap flips from “assembler project” to “Forth project.”
   400→
   401→Phase 3 — Move higher-level words into Forth source
   402→Once : and ; work, stop adding convenience features in assembler unless they are truly primitive or performance-critical.
   403→
   404→Now define in Forth:
   405→
   406→stack convenience words,
   407→
   408→arithmetic combinations,
   409→
   410→conditionals,
   411→
   412→loops,
   413→
   414→comments,
   415→
   416→variables/constants,
   417→
   418→simple tools,
   419→
   420→decompiler/introspection later.
   421→
   422→This is where the real educational value is.
   423→
   424→Phase 4 — Add defining words and control structures
   425→Classic Forth grows rapidly once you have:
   426→
   427→CREATE
   428→
   429→DOES>
   430→
   431→VARIABLE
   432→
   433→CONSTANT
   434→
   435→IMMEDIATE
   436→
   437→[ ]
   438→
   439→IF ELSE THEN
   440→
   441→BEGIN UNTIL AGAIN WHILE REPEAT
   442→
   443→DO LOOP later, if you want ANS-style loop indices
   444→
   445→These are usually not all primitive. Many are compiled with branch patching and immediate words.
   446→
   447→Phase 5 — Decide how standard you want to be
   448→At this point choose one of two goals:
   449→
   450→Goal A: Faithful historical Forth
   451→compact,
   452→
   453→minimal,
   454→
   455→close to FIG-Forth / early model,
   456→
   457→easier to understand and port,
   458→
   459→fewer portability guarantees.
   460→
   461→Goal B: ANS/ISO-ish Forth
   462→larger surface area,
   463→
   464→clearer conformance target,
   465→
   466→easier to compare against documentation and test suites,
   467→
   468→more work.
   469→
   470→For teaching, I would do A first, then B selectively.
   471→
   472→Should you start with C?
   473→My answer: use C only as a helper, not as the kernel foundation
   474→Because you said:
   475→
   476→no full C runtime,
   477→
   478→no loader/linker,
   479→
   480→.h and .c support is all local,
   481→
   482→memory is explicitly partitioned,
   483→
   484→you can emit inline assembler,
   485→
   486→interrupts are special.
   487→
   488→That makes C useful for:
   489→
   490→host-side experiments,
   491→
   492→generating tables,
   493→
   494→testing parsers,
   495→
   496→maybe prototyping the tokenizer or number parser,
   497→
   498→maybe a reference implementation of dictionary algorithms.
   499→
   500→But I would not make the Forth nucleus depend on malloc/free/printf/stdio unless you are creating a temporary host simulator version.
   501→
   502→Why not rely on malloc/free
   503→A real Forth usually does not want heap allocation in the C sense for its core dictionary model. It typically manages:
   504→
   505→dictionary space,
   506→
   507→stacks,
   508→
   509→buffers,
   510→
   511→user area
   512→explicitly.
   513→
   514→Why not rely on printf
   515→It gives you convenience early, but it delays the work of creating actual Forth I/O words and formatting.
   516→
   517→Best compromise
   518→Make two builds if useful:
   519→
   520→hosted reference build in C, for experimenting,
   521→
   522→target-native assembler build for the real system.
   523→
   524→But treat the hosted build as scaffolding, not the product.
   525→
   526→Clean-room vs porting an existing implementation
   527→Best practical answer: read widely, borrow ideas, but implement your own kernel cleanly
   528→That gives you the best combination of:
   529→
   530→educational value,
   531→
   532→license safety,
   533→
   534→architectural fit for COR24,
   535→
   536→historical faithfulness.
   537→
   538→Why a direct port is less attractive
   539→Most existing Forths assume:
   540→
   541→a conventional assembler,
   542→
   543→a host OS,
   544→
   545→richer calling conventions,
   546→
   547→file I/O,
   548→
   549→more mature loader/linker support,
   550→
   551→different memory assumptions.
   552→
   553→Porting can become harder than re-implementing a minimal kernel.
   554→
   555→When porting is worth it
   556→Port if you find a system that is:
   557→
   558→tiny,
   559→
   560→well documented,
   561→
   562→architecturally similar,
   563→
   564→under a permissive or public-domain-equivalent license,
   565→
   566→structurally close to what you want.
   567→
   568→For learning, JonesForth is excellent because it is a small, literate educational implementation and is released to the public domain. 
   569→
   570→For portability ideas, pForth is valuable because it is a portable ANS Forth in C and is described as public domain; Debian also lists it under BSD-0 in packaging metadata. 
   571→
   572→Historically, FIG-Forth and eForth are also strongly associated with public-domain-style distribution and portability-minded dissemination. 
   573→
   574→My recommendation
   575→Do a clean-room implementation of the kernel and dictionary layout.
   576→
   577→Use existing systems as references for ideas, sequencing, and tests, not as code donors.
   578→
   579→If you later copy code, do it only from a clearly compatible permissive/public-domain source and preserve notices.
   580→
   581→License guidance
   582→Important distinction: there is no single “license for Forth”
   583→Forth is a language family and standard, not one codebase. Implementations vary widely in license.
   584→
   585→Examples:
   586→
   587→Gforth is GPL. 
   588→
   589→JonesForth is public domain. 
   590→
   591→pForth is described as public domain / public-domain-equivalent. 
   592→
   593→historical FIG-Forth and related public distributions were widely circulated as public-domain style listings. 
   594→
   595→So what should you do?
   596→If your goal is maximum freedom and lowest legal friction:
   597→Use either:
   598→
   599→clean-room original implementation under MIT / BSD-2-Clause / BSD-0 / ISC, or
   600→
   601→borrow only from public domain / BSD-0 / MIT / BSD sources.
   602→
   603→Avoid for your purpose:
   604→GPL-licensed implementation code as a porting base, unless you are comfortable releasing your result accordingly.
   605→
   606→My strong recommendation
   607→For your project, choose one of these:
   608→
   609→MIT if you want conventional permissive licensing,
   610→
   611→BSD-2-Clause if you prefer classic minimal permissive licensing,
   612→
   613→BSD-0 / 0BSD if you want near-public-domain simplicity,
   614→
   615→or your own clean-room implementation plus a permissive license.
   616→
   617→That keeps the project reusable for hobby, educational, embedded, and commercial scenarios.
   618→
   619→Is there good public documentation on implementing Forth?
   620→Yes. The most useful public documentation sources for your purpose are:
   621→
   622→1. Forth 2012 standard / standard site
   623→This tells you how the standardized word sets are organized and what the required vs optional sets are. The standard says the Core word set is the only required word set; other word sets are optional additions. 
   624→
   625→2. Threaded interpretive language literature
   626→This explains the execution model and why Forth bootstraps so effectively. 
   627→
   628→3. Small literate implementations
   629→JonesForth is especially useful for understanding:
   630→
   631→dictionary layout,
   632→
   633→code fields,
   634→
   635→colon definitions,
   636→
   637→immediate words,
   638→
   639→the transition from primitives to self-extension. 
   640→
   641→What is the terminology for the base set and add-on sets?
   642→In standardized Forth terminology, the big organizing concept is the word set.
   643→
   644→The standard distinguishes:
   645→
   646→Core word set — the essential required set,
   647→
   648→Core Extension word set — common extensions,
   649→
   650→plus other optional word sets such as:
   651→
   652→Double-Number,
   653→
   654→Exception,
   655→
   656→Facility,
   657→
   658→File-Access,
   659→
   660→Floating-Point,
   661→
   662→Locals,
   663→
   664→Memory-Allocation,
   665→
   666→Programming-Tools,
   667→
   668→Search-Order,
   669→
   670→String,
   671→
   672→Block,
   673→and corresponding extension sets. The standard site and rationale explicitly describe the Core set as the only required set and the others as optional. 
   674→
   675→For an embedded COR24 system, you probably want to think in these layers:
   676→
   677→kernel / nucleus — your machine-level primitives and interpreter core,
   678→
   679→core words — enough to program and extend the language,
   680→
   681→extensions / optional word sets — files, tools, locals, exceptions, etc.,
   682→
   683→target-specific words — memory-mapped I/O, interrupts, board support.
   684→
   685→Which words are minimal?
   686→There are two answers.
   687→
   688→1. Minimal practical kernel for bootstrapping
   689→This is smaller than the standardized Core set.
   690→
   691→You need enough to support:
   692→
   693→Execution model
   694→EXIT
   695→
   696→LIT
   697→
   698→branch and conditional branch
   699→
   700→EXECUTE
   701→
   702→Stack
   703→DROP
   704→
   705→DUP
   706→
   707→SWAP
   708→
   709→OVER
   710→
   711→>R
   712→
   713→R>
   714→
   715→R@
   716→
   717→Arithmetic / compare
   718→+
   719→
   720→-
   721→
   722→=
   723→
   724→0=
   725→
   726→<
   727→
   728→maybe AND OR XOR
   729→
   730→Memory
   731→@
   732→
   733→!
   734→
   735→C@
   736→
   737→C!
   738→
   739→,
   740→
   741→C,
   742→
   743→HERE
   744→
   745→ALLOT
   746→
   747→Dictionary / compiler
   748→FIND
   749→
   750→number conversion
   751→
   752→:
   753→
   754→;
   755→
   756→IMMEDIATE
   757→
   758→[
   759→
   760→]
   761→
   762→Terminal
   763→KEY
   764→
   765→EMIT
   766→
   767→token parsing
   768→
   769→That is enough to get a living language.
   770→
   771→2. Minimal standard-conforming base
   772→If you aim at standard terminology, the relevant anchor is the Core word set from Forth 2012. 
   773→
   774→But I would not try to implement the full standard Core up front.
   775→First implement a bootstrap nucleus, then grow toward a useful subset of Core.
   776→
   777→How are more features added?
   778→Forths usually grow in concentric rings:
   779→
   780→Ring 1 — primitives in machine code
   781→Only what cannot be conveniently or efficiently expressed yet.
