1→# Development Tools
     2→
     3→This document describes the recommended tools for working with this project, particularly for AI coding agents and developers.
     4→
     5→## Tool Location
     6→
     7→Recommended tools are installed in `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`:
     8→
     9→```bash
    10→# List available tools
    11→ls -la ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
    12→
    13→# Add to PATH (if not already in ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc)
    14→export PATH="$HOME/.local/softwarewrighter/bin:$PATH"
    15→```
    16→
    17→## Tool Help Convention
    18→
    19→**IMPORTANT**: All tools in `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/` support `--help` with AI coding agent-specific sections. These extended help sections provide additional context, examples, and best practices tailored for AI agents.
    20→
    21→```bash
    22→# Standard help (short)
    23→tool-name -h
    24→
    25→# Extended help with AI sections (recommended)
    26→tool-name --help
    27→```
    28→
    29→## Core Tools
    30→
    31→### 1. proact - AI Agent Documentation Generator
    32→
    33→**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/proact
    34→**Version**: 0.1.0
    35→
    36→**Purpose**: Generates comprehensive AI coding agent documentation for projects
    37→
    38→**When to Use**:
    39→- When starting work on a new project
    40→- When you want standardized AI agent instructions
    41→- When docs/learnings.md needs to be initialized or updated
    42→- When project processes/standards have changed
    43→
    44→**Installation**:
    45→```bash
    46→# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
    47→```
    48→
    49→**Usage**:
    50→```bash
    51→# Generate docs for current project
    52→proact .
    53→
    54→# Generate docs for specific project
    55→proact /path/to/project
    56→
    57→# Custom output directory
    58→proact /path/to/project -o documentation
    59→
    60→# Preview what would be generated (dry-run)
    61→proact . --dry-run
    62→
    63→# Verbose output
    64→proact . --verbose
    65→```
    66→
    67→**What It Generates**:
    68→- `docs/ai_agent_instructions.md` - Comprehensive AI coding agent guidelines
    69→  - Process-oriented workflow
    70→  - Quality standards
    71→  - Testing strategies
    72→  - Tech debt avoidance
    73→  - Playwright MCP setup
    74→  - Project-specific guidelines (Rust, WASM, etc.)
    75→- `docs/learnings.md` - Template for capturing issues and solutions
    76→  - Common patterns that cause problems
    77→  - Proactive prevention strategies
    78→  - Root cause analysis examples
    79→
    80→**Key Features**:
    81→- Enforces checkpoint process (tests, linting, formatting, docs, git)
    82→- Includes Playwright MCP setup instructions
    83→- Project-specific guidelines based on technology detected
    84→- Continuous improvement focus
    85→- Tech debt limits (file size, TODO count, etc.)
    86→
    87→**AI Agent Notes**:
    88→When proact generates documentation for a project, it creates a baseline. You should:
    89→1. Read the generated docs/ai_agent_instructions.md
    90→2. Follow the checkpoint process it describes
    91→3. Update docs/learnings.md when you encounter issues
    92→4. Run proact again if project structure changes significantly
    93→
    94→### 2. markdown-checker - Markdown Validation Tool
    95→
    96→**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/markdown-checker
    97→**Version**: 0.1.0
    98→
    99→**Purpose**: Ensures markdown files use ASCII-only characters for maximum portability
   100→
   101→**When to Use**:
   102→- BEFORE committing any markdown changes (part of pre-commit process)
   103→- After editing README.md or any docs/*.md files
   104→- When preparing documentation for GitHub (web preview compatibility)
   105→- To fix Unicode characters (arrows, emojis, box-drawing)
   106→
   107→**Installation**:
   108→```bash
   109→# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
   110→```
   111→
   112→**Usage**:
   113→```bash
   114→# Validate README.md in current directory
   115→markdown-checker
   116→
   117→# Validate specific file
   118→markdown-checker -f CONTRIBUTING.md
   119→
   120→# Validate all markdown in directory
   121→markdown-checker -f "*.md"
   122→
   123→# Validate recursively (all markdown in docs/)
   124→markdown-checker -p docs -f "**/*.md"
   125→
   126→# Auto-fix tree symbols
   127→markdown-checker --fix
   128→
   129→# Preview fixes without applying
   130→markdown-checker --dry-run
   131→
   132→# Verbose output
   133→markdown-checker -v
   134→```
   135→
   136→**Validation Rules**:
   137→- [x] ASCII characters (32-126)
   138→- [x] Whitespace (space, tab, LF, CR)
   139→- [x] Unicode arrows (-> becomes ->)
   140→- [x] Emojis ([x] becomes [x] or remove)
   141→- [x] Box-drawing characters (+|+- becomes +|-)
   142→
   143→**Auto-Fixable**:
   144→- Tree symbols: `+ -> +`, `| -> |`, `+ -> +`, `- -> -`
   145→
   146→**Not Auto-Fixable** (manual intervention required):
   147→- Unicode arrows (->)
   148→- Emojis ([x] [x] [WIP])
   149→- Accented characters (e a n)
   150→- Other non-ASCII Unicode
   151→
   152→**Why ASCII-Only?**:
   153→- GitHub README web preview works correctly
   154→- Compatible with all terminals and editors
   155→- Works in CI/CD pipelines
   156→- Accessible to screen readers
   157→- No encoding issues
   158→
   159→**Pre-Commit Integration**:
   160→```bash
   161→# Part of mandatory pre-commit process
   162→markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"
   163→# Fix any issues before committing
   164→```
   165→
   166→**AI Agent Notes**:
   167→- ALWAYS run markdown-checker before committing documentation
   168→- Use --fix for tree symbols, manually fix other Unicode
   169→- If validation fails, FIX the issues (never skip this step)
   170→- Common fixes:
   171→  - `->` becomes `->`
   172→  - `[x]` becomes `[x]` in checklists
   173→  - `[WIP]` becomes `[WIP]` or remove
   174→  - Box-drawing trees become ASCII trees
   175→
   176→### 3. sw-install - Binary Installation Tool
   177→
   178→**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/sw-install
   179→**Version**: 0.1.0
   180→
   181→**Purpose**: Installs compiled Rust binaries to `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`
   182→
   183→**When to Use**:
   184→- First-time setup of development environment
   185→- After building a new Rust CLI tool locally
   186→- When updating an existing tool
   187→- When you need a specific version of a tool
   188→
   189→**Installation**:
   190→```bash
   191→# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
   192→```
   193→
   194→**First-Time Setup**:
   195→```bash
   196→# 1. Setup installation directory and PATH
   197→sw-install --setup-install-dir
   198→
   199→# 2. Reload shell configuration
   200→source ~/.bashrc  # or ~/.zshrc
   201→```
   202→
   203→**Usage**:
   204→```bash
   205→# Install a binary from project (release build)
   206→sw-install -p ~/projects/markdown-checker
   207→
   208→# Install with custom name
   209→sw-install -p ~/projects/ask -r ask-dev
   210→
   211→# Install debug build (for development)
   212→sw-install -p ~/projects/proact -d
   213→
   214→# Uninstall a binary
   215→sw-install -u markdown-checker
   216→
   217→# Dry-run (preview what would happen)
   218→sw-install -p ~/projects/ask --dry-run
   219→
   220→# Verbose output
   221→sw-install -p ~/projects/ask --verbose
   222→```
   223→
   224→**How It Works**:
   225→1. Detects Rust project (looks for Cargo.toml)
   226→2. Finds binary name from Cargo.toml [[bin]] section
   227→3. Copies from `target/release/` or `target/debug/`
   228→4. Installs to `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`
   229→5. Makes executable (chmod +x)
   230→
   231→**PATH Configuration**:
   232→After `--setup-install-dir`, adds to your shell RC file:
   233→```bash
   234→export PATH="$HOME/.local/softwarewrighter/bin:$PATH"
   235→```
   236→
   237→**AI Agent Notes**:
   238→- Use sw-install when you build a new tool and want it available globally
   239→- Prefer release builds for performance: `sw-install -p <path>`
   240→- Use debug builds for development: `sw-install -p <path> -d`
   241→- Check which tools are available: `ls ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`
   242→
   243→### 4. ask - Command-Line LLM Query Tool
   244→
   245→**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/ask
   246→**Version**: 0.1.0
   247→
   248→**Purpose**: Query language models from the command line for quick assistance
   249→
   250→**When to Use**:
   251→- Need quick answers without opening a full AI interface
   252→- Testing API integrations
   253→- Generating code snippets
   254→- Answering technical questions
   255→- Prototyping prompts
   256→
   257→**Installation**:
   258→```bash
   259→# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
   260→```
   261→
   262→**Usage**:
   263→```bash
   264→# Query with default settings (Ollama, phi3:3.8b)

   266→

   268→ask -p openai -m gpt-4 "Explain async/await in Rust"
   269→
   270→# Use shortcut for platform/model combo
   271→ask -s  # List available shortcuts
   272→ask -s sonnet "Write a bash script to..."
   273→
   274→# Dry run (show request without calling API)
   275→ask -n "Test query"
   276→
   277→# Verbose output

   280→# Show usage statistics
   281→ask --usage-report

   283→
   284→**Supported Platforms**:
   285→- Ollama (default, local)
   286→- OpenAI (requires API key)
   287→- Anthropic (requires API key)
   288→
   289→**Configuration**:
   290→```bash
   291→# API keys via environment variables
   292→export OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-..."
   293→export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..."
   294→
   295→# Or via config file
   296→~/.config/ask/config.toml
   297→```
   298→
   299→**AI Agent Notes**:
   300→- Useful for quick references without context switching
   301→- Ollama is default (runs locally, no API costs)
   302→- Set API keys in environment for OpenAI/Anthropic
   303→- Use --usage-report to track API costs
   304→
   305→### 5. sw-checklist - Software Wrighter Project Requirements Checker
   306→
   307→**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/sw-checklist
   308→**Version**: 0.1.0
   309→
   310→**Purpose**: Ensures that a project meets Software Wrighter standards and requirements
   311→
   312→**When to Use**:
   313→- Before committing changes to verify project compliance
   314→- When setting up a new project to ensure all requirements are met
   315→- During pre-commit process to validate project structure
   316→- To identify missing or non-compliant project elements
   317→
   318→**Installation**:
   319→```bash
   320→# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
   321→```
   322→
   323→**Usage**:
   324→```bash
   325→# Run checklist on current project
   326→sw-checklist
   327→
   328→# Show extended help with AI agent guidance
   329→sw-checklist --help
   330→
   331→# Run with verbose output
   332→sw-checklist -v

   334→# Dry run (preview checks without modifications)
   335→sw-checklist --dry-run
   336→```
   337→
   338→**What It Checks**:
   339→- Project structure and required files
   340→- Documentation completeness
   341→- Code quality standards
   342→- Testing requirements
   343→- License and copyright information
   344→- Build and deployment configuration
   345→
   346→**AI Agent Notes**:
   347→- Run sw-checklist before creating commits to ensure compliance
   348→- Use --help to understand specific requirements for your project type
   349→- Address all checklist failures before pushing to remote
   350→- Integrate into pre-commit workflow for automated validation
   351→
   352→### 6. favicon - Favicon Generator
   353→
   354→**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/favicon
   355→**Version**: 0.1.0
   356→
   357→**Purpose**: Generates favicon.ico files for web projects
   358→
   359→**When to Use**:
   360→- Setting up a new web project that needs a favicon
   361→- Creating placeholder favicons during development
   362→- Generating production-ready favicon files
   363→
   364→**Installation**:
   365→```bash
   366→# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
   367→```
   368→
   369→**Usage**:
   370→```bash
   371→# Show extended help with AI agent guidance
   372→favicon --help
   373→
   374→# Generate red question mark on transparent background (placeholder)
   375→# Run this in the directory where index.html is served (e.g., public/ or static/)
   376→favicon -T -t "?" -b ff0000
   377→
   378→# Common options:
   379→# -T          : Transparent background
   380→# -t "TEXT"   : Text to render in favicon
   381→# -b RRGGBB   : Background color (hex)
   382→# -f RRGGBB   : Foreground/text color (hex)
   383→```
   384→
   385→**Common Patterns**:
   386→
   387→```bash
   388→# Placeholder during development (red question mark)
   389→cd public  # or wherever index.html is served
   390→favicon -T -t "?" -b ff0000
   391→
   392→# Development marker (yellow "D")
   393→favicon -T -t "D" -b ffff00 -f 000000
   394→
   395→# Staging marker (orange "S")
   396→favicon -T -t "S" -b ff8800 -f ffffff
   397→
   398→# Production placeholder (blue "P")
   399→favicon -T -t "P" -b 0066ff -f ffffff
   400→```
   401→
   402→**AI Agent Notes**:
   403→- ALWAYS run favicon in the directory where index.html is served
   404→- Common locations: public/, static/, dist/, www/
   405→- Replace placeholder favicons before production deployment
   406→- Use distinctive colors/text for different environments (dev/staging/prod)
   407→- The -T flag creates transparent backgrounds for better visual appearance
   408→
   409→## Tool Discovery
   410→
   411→When new tools are added to `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`:
   412→
   413→1. **List available tools**:
   414→   ```bash
   415→   ls -la ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
   416→   ```
   417→
   418→2. **Check tool help**:
   419→   ```bash
   420→   <tool-name> --help  # Extended help with AI sections
   421→   <tool-name> -h      # Short help
   422→   ```
   423→
   424→3. **Test with dry-run** (if supported):
   425→   ```bash
   426→   <tool-name> --dry-run <args>
   427→   ```
   428→
   429→4. **Update this doc** when new useful tools are discovered
   430→
   431→## Integration with This Project
   432→
   433→### Pre-Commit Process
   434→
   435→From docs/process.md, the following tools are used:
   436→
   437→```bash
   438→# Step 1: Tests
   439→cargo test
   440→
   441→# Step 2: Linting
   442→cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings
   443→
   444→# Step 3: Formatting
   445→cargo fmt --all
   446→
   447→# Step 4: Markdown validation (THIS IS WHERE markdown-checker IS USED)
   448→markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"
   449→
   450→# Step 6: Update documentation
   451→# If issues found, update docs/learnings.md
   452→# Can use proact to regenerate if major changes
   453→```
   454→
   455→### Documentation Workflow
   456→
   457→```bash
   458→# 1. Make code/doc changes
   459→vim README.md
   460→
   461→# 2. Validate markdown before commit
   462→markdown-checker -f "README.md"
   463→
   464→# 3. Fix any issues
   465→# (manually replace Unicode, or use --fix for tree symbols)
   466→
   467→# 4. If new patterns found, update docs/learnings.md
   468→
   469→# 5. Optionally regenerate full docs
   470→proact .
   471→
   472→# 6. Commit changes
   473→git commit -m "docs: Update README with new features"
   474→```
   475→
   476→## Best Practices
   477→
   478→### For AI Coding Agents
   479→
   480→1. **Always check tool help first**:
   481→   ```bash
   482→   <tool> --help  # Not -h, use --help for AI-specific sections
   483→   ```
   484→
   485→2. **Use dry-run mode when learning**:
   486→   ```bash
   487→   proact . --dry-run
   488→   markdown-checker --dry-run
   489→   ```
   490→
   491→3. **Integrate into workflow**:
   492→   - Use markdown-checker in pre-commit process (mandatory)
   493→   - Use proact when starting new projects
   494→   - Update docs/learnings.md when encountering issues
   495→
   496→4. **Validate before committing**:
   497→   ```bash
   498→   # Always run before commit if markdown changed
   499→   markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"
   500→   ```
   501→
   502→5. **Keep tools updated**:
   503→   ```bash
   504→   # Rebuild and reinstall tool
   505→   cd ~/projects/markdown-checker
   506→   cargo build --release
   507→   sw-install -p .
   508→   ```
   509→
   510→### For Human Developers
   511→
   512→1. **Setup PATH once**:
   513→   ```bash
   514→   sw-install --setup-install-dir
   515→   source ~/.bashrc
   516→   ```
   517→
   518→2. **Install tools as needed**:
   519→   ```bash
   520→   # Clone and build
   521→   git clone https://github.com/softwarewrighter/<tool>.git
   522→   cd <tool>
   523→   cargo build --release
   524→
   525→   # Install globally
   526→   sw-install -p .
   527→   ```
   528→
   529→3. **Use in pre-commit hooks** (future):
   530→   ```bash
   531→   # .git/hooks/pre-commit
   532→   #!/bin/bash
   533→   markdown-checker -f "**/*.md" || exit 1
   534→   ```
   535→
   536→## Troubleshooting
   537→
   538→### Tool Not Found
   539→
   540→```bash
   541→# Check if tool exists
   542→ls ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
   543→
   544→# Check if PATH is set
   545→echo $PATH | grep softwarewrighter
   546→
   547→# Re-run setup if needed
   548→sw-install --setup-install-dir
   549→source ~/.bashrc  # or ~/.zshrc
   550→```
   551→
   552→### markdown-checker Validation Failures
   553→
   554→```bash
   555→# See detailed errors
   556→markdown-checker -v
   557→
   558→# Try auto-fix for tree symbols
   559→markdown-checker --fix
   560→
   561→# For other Unicode, manually replace:
   562→# -> with ->
   563→# [x] with [x]
   564→# [WIP] with [WIP] or remove
   565→```
   566→
   567→### Permission Denied
   568→
   569→```bash
   570→# Tools should be executable, but if not:
   571→chmod +x ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/<tool-name>
   572→```
   573→
   574→## Future Tools
   575→
   576→As new tools are added to `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`, document them here:
   577→
   578→**Template**:
   579→```markdown
   580→### Tool Name
   581→
   582→**Purpose**: Brief description
   583→
   584→**When to Use**:
   585→- Use case 1
   586→- Use case 2
   587→
   588→**Usage**:
   589→```bash
   590→tool-name --help
   591→tool-name <common-usage>
   592→```
   593→
   594→**AI Agent Notes**:
   595→- Specific guidance for AI agents
   596→- Integration with project workflow
   597→```
   598→

# Development Tools

This document describes the recommended tools for working with this project, particularly for AI coding agents and developers.

## Tool Location

Recommended tools are installed in `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`:

```bash
# List available tools
ls -la ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/

# Add to PATH (if not already in ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc)
export PATH="$HOME/.local/softwarewrighter/bin:$PATH"
```

## Tool Help Convention

**IMPORTANT**: All tools in `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/` support `--help` with AI coding agent-specific sections. These extended help sections provide additional context, examples, and best practices tailored for AI agents.

```bash
# Standard help (short)
tool-name -h

# Extended help with AI sections (recommended)
tool-name --help
```

## Core Tools

### 1. proact - AI Agent Documentation Generator

**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/proact
**Version**: 0.1.0

**Purpose**: Generates comprehensive AI coding agent documentation for projects

**When to Use**:
- When starting work on a new project
- When you want standardized AI agent instructions
- When docs/learnings.md needs to be initialized or updated
- When project processes/standards have changed

**Installation**:
```bash
# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
```

**Usage**:
```bash
# Generate docs for current project
proact .

# Generate docs for specific project
proact /path/to/project

# Custom output directory
proact /path/to/project -o documentation

# Preview what would be generated (dry-run)
proact . --dry-run

# Verbose output
proact . --verbose
```

**What It Generates**:
- `docs/ai_agent_instructions.md` - Comprehensive AI coding agent guidelines
  - Process-oriented workflow
  - Quality standards
  - Testing strategies
  - Tech debt avoidance
  - Playwright MCP setup
  - Project-specific guidelines (Rust, WASM, etc.)
- `docs/learnings.md` - Template for capturing issues and solutions
  - Common patterns that cause problems
  - Proactive prevention strategies
  - Root cause analysis examples

**Key Features**:
- Enforces checkpoint process (tests, linting, formatting, docs, git)
- Includes Playwright MCP setup instructions
- Project-specific guidelines based on technology detected
- Continuous improvement focus
- Tech debt limits (file size, TODO count, etc.)

**AI Agent Notes**:
When proact generates documentation for a project, it creates a baseline. You should:
1. Read the generated docs/ai_agent_instructions.md
2. Follow the checkpoint process it describes
3. Update docs/learnings.md when you encounter issues
4. Run proact again if project structure changes significantly

### 2. markdown-checker - Markdown Validation Tool

**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/markdown-checker
**Version**: 0.1.0

**Purpose**: Ensures markdown files use ASCII-only characters for maximum portability

**When to Use**:
- BEFORE committing any markdown changes (part of pre-commit process)
- After editing README.md or any docs/*.md files
- When preparing documentation for GitHub (web preview compatibility)
- To fix Unicode characters (arrows, emojis, box-drawing)

**Installation**:
```bash
# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
```

**Usage**:
```bash
# Validate README.md in current directory
markdown-checker

# Validate specific file
markdown-checker -f CONTRIBUTING.md

# Validate all markdown in directory
markdown-checker -f "*.md"

# Validate recursively (all markdown in docs/)
markdown-checker -p docs -f "**/*.md"

# Auto-fix tree symbols
markdown-checker --fix

# Preview fixes without applying
markdown-checker --dry-run

# Verbose output
markdown-checker -v
```

**Validation Rules**:
- [x] ASCII characters (32-126)
- [x] Whitespace (space, tab, LF, CR)
- [x] Unicode arrows (-> becomes ->)
- [x] Emojis ([x] becomes [x] or remove)
- [x] Box-drawing characters (+|+- becomes +|-)

**Auto-Fixable**:
- Tree symbols: `+ -> +`, `| -> |`, `+ -> +`, `- -> -`

**Not Auto-Fixable** (manual intervention required):
- Unicode arrows (->)
- Emojis ([x] [x] [WIP])
- Accented characters (e a n)
- Other non-ASCII Unicode

**Why ASCII-Only?**:
- GitHub README web preview works correctly
- Compatible with all terminals and editors
- Works in CI/CD pipelines
- Accessible to screen readers
- No encoding issues

**Pre-Commit Integration**:
```bash
# Part of mandatory pre-commit process
markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"
# Fix any issues before committing
```

**AI Agent Notes**:
- ALWAYS run markdown-checker before committing documentation
- Use --fix for tree symbols, manually fix other Unicode
- If validation fails, FIX the issues (never skip this step)
- Common fixes:
  - `->` becomes `->`
  - `[x]` becomes `[x]` in checklists
  - `[WIP]` becomes `[WIP]` or remove
  - Box-drawing trees become ASCII trees

### 3. sw-install - Binary Installation Tool

**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/sw-install
**Version**: 0.1.0

**Purpose**: Installs compiled Rust binaries to `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`

**When to Use**:
- First-time setup of development environment
- After building a new Rust CLI tool locally
- When updating an existing tool
- When you need a specific version of a tool

**Installation**:
```bash
# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
```

**First-Time Setup**:
```bash
# 1. Setup installation directory and PATH
sw-install --setup-install-dir

# 2. Reload shell configuration
source ~/.bashrc  # or ~/.zshrc
```

**Usage**:
```bash
# Install a binary from project (release build)
sw-install -p ~/projects/markdown-checker

# Install with custom name
sw-install -p ~/projects/ask -r ask-dev

# Install debug build (for development)
sw-install -p ~/projects/proact -d

# Uninstall a binary
sw-install -u markdown-checker

# Dry-run (preview what would happen)
sw-install -p ~/projects/ask --dry-run

# Verbose output
sw-install -p ~/projects/ask --verbose
```

**How It Works**:
1. Detects Rust project (looks for Cargo.toml)
2. Finds binary name from Cargo.toml [[bin]] section
3. Copies from `target/release/` or `target/debug/`
4. Installs to `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`
5. Makes executable (chmod +x)

**PATH Configuration**:
After `--setup-install-dir`, adds to your shell RC file:
```bash
export PATH="$HOME/.local/softwarewrighter/bin:$PATH"
```

**AI Agent Notes**:
- Use sw-install when you build a new tool and want it available globally
- Prefer release builds for performance: `sw-install -p <path>`
- Use debug builds for development: `sw-install -p <path> -d`
- Check which tools are available: `ls ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`

### 4. ask - Command-Line LLM Query Tool

**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/ask
**Version**: 0.1.0

**Purpose**: Query language models from the command line for quick assistance

**When to Use**:
- Need quick answers without opening a full AI interface
- Testing API integrations
- Generating code snippets
- Answering technical questions
- Prototyping prompts

**Installation**:
```bash
# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
```

**Usage**:
```bash
# Query with default settings (Ollama, phi3:3.8b)
ask "How do I parse JSON in Rust?"

# Use a specific platform and model
ask -p openai -m gpt-4 "Explain async/await in Rust"

# Use shortcut for platform/model combo
ask -s  # List available shortcuts
ask -s sonnet "Write a bash script to..."

# Dry run (show request without calling API)
ask -n "Test query"

# Verbose output
ask -v "Debug query"

# Show usage statistics
ask --usage-report
```

**Supported Platforms**:
- Ollama (default, local)
- OpenAI (requires API key)
- Anthropic (requires API key)

**Configuration**:
```bash
# API keys via environment variables
export OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-..."
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..."

# Or via config file
~/.config/ask/config.toml
```

**AI Agent Notes**:
- Useful for quick references without context switching
- Ollama is default (runs locally, no API costs)
- Set API keys in environment for OpenAI/Anthropic
- Use --usage-report to track API costs

### 5. sw-checklist - Software Wrighter Project Requirements Checker

**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/sw-checklist
**Version**: 0.1.0

**Purpose**: Ensures that a project meets Software Wrighter standards and requirements

**When to Use**:
- Before committing changes to verify project compliance
- When setting up a new project to ensure all requirements are met
- During pre-commit process to validate project structure
- To identify missing or non-compliant project elements

**Installation**:
```bash
# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
```

**Usage**:
```bash
# Run checklist on current project
sw-checklist

# Show extended help with AI agent guidance
sw-checklist --help

# Run with verbose output
sw-checklist -v

# Dry run (preview checks without modifications)
sw-checklist --dry-run
```

**What It Checks**:
- Project structure and required files
- Documentation completeness
- Code quality standards
- Testing requirements
- License and copyright information
- Build and deployment configuration

**AI Agent Notes**:
- Run sw-checklist before creating commits to ensure compliance
- Use --help to understand specific requirements for your project type
- Address all checklist failures before pushing to remote
- Integrate into pre-commit workflow for automated validation

### 6. favicon - Favicon Generator

**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/favicon
**Version**: 0.1.0

**Purpose**: Generates favicon.ico files for web projects

**When to Use**:
- Setting up a new web project that needs a favicon
- Creating placeholder favicons during development
- Generating production-ready favicon files

**Installation**:
```bash
# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
```

**Usage**:
```bash
# Show extended help with AI agent guidance
favicon --help

# Generate red question mark on transparent background (placeholder)
# Run this in the directory where index.html is served (e.g., public/ or static/)
favicon -T -t "?" -b ff0000

# Common options:
# -T          : Transparent background
# -t "TEXT"   : Text to render in favicon
# -b RRGGBB   : Background color (hex)
# -f RRGGBB   : Foreground/text color (hex)
```

**Common Patterns**:

```bash
# Placeholder during development (red question mark)
cd public  # or wherever index.html is served
favicon -T -t "?" -b ff0000

# Development marker (yellow "D")
favicon -T -t "D" -b ffff00 -f 000000

# Staging marker (orange "S")
favicon -T -t "S" -b ff8800 -f ffffff

# Production placeholder (blue "P")
favicon -T -t "P" -b 0066ff -f ffffff
```

**AI Agent Notes**:
- ALWAYS run favicon in the directory where index.html is served
- Common locations: public/, static/, dist/, www/
- Replace placeholder favicons before production deployment
- Use distinctive colors/text for different environments (dev/staging/prod)
- The -T flag creates transparent backgrounds for better visual appearance

## Tool Discovery

When new tools are added to `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`:

1. **List available tools**:
   ```bash
   ls -la ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
   ```

2. **Check tool help**:
   ```bash
   <tool-name> --help  # Extended help with AI sections
   <tool-name> -h      # Short help
   ```

3. **Test with dry-run** (if supported):
   ```bash
   <tool-name> --dry-run <args>
   ```

4. **Update this doc** when new useful tools are discovered

## Integration with This Project

### Pre-Commit Process

From docs/process.md, the following tools are used:

```bash
# Step 1: Tests
cargo test

# Step 2: Linting
cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings

# Step 3: Formatting
cargo fmt --all

# Step 4: Markdown validation (THIS IS WHERE markdown-checker IS USED)
markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"

# Step 6: Update documentation
# If issues found, update docs/learnings.md
# Can use proact to regenerate if major changes
```

### Documentation Workflow

```bash
# 1. Make code/doc changes
vim README.md

# 2. Validate markdown before commit
markdown-checker -f "README.md"

# 3. Fix any issues
# (manually replace Unicode, or use --fix for tree symbols)

# 4. If new patterns found, update docs/learnings.md

# 5. Optionally regenerate full docs
proact .

# 6. Commit changes
git commit -m "docs: Update README with new features"
```

## Best Practices

### For AI Coding Agents

1. **Always check tool help first**:
   ```bash
   <tool> --help  # Not -h, use --help for AI-specific sections
   ```

2. **Use dry-run mode when learning**:
   ```bash
   proact . --dry-run
   markdown-checker --dry-run
   ```

3. **Integrate into workflow**:
   - Use markdown-checker in pre-commit process (mandatory)
   - Use proact when starting new projects
   - Update docs/learnings.md when encountering issues

4. **Validate before committing**:
   ```bash
   # Always run before commit if markdown changed
   markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"
   ```

5. **Keep tools updated**:
   ```bash
   # Rebuild and reinstall tool
   cd ~/projects/markdown-checker
   cargo build --release
   sw-install -p .
   ```

### For Human Developers

1. **Setup PATH once**:
   ```bash
   sw-install --setup-install-dir
   source ~/.bashrc
   ```

2. **Install tools as needed**:
   ```bash
   # Clone and build
   git clone https://github.com/softwarewrighter/<tool>.git
   cd <tool>
   cargo build --release

   # Install globally
   sw-install -p .
   ```

3. **Use in pre-commit hooks** (future):
   ```bash
   # .git/hooks/pre-commit
   #!/bin/bash
   markdown-checker -f "**/*.md" || exit 1
   ```

## Troubleshooting

### Tool Not Found

```bash
# Check if tool exists
ls ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/

# Check if PATH is set
echo $PATH | grep softwarewrighter

# Re-run setup if needed
sw-install --setup-install-dir
source ~/.bashrc  # or ~/.zshrc
```

### markdown-checker Validation Failures

```bash
# See detailed errors
markdown-checker -v

# Try auto-fix for tree symbols
markdown-checker --fix

# For other Unicode, manually replace:
# -> with ->
# [x] with [x]
# [WIP] with [WIP] or remove
```

### Permission Denied

```bash
# Tools should be executable, but if not:
chmod +x ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/<tool-name>
```

## Future Tools

As new tools are added to `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`, document them here:

**Template**:
```markdown
### Tool Name

- Use case 2

**Usage**:
```bash
tool-name --help
tool-name <common-usage>
```

**AI Agent Notes**:
- Specific guidance for AI agents
- Integration with project workflow
```

file.content:
; forth.s — tf24a Phase 1: Bootstrap, UART I/O, Stack Tests
; COR24 DTC Forth bootstrap
;
; Register allocation (frozen):
;   r0 = W (work/scratch)
;   r1 = RSP (return stack pointer, grows down from 0x0F0000)
;   r2 = IP (instruction pointer — unused in Phase 1 tests)
;   sp = DSP (data stack, hardware push/pop in EBR)
;   fp = limited scratch (many instructions don't support fp)
;
; UART: data at 0xFF0100 (-65280), status at 0xFF0101 (-65279)
;   TX busy = status bit 7, RX ready = status bit 0

; ============================================================
; Entry point (address 0)
; ============================================================
_start:
    ; sp is set by hardware (EBR at 0xFEEC00)
    la r1, 983040       ; r1 = 0x0F0000 return stack base

    ; ============================================================
    ; Test 1: Data stack + UART — print "OK\n"
    ; Push in reverse order (LIFO), pop and emit in order
    ; ============================================================
    lc r0, 10           ; '\n'
    push r0
    lc r0, 75           ; 'K'
    push r0
    lc r0, 79           ; 'O'
    push r0

    la r2, -65280       ; r2 = UART data (IP not needed in Phase 1)

    ; Emit 'O' — poll TX busy, then write
    pop r0
    push r0             ; save byte while polling
tx1:
    lb r0, 1(r2)        ; status (sign-extended; bit 7 → negative)
    cls r0, z           ; C = (status < 0) = TX busy
    brt tx1
    pop r0              ; restore byte
    sb r0, 0(r2)        ; write to UART

    ; Emit 'K'
    pop r0
    push r0
tx2:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt tx2
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)

    ; Emit '\n'
    pop r0
    push r0
tx3:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt tx3
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)

    ; ============================================================
    ; Test 2: Return stack — push 42, clear r0, pop, emit '*'
    ; ============================================================
    lc r0, 42           ; 42 = ASCII '*'
    add r1, -3          ; RSP -= cell
    sw r0, 0(r1)        ; push to return stack

    lc r0, 0            ; clear r0 to prove round-trip

    lw r0, 0(r1)        ; pop from return stack
    add r1, 3           ; RSP += cell

    ; Emit '*' with TX polling
    push r0
tx4:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt tx4
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)

    ; Emit '\n'
    lc r0, 10
    push r0
tx5:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt tx5
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)

    ; ============================================================
    ; Halt
    ; ============================================================
halt:
    bra halt

; ============================================================
; EMIT ( c -- ) : Write character to UART TX with busy-wait
; Saves byte and IP (r2) on return stack
; ============================================================
do_emit:
    pop r0              ; r0 = character from data stack
    add r1, -3          ; save IP on return stack
    sw r2, 0(r1)
    add r1, -3          ; save byte on return stack
    sw r0, 0(r1)
emit_poll:
    la r0, -65280       ; r0 = UART base
    lb r0, 1(r0)        ; r0 = status (sign-extended; bit 7 → negative)
    cls r0, z           ; C = (status < 0) = TX busy
    brt emit_poll       ; loop while TX busy
    lw r0, 0(r1)        ; restore byte
    add r1, 3
    la r2, -65280       ; r2 = UART base (temporarily clobber IP)
    sb r0, 0(r2)        ; write byte to UART TX
    lw r2, 0(r1)        ; restore IP
    add r1, 3
    ; NEXT
    lw r0, 0(r2)        ; W = mem[IP]
    add r2, 3           ; IP += cell
    jmp (r0)            ; execute next word

; ============================================================
; KEY ( -- c ) : Read character from UART RX with busy-wait
; Saves IP (r2) on return stack
; ============================================================
do_key:
    add r1, -3          ; save IP on return stack
    sw r2, 0(r1)
key_poll:
    la r0, -65280       ; r0 = UART base
    lbu r0, 1(r0)       ; r0 = status byte
    lcu r2, 1           ; r2 = bit 0 mask (RX ready)
    and r0, r2          ; isolate bit 0
    ceq r0, z           ; C = (bit0 == 0) = RX not ready
    brt key_poll        ; loop until RX ready
    la r0, -65280       ; reload UART base
    lbu r0, 0(r0)       ; read byte (acknowledges RX)
    push r0             ; push to data stack
    lw r2, 0(r1)        ; restore IP
    add r1, 3
    ; NEXT
    lw r0, 0(r2)        ; W = mem[IP]
    add r2, 3           ; IP += cell
    jmp (r0)            ; execute next word

content.content:
1→# CLAUDE.md
     2→
     3→This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
     4→
     5→## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)
     6→
     7→This project uses AgentRail. Every session follows this exact sequence:
     8→
     9→### 1. START (do this FIRST, before anything else)
    10→```bash
    11→agentrail next
    12→```
    13→Read the output carefully. It tells you your current step, prompt, skill docs, and past trajectories.
    14→
    15→### 2. BEGIN (immediately after reading the next output)
    16→```bash
    17→agentrail begin
    18→```
    19→
    20→### 3. WORK (do what the step prompt says)
    21→Do NOT ask the user "want me to proceed?" or "shall I start?". The step prompt IS your instruction. Execute it.
    22→
    23→### 4. COMMIT (after the work is done)
    24→Commit your code changes with git.
    25→
    26→### 5. COMPLETE (LAST thing, after committing)
    27→```bash
    28→agentrail complete --summary "what you accomplished" \
    29→  --reward 1 \
    30→  --actions "tools and approach used"
    31→```
    32→If the step failed: `--reward -1 --failure-mode "what went wrong"`
    33→If the saga is finished: add `--done`
    34→
    35→### 6. STOP (after complete, DO NOT continue working)
    36→Do NOT make any further code changes after running agentrail complete.
    37→Any changes after complete are untracked and invisible to the next session.
    38→If you see more work to do, it belongs in the NEXT step, not this session.
    39→
    40→Do NOT skip any of these steps. The next session depends on your trajectory recording.
    41→
    42→## Project: tf24a — Tiny Forth for COR24 in Assembler
    43→
    44→Clean-room DTC Forth for the COR24 24-bit RISC ISA. Assembler kernel, self-extending in Forth.
    45→
    46→### Tools
    47→- `cor24-run --run file.s [opts]` — assemble and run COR24 assembly
    48→- `cor24-run --run file.s --dump --speed 0 -n N` — run N instructions, dump state
    49→- `cor24-run --run file.s --terminal --echo --speed 0` — interactive UART session
    50→- `cor24-run --run file.s -u 'input' --speed 0 --dump` — feed UART input, dump state
    51→
    52→### COR24 Assembly Syntax
    53→- Labels on own line: `label:` (no inline `label: instr`)
    54→- Comments: `;` only (not `#`)
    55→- Decimal immediates (not hex): `la r0, -65280` not `la r0, 0xFF0100`

    63→- r2 = IP (instruction pointer for threaded code)
    64→- sp = DSP (data stack, hardware push/pop in EBR)

file.content:
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)

This project uses AgentRail. Every session follows this exact sequence:

### 1. START (do this FIRST, before anything else)
```bash
agentrail next
```
Read the output carefully. It tells you your current step, prompt, skill docs, and past trajectories.

### 2. BEGIN (immediately after reading the next output)
```bash
agentrail begin
```

### 3. WORK (do what the step prompt says)
Do NOT ask the user "want me to proceed?" or "shall I start?". The step prompt IS your instruction. Execute it.

### 4. COMMIT (after the work is done)
Commit your code changes with git.

### 5. COMPLETE (LAST thing, after committing)
```bash
agentrail complete --summary "what you accomplished" \
  --reward 1 \
  --actions "tools and approach used"
```
If the step failed: `--reward -1 --failure-mode "what went wrong"`
If the saga is finished: add `--done`

### 6. STOP (after complete, DO NOT continue working)
Do NOT make any further code changes after running agentrail complete.
Any changes after complete are untracked and invisible to the next session.
If you see more work to do, it belongs in the NEXT step, not this session.

Do NOT skip any of these steps. The next session depends on your trajectory recording.

## Project: tf24a — Tiny Forth for COR24 in Assembler

Clean-room DTC Forth for the COR24 24-bit RISC ISA. Assembler kernel, self-extending in Forth.

### Tools
- `cor24-run --run file.s [opts]` — assemble and run COR24 assembly
- `cor24-run --run file.s --dump --speed 0 -n N` — run N instructions, dump state
- `cor24-run --run file.s --terminal --echo --speed 0` — interactive UART session
- `cor24-run --run file.s -u 'input' --speed 0 --dump` — feed UART input, dump state

### COR24 Assembly Syntax
- Labels on own line: `label:` (no inline `label: instr`)
- Comments: `;` only (not `#`)
- Decimal immediates (not hex): `la r0, -65280` not `la r0, 0xFF0100`
- `.word label` — emits 24-bit address (one label per directive)
- `.byte 72, 101, 108` — raw bytes (no string literals)
- No `.align` directive; manually pad with `.byte 0`

### Register Allocation (Frozen)
- r0 = W (work register) / scratch
- r1 = RSP (return stack pointer, SRAM ~0x0F0000 growing down)
- r2 = IP (instruction pointer for threaded code)
- sp = DSP (data stack, hardware push/pop in EBR)
- fp = available as extra scratch

### Key ISA Constraints
- Only r0, r1, r2, sp support `add rX, imm8`; fp does NOT
- push/pop only work with sp (1-byte instructions)
- Branch offset ±127 bytes (signed 8-bit)
- `jal r1,(r0)` sets r1=PC+1, jumps to r0 — conflicts with r1=RSP
- Cell size = 3 bytes (24-bit words)

### DTC Inner Interpreter
```
; NEXT (inline everywhere or as tail of each primitive):
;   lw r0, 0(r2)    ; W = mem[IP] — fetch CFA from thread
;   add r2, 3       ; IP += cell
;   jmp (r0)        ; execute code at CFA
```

### UART I/O
- Data register: address -65280 (0xFF0100)
- Status register: address -65279 (0xFF0101)
- TX busy: bit 7 of status
- RX ready: bit 0 of status

content.text:
OK, I've now thoroughly researched all the referenced projects. Here's my understanding and questions:

## What I Understand

**The goal**: Build `web-tf24a` — a Yew/Rust/WASM web app that runs the **tf24a Forth interpreter** (COR24 assembly) inside the **cor24-rs emulator**, with a **Forth-aware debugger UI** as described in `docs/research.txt`. Track development with **agentrail sagas**.

**The architecture** (following web-tml24c's pattern):
- **Trunk** builds WASM, serves on a local port
- **cor24-emulator** (path dep to `../../sw-embed/cor24-rs`) provides `EmulatorCore` + `Assembler`
- **Yew** framework for the UI (CSR mode)
- `forth.s` from tf24a is `include_str!`'d and assembled at runtime
- UART I/O bridges user input to the Forth interpreter
- Byte-at-a-time UART feeding with prompt detection

**Key difference from web-tml24c**: Instead of a simple REPL, this needs a **Forth debugger** with:
- Data stack & return stack visualization
- Dictionary browser (link chain, header/body, CFA/PFA)
- Current word / IP / caller chain execution trace
- Memory region map (kernel, dictionary, stacks, free space)
- Dual semantic + raw views
- Word-level stepping (step word, step primitive, step out)

**agentrail**: I need to `agentrail init` a saga to track phased development.

## Questions

1. **tf24a is still Phase 1-2** (bootstrap + some primitives, no dictionary yet). Should the debugger UI initially focus on the CPU/assembly level (registers, stacks, memory map) and progressively add Forth-semantic views as tf24a grows? Or do you want the full Forth debugger UI scaffolded now even though most panels will be empty?

2. **Assembly source**: Should I embed `forth.s` directly from `../../tf24a/forth.s` via `include_str!`, or copy it into an `asm/` directory like web-tml24c does with its prelude tiers?

3. **Multiple tiers**: web-tml24c has 5 prelude tiers. tf24a currently has one `forth.s`. Should I plan for multiple assembly variants (e.g., Phase 1 tests, Phase 2 with inner interpreter, future phases), or just one for now?

4. **Saga naming/structure**: Any preference for the saga name or initial step breakdown? I'm thinking something like:
   - Step 1: Scaffold (Trunk + Yew + cor24 deps, basic WASM build)
   - Step 2: Basic REPL (assemble forth.s, run in emulator, UART terminal)
   - Step 3: Debugger panels (stacks, registers, memory, step controls)
   - Step 4: Forth-semantic views (dictionary browser, word inspector)

5. **Port number**: web-tml24c uses 9135. Any preference for this project's dev server port?

message.content: 1 yes; 2 asm/ dir; 3 there may be a similar concept of base dictionary and sets of words added in groups so plan for more than one; 4 fine; 5 9181

file.content:
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)

This project uses AgentRail. Every session follows this exact sequence:

### 1. START (do this FIRST, before anything else)
```bash
agentrail next
```
Read the output carefully. It tells you your current step, prompt, skill docs, and past trajectories.

### 2. BEGIN (immediately after reading the next output)
```bash
agentrail begin
```

### 3. WORK (do what the step prompt says)
Do NOT ask the user "want me to proceed?" or "shall I start?". The step prompt IS your instruction. Execute it.

### 4. COMMIT (after the work is done)
Commit your code changes with git.

### 5. COMPLETE (LAST thing, after committing)
```bash
agentrail complete --summary "what you accomplished" \
  --reward 1 \
  --actions "tools and approach used"
```
If the step failed: `--reward -1 --failure-mode "what went wrong"`
If the saga is finished: add `--done`

### 6. STOP (after complete, DO NOT continue working)
Do NOT make any further code changes after running agentrail complete.
Any changes after complete are untracked and invisible to the next session.
If you see more work to do, it belongs in the NEXT step, not this session.

Do NOT skip any of these steps. The next session depends on your trajectory recording.

input.old_string: Do NOT skip any of these steps. The next session depends on your trajectory recording.
input.new_string:
Do NOT skip any of these steps. The next session depends on your trajectory recording.

## Project: web-tf24a -- Forth Debugger on COR24

Browser-based Forth debugger running the tf24a DTC Forth interpreter on the cor24-rs emulator via WASM.

### Build and Serve

```bash
trunk build                    # Build WASM to dist/
./scripts/serve.sh             # Dev server on port 9181
cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings  # Lint
cargo fmt --all                # Format
```

### Architecture

- **Trunk** builds the WASM binary and serves it
- **cor24-emulator** provides `EmulatorCore` + `Assembler` (path dep to `../../sw-embed/cor24-rs`)
- **Yew 0.21** CSR framework for the UI
- Assembly files in `asm/` are `include_str!`'d and assembled at runtime
- UART I/O bridges user input to the Forth interpreter running in the emulator

### Key Files

- `src/debugger.rs` -- Main debugger component (emulator loop, UI panels)
- `src/config.rs` -- ForthTier enum (multi-tier assembly) + StackSize
- `asm/forth-bootstrap.s` -- Phase 1 tf24a Forth kernel (copied from tf24a)
- `index.html` -- Entry point with Catppuccin Mocha theme
- `src/debugger.css` -- Debugger panel styling

### COR24 Register Allocation (from tf24a)

- r0 = W (work/scratch)
- r1 = RSP (return stack pointer, SRAM ~0x0F0000 growing down)
- r2 = IP (instruction pointer for threaded code)
- sp = DSP (data stack, hardware push/pop in EBR)
- fp = available as extra scratch
- Cell size = 3 bytes (24-bit words)

# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)

This project uses AgentRail. Every session follows this exact sequence:

### 1. START (do this FIRST, before anything else)
```bash
agentrail next
```
Read the output carefully. It tells you your current step, prompt, skill docs, and past trajectories.

### 2. BEGIN (immediately after reading the next output)
```bash
agentrail begin
```

### 3. WORK (do what the step prompt says)
Do NOT ask the user "want me to proceed?" or "shall I start?". The step prompt IS your instruction. Execute it.

### 4. COMMIT (after the work is done)
Commit your code changes with git.

### 5. COMPLETE (LAST thing, after committing)
```bash
agentrail complete --summary "what you accomplished" \
  --reward 1 \
  --actions "tools and approach used"
```
If the step failed: `--reward -1 --failure-mode "what went wrong"`
If the saga is finished: add `--done`

### 6. STOP (after complete, DO NOT continue working)
Do NOT make any further code changes after running agentrail complete.
Any changes after complete are untracked and invisible to the next session.
If you see more work to do, it belongs in the NEXT step, not this session.

---
name: web-tf24a project overview
description: Browser-based Forth debugger (Yew/WASM) running tf24a on cor24-rs emulator, with agentrail saga tracking
type: project
---

web-tf24a is a Forth-aware debugger web app built with Yew/Rust/WASM.

**Why:** The tf24a Forth interpreter runs on the COR24 24-bit RISC ISA, and needs a visual debugger that shows Forth concepts (stacks, dictionary, word execution) not just raw CPU state.

**How to apply:**
- tf24a is in early phases (Phase 1-2), so debugger starts CPU/assembly-focused and will add Forth-semantic views as tf24a grows
- Assembly tiers in asm/ directory (like web-tml24c's prelude tiers) -- plan for multiple
- cor24-emulator is a path dependency at ../../sw-embed/cor24-rs
- Dev server port: 9181
- Uses agentrail saga "web-tf24a-forth-debugger" for development tracking
- docs/research.txt contains detailed Forth debugger UI design from AI research (dictionary browser, stack views, memory regions, word inspector)
